topics.title: Overview of Machine Learning - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Machine Learning - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Anchor (Module 1):** Machine Learning is the scientific study of algorithms and statistical models that computer systems use to progressively improve their performance on a specific task by relying on patterns and inference, without being explicitly programmed for every possible input.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Board Standard)

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** that enables a computational system to **learn from experience (data)** with respect to some **class of tasks (T)** and a **performance measure (P)**, such that its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience **E**.

This is the classical formal definition framed by **Tom M. Mitchell (1997)**, which is directly adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1 – Overview of Machine Learning).

Mathematically, the learning hypothesis is:

$$
\text{ML} : \{E,\ T,\ P\} \longrightarrow \text{Improving Function } f(x)
$$

Where:
- **E (Experience)** = The dataset (training data) the algorithm is exposed to.
- **T (Task)** = The objective the algorithm is trying to achieve (e.g., classification, regression).
- **P (Performance Measure)** = The metric used to quantify how well the task is being performed (e.g., accuracy, F1-score, RMSE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Examiner Insight:** If a question asks to "Define Machine Learning", you MUST explicitly mention all three components — **Task (T), Experience (E), and Performance (P)**. Writing a generic "ML is a sub-field of AI" definition will fetch only partial marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Learning Like a Child

Imagine teaching a small child to recognize a **cat** vs a **dog**:
- You do not hard-code rules like *"if ears are pointed AND whiskers are long THEN cat"*.
- Instead, you show the child **hundreds of photographs** (experience) of cats and dogs, and the child **gradually figures out the distinguishing patterns** on his/her own.
- With more practice (more experience), the child gets **better** at distinguishing them (performance improves).

Machine Learning works **exactly the same way** — instead of a child, we have a **mathematical model (algorithm)**, and instead of photographs, we feed it a **dataset**. The model adjusts its internal parameters to minimize errors and improve its predictions over time.

### 1.3 Why Traditional Programming Falls Short

In **traditional programming**, the developer writes a fixed set of rules:

$$
\text{Output} = f(\text{Input},\ \text{Program})
$$

But in **Machine Learning**, the program itself is *learned* from data:

$$
\text{Program} = f(\text{Input},\ \text{Output})
$$

| Aspect | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Rule Source** | Hand-coded by developer | Derived from data |
| **Scalability** | Poor for complex patterns | Excellent for high-dimensional data |
| **Adaptability** | Static (no learning) | Dynamic (improves with data) |
| **Data Dependency** | Low | High (data is the fuel) |
| **Best Suited For** | Well-defined, deterministic tasks | Pattern recognition, prediction |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember ML Definition:** **"T-E-P"** → **Task, Experience, Performance** — these three are the *heart* of Mitchell's definition. Always use this in board exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breaking Down the Learning Process

The Machine Learning workflow can be broken into **six logical stages**:

1. **Data Collection (Experience E):** Gather raw data relevant to the problem domain. Without quality data, no ML model can perform reliably.
2. **Data Pre-processing:** Clean, normalize, and transform the data. Handle missing values, outliers, and categorical encodings.
3. **Model Selection (Task T):** Choose an appropriate learning algorithm based on the task — e.g., decision tree for classification, linear regression for continuous prediction.
4. **Training Phase:** Feed the training data to the model. The algorithm iteratively adjusts its internal parameters (weights, biases) to minimize a **loss function** $L(\theta)$.
5. **Evaluation (Performance P):** Test the trained model on *unseen* data using a metric like accuracy, precision, recall, or RMSE.
6. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Push the model into production and continuously monitor its performance drift over time.

### 2.2 The Underlying Objective — Loss Minimization

At the heart of almost every ML algorithm lies an **optimization problem**. The goal is to find model parameters $\theta$ that minimize a loss function:

$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta}\ L(\theta)
$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = the set of learnable parameters (weights and biases) of the model.
- $L(\theta)$ = the loss function measuring the difference between predicted and actual outputs.
- $\theta^{*}$ = the optimal set of parameters that yields the minimum loss.

Common loss functions include:
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** for regression: $L = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
- **Cross-Entropy Loss** for classification: $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Unit / Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T$ | Task the model must learn | Conceptual | Mitchell's Definition |
| $E$ | Experience = Training Dataset | Number of samples ($n$) | Mitchell's Definition |
| $P$ | Performance Metric | Accuracy: 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%) | Mitchell's Definition |
| $\theta$ | Model parameters (weights) | Continuous (Real numbers) | Optimization |
| $L(\theta)$ | Loss / Cost function | Non-negative scalar | Training |
| $\nabla L(\theta)$ | Gradient of Loss w.r.t $\theta$ | Vector of partial derivatives | Gradient Descent |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | Typically 0.001 to 0.1 | Optimization |
| $y_i$ | True label (ground truth) | Categorical / Continuous | Evaluation |
| $\hat{y}_i$ | Predicted output | Categorical / Continuous | Evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Do not confuse the *learning rate* $\eta$ with the *performance metric* $P$. The learning rate controls how fast a model updates its weights during training, while $P$ measures how well the model performs after training.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Machine Learning is no longer a niche research topic — it powers critical systems across every engineering discipline:

- **Healthcare:** Disease prediction from medical imaging (X-rays, MRIs).
- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring, crack detection in bridges.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Predictive maintenance of turbines, fault diagnosis.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Smart grid load forecasting, anomaly detection in circuits.
- **Computer Science (IT):** Spam filtering, recommendation engines, autonomous vehicles.

The unifying principle: **anywhere patterns exist in data, ML can extract value.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Derivation of Mitchell's Learning Criterion

Mitchell's definition can be formally expressed as a measurable improvement condition. A program is said to *learn* from experience $E$ with respect to task $T$ and performance measure $P$ **if and only if** its performance at $T$, as measured by $P$, **improves monotonically** with experience $E$.

Let:
- $P_t$ = Performance of the model at training step $t$.
- $E_t$ = Cumulative experience (data points) seen up to step $t$.

The learning condition is:

$$
P(E_{t+1}) > P(E_t) \quad \text{for all } t \geq 0
$$

Expanding this:

$$
P(E_{t+1}) - P(E_t) = \Delta P > 0
$$

Now, performance $P$ is typically computed as:

$$
P = 1 - \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert
$$

Substituting into the improvement condition:

$$
1 - \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert_{t+1} \; > \; 1 - \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert_{t}
$$

Simplifying the constant term $1$ on both sides:

$$
-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert_{t+1} \; > \; -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert_{t}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $-n$ (inequality flips sign):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert_{t+1} \; < \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert_{t}
$$

This reduces to the equivalent statement:

$$
L(\theta_{t+1}) < L(\theta_t)
$$

> **Interpretation:** Learning has mathematically occurred **if and only if** the loss at the next iteration is *strictly less* than the loss at the current iteration. This is the foundational *Why* behind every optimizer (Gradient Descent, Adam, RMSProp).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Illustrating Learning

**Problem:** A spam classifier is trained iteratively. At iteration $t=1$, it makes errors on 40 out of 100 emails. At iteration $t=2$, errors drop to 25. Verify whether the system has *learned*.

**Step 1:** Compute accuracy at $t=1$:

$$
P_1 = \frac{100 - 40}{100} = 0.60
$$

**Step 2:** Compute accuracy at $t=2$:

$$
P_2 = \frac{100 - 25}{100} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3:** Verify the learning condition $P_2 > P_1$:

$$
0.75 > 0.60 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the performance gain $\Delta P$:

$$
\Delta P = 0.75 - 0.60 = 0.15
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\Delta P > 0$, the system satisfies Mitchell's learning condition. Hence, the model is genuinely *learning* from experience.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Does ML Make Sense?

| Scenario | Use Traditional Programming? | Use Machine Learning? |
|---|---|---|
| Computing $a^2 + b^2 = c$ | Yes (deterministic) | No (overkill) |
| Predicting house prices from 50 features | No (too many rules) | Yes (regression model) |
| Detecting fraud in 1M transactions | No (rules explode) | Yes (anomaly detection) |
| Sorting an array | Yes (deterministic algorithm) | No (unnecessary) |
| Recognizing handwritten digits | No (impossible to hard-code) | Yes (image classification) |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Viva:** Apply ML when *the rules cannot be explicitly written down* but *examples are abundantly available*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Machine Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Collection] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Split Dataset]
    D --> E[Training Set]
    D --> F[Testing Set]
    E --> G[Model Training]
    G --> H[Trained ML Model]
    H --> I[Model Evaluation on Test Set]
    I --> J{Performance Acceptable?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Deploy to Production]
    J -->|No| L[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    L --> G
    K --> M[Monitor and Retrain Periodically]
```

### 4.2 Mitchell's Definition — Triadic Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Experience E\nTraining Data] --> ML[Machine Learning Algorithm]
    T[Task T\nProblem Objective] --> ML
    ML --> P[Performance P\nEvaluation Metric]
    P -->|Feedback Loop| ML
```

### 4.3 Traditional Programming vs Machine Learning Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TP[Traditional Programming]
        TP1[Input Data] --> TP2[Hand-coded Program]
        TP2 --> TP3[Output]
    end
    subgraph MLP[Machine Learning]
        ML1[Input Data] --> ML2[ML Algorithm]
        ML3[Desired Output] --> ML2
        ML2 --> ML4[Learned Program Rules]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Learning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input Layer: Raw Features X] --> S2[Model: Hypothesis Function h theta of X]
    S2 --> S3[Prediction: y hat]
    S3 --> S4[Loss Module: L of theta]
    S4 --> S5[Optimizer: Gradient Descent]
    S5 -->|Update Weights| S2
    S6[Ground Truth Labels Y] --> S4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the ML workflow", always show the **feedback loop** from evaluation back to training. Examiners specifically test whether students understand that ML is *iterative*, not one-shot.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define Machine Learning. State and briefly explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition.

**Model Answer:**
Machine Learning is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn from data without being explicitly programmed. According to **Tom Mitchell (1997)**, *a computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.* For example, in a spam filter (T), the system learns from labeled emails (E) and improves classification accuracy (P) over time. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between traditional programming and Machine Learning based on the role of data and program.

**Model Answer:**
In traditional programming, the program (rules) is hand-coded by the developer, and data is fed as input to produce output. In Machine Learning, both the data AND the desired output are provided as input, and the algorithm generates the program (rules) itself. Traditional programming is best for deterministic, well-defined tasks, while ML excels in pattern recognition tasks where explicit rules are infeasible. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice means you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Machine Learning with reference to Tom Mitchell's definition. Explain each component (Task, Experience, Performance) using a suitable real-world example of your choice.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (3 marks):**
Machine Learning is defined by Tom Mitchell as: *A program learns from experience E with respect to task T and performance measure P, if its performance at T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.*

**Components Explained (4 marks):**

Using the example of a **weather prediction system**:
- **Task (T):** Predict whether tomorrow will be sunny, rainy, or cloudy.
- **Experience (E):** Historical weather data over the past 10 years — including temperature, humidity, wind speed, and pressure.
- **Performance (P):** The percentage of days the prediction is correct (accuracy).

As the system is exposed to more historical data (E grows), the accuracy (P) at predicting weather (T) increases — this is the essence of learning.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the typical workflow of a Machine Learning system. Highlight the importance of the feedback loop between evaluation and training.

**Model Solution:**

**Workflow (5 marks):**

1. **Data Collection:** Gather raw data from sensors, databases, or APIs.
2. **Pre-processing:** Clean missing values, handle outliers, normalize features.
3. **Feature Engineering:** Select or construct meaningful input variables.
4. **Train/Test Split:** Divide data into a training set (e.g., 80%) and a test set (20%).
5. **Model Training:** Feed training data into the chosen algorithm (e.g., Decision Tree).
6. **Model Evaluation:** Test on unseen data using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall.
7. **Deployment:** If performance is acceptable, deploy to production.

**Feedback Loop Importance (2 marks):**
The feedback loop from evaluation back to training is **critical** because:
- It enables **continuous improvement** of the model.
- It allows **hyperparameter tuning** to optimize performance.
- In real-world systems, **data drift** (changing patterns over time) necessitates periodic retraining — which is impossible without a feedback loop.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Past Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with examples why Machine Learning is required. Why can't traditional rule-based programming handle problems like image recognition or speech recognition?

**Model Solution:**

**Why ML is Required (4 marks):**
- Modern problems involve **high-dimensional data** (images have millions of pixels).
- The relationships between inputs and outputs are **non-linear and complex**.
- It is **impossible to hand-code rules** for every possible input variation.
- The system must **adapt over time** as new data arrives.

**Why Traditional Programming Fails (3 marks):**
- A handwritten digit (e.g., the digit "7") can be written in millions of distinct ways — slanted, thick, thin, rotated. Writing explicit rules for each variation is infeasible.
- In speech recognition, the same word spoken by different people sounds vastly different due to accent, pitch, and background noise. Rule-based systems cannot handle this variability.
- ML solves this by **learning statistical patterns** directly from large datasets instead of relying on hand-crafted rules.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the mathematical formulation of the learning objective. Explain how gradient descent updates the model parameters to minimize the loss function.

**Model Solution:**

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**

The learning objective is to find optimal parameters $\theta^*$:

$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} L(\theta)
$$

Where $L(\theta)$ is the loss function, e.g., for regression:

$$
L(\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Gradient Descent Update Rule (4 marks):**

The parameters are updated iteratively using the negative gradient of the loss:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t)
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = learning rate (step size)
- $\nabla L(\theta_t)$ = gradient of loss with respect to parameters
- The negative sign ensures we move *against* the gradient, i.e., towards the minimum.

**Worked Example (Bonus):**
If $L(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^2$, then $\nabla L = 2(\theta - 5)$. Starting at $\theta_0 = 10$ with $\eta = 0.1$:

- $\theta_1 = 10 - 0.1 \cdot 2(10-5) = 10 - 1 = 9$
- $\theta_2 = 9 - 0.1 \cdot 2(9-5) = 9 - 0.8 = 8.2$

The parameters gradually approach the optimum $\theta^* = 5$, demonstrating learning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** define ML as "a system that thinks like a human" — this is the definition of AI, not ML. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Always mention Task (T), Experience (E), Performance (P)** in Mitchell's definition. Omitting any one component results in an incomplete answer.
> 3. In numerical problems, **show substitution steps** clearly. Writing only the final answer without intermediate steps costs method marks.
> 4. When drawing workflow diagrams, the **feedback loop is mandatory**. A linear flowchart without the loop will lose at least 2 marks.
> 5. Avoid using vague terms like "ML is smart" or "ML works magically" — be precise and use technical vocabulary (loss function, parameters, optimization).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- Machine Learning is a **sub-field of AI** that learns from data without explicit programming.
- **Mitchell's Definition** is the gold standard: $P$ improves with $E$ on task $T$.
- The three pillars of Mitchell's definition are **Task (T), Experience (E), Performance (P)**.
- The ML objective is mathematically: $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} L(\theta)$.
- The fundamental learning condition: $L(\theta_{t+1}) < L(\theta_t)$ — loss must decrease monotonically.
- Traditional programming = **Data + Program → Output**; ML = **Data + Output → Program**.
- Use ML when **rules are infeasible to hand-code** but **examples are abundant**.
- Loss functions: **MSE for regression**, **Cross-Entropy for classification**.
- Gradient Descent update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla L(\theta_t)$.
- The ML workflow is **iterative**, not linear — always include the feedback loop in diagrams.
- High-yield exam keywords: **Mitchell's Definition, Loss Function, Gradient Descent, Training Data, Feedback Loop, Performance Metric**.
- Common application domains: **Healthcare, Finance, NLP, Computer Vision, Predictive Maintenance**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Machine Learning - Supervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Machine Learning - Supervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Machine Learning

According to the classical formulation widely adopted in KTU-referenced academic literature, **Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of artificial intelligence that enables computational systems to learn patterns, relationships, and decision boundaries from data without being explicitly programmed for every specific case. Instead of relying on hard-coded instructions, an ML model improves its performance on a given task **P** with respect to a performance metric **M**, as its experience **E** (in the form of data) increases over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitchell's Formal Definition (1997):**
> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

## 1.2 The Three (Plus One) Paradigms of Machine Learning

Machine Learning systems are taxonomically classified based on the **nature of the supervisory signal** available during training:

| # | Learning Paradigm | Supervisory Signal | Core Objective |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Supervised Learning** | Labeled input-output pairs (X, Y) | Learn the mapping function **f: X → Y** |
| 2 | **Unsupervised Learning** | Unlabeled data (X only) | Discover hidden structure, clusters, or patterns |
| 3 | **Reinforcement Learning** | Reward / Penalty signals from environment | Learn an optimal policy maximizing cumulative reward |
| 4 | **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Small labeled + large unlabeled set | Leverage unlabeled data with limited labels |

## 1.3 Supervised Learning — The Heart of This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Supervised Learning** is the machine learning paradigm wherein the algorithm is trained on a **labeled dataset** — i.e., each training example is a pair consisting of an input vector **x** and its corresponding target output **y**. The objective is to learn a generalized mapping (hypothesis) **h(x) ≈ y** that can accurately predict outputs for *unseen* inputs.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student and Teacher" Model

Imagine a young student preparing for the Kerala SSLC Mathematics examination. The student is given a stack of **previous year question papers (training data)** where **every question already has the correct answer (labels)** marked by the teacher. By repeatedly studying these solved examples, the student identifies the *pattern* — what type of calculation each question requires. When faced with a **new, unseen question** in the actual exam, the student applies the learned pattern to produce a correct answer.

- **Student** → ML Algorithm (e.g., Decision Tree, SVM, Neural Network)
- **Previous papers with answers** → Labeled Training Dataset
- **Pattern identification** → Model Training / Learning
- **New exam question** → Test Input
- **Student's answer** → Predicted Output

This is precisely how supervised learning operates: **the model learns the input–output correspondence from labeled examples and generalizes to novel inputs.**

## 1.4 Why Supervised Learning Matters in Engineering

Supervised learning is the most industrially deployed and academically mature branch of ML. Its importance to B.Tech engineers stems from:

- **Predictive Maintenance** in manufacturing (predicting machine failure)
- **Medical Diagnosis** (classifying tumors as malignant or benign)
- **Spam Filtering** in email systems
- **Credit Scoring** in banking & fintech
- **Weather Forecasting** and **Demand Prediction**
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** and **Speech Recognition**

> [!TIP]
> Whenever your dataset has a **"right answer"** attached to each input, you are dealing with a supervised learning problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Mathematical Framework of Supervised Learning

Let us define the supervised learning problem rigorously using mathematical notation consistent with KTU 2024 Scheme graduate-level expectations.

### 2.1.1 The Dataset

A supervised learning dataset is a finite collection of **n** labeled examples:

$$D = \{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (x^{(n)}, y^{(n)})\}$$

Where:
- $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a **d-dimensional feature vector** representing the *i-th* input
- $y^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}$ (regression) or $y^{(i)} \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ (classification) is the corresponding **target / label**
- $n$ is the total number of training samples

### 2.1.2 The Hypothesis Space

The learning algorithm searches within a **hypothesis space** $\mathcal{H}$ for a function $h \in \mathcal{H}$ that best approximates the true underlying mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.

$$h_{\theta}(x) \approx f(x)$$

Where $\theta$ denotes the **parameter vector** of the model (e.g., weights in a neural network, coefficients in linear regression).

### 2.1.3 The Loss Function

To measure how well a hypothesis performs, we use a **loss function** $L(h(x), y)$ that quantifies the penalty for incorrect predictions:

$$\mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$$

The training objective is to find the optimal parameters:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathcal{J}(\theta)$$

## 2.2 The Two Sub-Branches of Supervised Learning

### 📊 (A) Classification

When the target variable $y$ is **categorical / discrete**, the task is **classification**.

- **Binary Classification:** $y \in \{0, 1\}$ — e.g., Spam vs. Ham
- **Multiclass Classification:** $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ — e.g., Digit recognition (0–9)
- **Multilabel Classification:** Multiple labels per instance

### 📈 (B) Regression

When the target variable $y$ is **continuous / real-valued**, the task is **regression**.

- e.g., Predicting house prices, temperature forecasting, stock market prediction

## 2.3 🧮 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you are expected to know for Module 1 examination questions on Supervised Learning. **Memorize these — they appear every year.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Use Case | Key Symbol Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Hypothesis (Linear) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | Simple regression line | $\theta_0$ = bias/intercept |
| 2 | Hypothesis (Multivariate) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j$ | Multi-feature regression | $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ |
| 3 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$ | Regression loss | Measures avg. squared deviation |
| 4 | Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust regression loss | Less sensitive to outliers |
| 5 | Root Mean Squared Error | $RMSE = \sqrt{J_{MSE}}$ | Interpretable in same units as y | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy Loss (Binary) | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum [y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Binary classification | $\hat{y}$ = predicted probability |
| 7 | Cross-Entropy Loss (Multiclass) | $J(\theta) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$ | K-class classification | Uses one-hot encoded $y_k$ |
| 8 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Iterative parameter optimization | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| 9 | Updated $\theta_j$ for Linear Reg. | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{n} \sum (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Specific to linear regression | Simultaneous update required |
| 10 | Accuracy | $Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification evaluation | Range: [0, 1] or [0, 100%] |
| 11 | Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions | High precision → low false alarms |
| 12 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | High recall → low miss rate |
| 13 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R | Balanced P-R trade-off |
| 14 | Normal Equation | $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Closed-form linear regression | Avoids iterative descent |
| 15 | Sigmoid Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Logistic regression output | Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$ |
| 16 | Softmax Function | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Multiclass probability | Outputs sum to 1 |
| 17 | Bias-Variance Tradeoff | $E[(y - \hat{f})^2] = Bias^2 + Variance + \sigma^2$ | Generalization analysis | Lower ≠ always better |

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How" — Operational Logic Flow

The supervised learning pipeline follows a strict sequence:

1. **Data Collection** → Gather labeled examples $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$.
2. **Data Preprocessing** → Handle missing values, normalize/standardize features, encode categorical variables.
3. **Train-Validation-Test Split** → Typically 70%/15%/15% or 80%/20%.
4. **Model Selection** → Choose a hypothesis class (e.g., linear, polynomial, tree-based).
5. **Loss Function Definition** → Quantify prediction error.
6. **Optimization** → Minimize the loss (gradient descent, normal equation, etc.).
7. **Evaluation** → Measure performance on unseen test data.
8. **Hyperparameter Tuning** → Cross-validation, grid search, random search.
9. **Deployment** → Integrate the trained model into production systems.

> [!NOTE]
> The **fundamental assumption** of supervised learning is that training data and test data are drawn from the **same underlying distribution** — this is the **i.i.d. assumption** (independent and identically distributed).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Supervised Learning Systems

| Domain | Application | Algorithm Typically Used |
|--------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Healthcare | Disease diagnosis from X-rays | CNN, Random Forest |
| Finance | Credit card fraud detection | Logistic Regression, XGBoost |
| Retail | Customer churn prediction | SVM, Gradient Boosting |
| Automotive | Lane and pedestrian detection | Deep CNNs |
| NLP | Sentiment analysis | BERT, Naive Bayes |
| Agriculture | Crop yield prediction | Linear Regression, LSTM |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

Linear regression is the canonical supervised learning algorithm. We derive the parameter update rule from first principles — a question that **frequently appears in KTU Module 1 examinations**.

### Step 1: Define the Hypothesis

For a single feature $x$, the linear hypothesis is:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

For $d$ features in vector form:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j \quad \text{where } x_0 = 1$$

> *Logic: We are assuming the relationship between x and y is linear. The parameters $\theta_j$ are the slopes, and $\theta_0$ is the y-intercept.*

### Step 2: Define the Cost Function (MSE)

The cost function $J(\theta)$ measures the average squared error across all $n$ training examples:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^{2}$$

> *Logic: We square the differences to (a) penalize larger errors more heavily, and (b) ensure positive and negative errors don't cancel out. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included for mathematical convenience (it cancels during differentiation).*

### Step 3: Compute the Partial Derivative

We compute $\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ to determine the slope of the cost function with respect to each parameter.

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}(h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$$

> *Logic: Applying the chain rule. The derivative of $(h - y)^2$ is $2(h - y)$, and the derivative of $h = \theta^T x$ with respect to $\theta_j$ is $x_j$.*

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 4: Formulate the Update Rule

Gradient descent updates each parameter by moving it in the direction of steepest descent (negative gradient):

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$$

> *Logic: The negative sign ensures we move *toward* the minimum. The learning rate $\alpha$ controls the step size. If $\alpha$ is too small, convergence is slow; if too large, we may overshoot the minimum.*

Substituting the partial derivative from Step 3:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

> *Logic: For each parameter, we compute the average error times the corresponding feature value across all training examples, scaled by the learning rate, and subtract it from the current parameter value.*

### Step 5: Convergence Criterion

The algorithm is said to have converged when either:

$$|J(\theta)^{(t)} - J(\theta)^{(t-1)}| < \epsilon$$

or when the gradient norm falls below a threshold:

$$\left\| \nabla J(\theta) \right\| < \epsilon$$

> *Logic: Once the cost function stops changing significantly between iterations, the model has effectively reached (or is extremely close to) the optimal parameter values.*

## 3.2 Derivation: Logistic Regression Hypothesis (Sigmoid)

For binary classification, linear regression is unsuitable as it can output values outside $[0, 1]$. We need to map predictions to probabilities using the **sigmoid function**.

### Step 1: Start with the Linear Combination

$$z = \theta^T x = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \ldots + \theta_d x_d$$

### Step 2: Apply the Sigmoid Transformation

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

> *Logic: The sigmoid function squashes any real-valued input $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ into the open interval $(0, 1)$, which we interpret as a probability. As $z \to \infty$, $\sigma(z) \to 1$; as $z \to -\infty$, $\sigma(z) \to 0$.*

### Step 3: Derive the Cross-Entropy Loss

For a single training example, the loss function is:

$$L(\hat{y}, y) = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})]$$

> *Logic: This is derived from the principle of Maximum Likelihood Estimation. If $y = 1$, the loss penalizes low $\hat{y}$ (we want predicted probability close to 1). If $y = 0$, the loss penalizes high $\hat{y}$ (we want predicted probability close to 0). The logarithm heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions.*

### Step 4: Full Cost Function (Average over all examples)

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison for KTU Exam

| Algorithm | Task Type | Loss Function | Key Strength | Key Limitation |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| Linear Regression | Regression | MSE | Simple, interpretable, closed-form | Assumes linearity |
| Logistic Regression | Classification | Cross-Entropy | Probabilistic output, fast | Linear decision boundary only |
| Decision Tree (CART) | Both | Gini / Entropy / MSE | Handles non-linearity, interpretable | Prone to overfitting |
| k-Nearest Neighbors | Both | Distance-based (no training) | Lazy learner, no assumptions | Slow at prediction, curse of dimensionality |
| Naive Bayes | Classification | Log-likelihood | Works with small data, fast | Strong independence assumption |
| Support Vector Machine | Both | Hinge loss | Effective in high-dim spaces | Slow on large datasets |
| Random Forest | Both | Ensemble of trees | Robust, handles missing data | Less interpretable |
| Neural Networks | Both | MSE / Cross-Entropy | Universal function approximator | Requires lots of data, black-box |

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Define **Supervised Learning**. With a suitable example, explain how it differs from **Unsupervised Learning**.

**Model Answer:**

Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where the model is trained on a **labeled dataset** — each input $x^{(i)}$ is paired with a known output $y^{(i)}$. The algorithm learns the mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ by minimizing a loss function that measures the discrepancy between predicted and actual outputs.

**Example:** Training a model to classify emails as *"Spam"* or *"Not Spam"* using a dataset of 10,000 pre-labeled emails.

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Labels | Available | Not available |
| Goal | Predict output | Discover structure |
| Example Algorithm | Logistic Regression, SVM | K-Means, PCA |
| Example Task | Email spam detection | Customer segmentation |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for example + 1 for differentiation table)** ✅

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> Explain the concepts of **classification** and **regression** with one real-world example each. State the type of output variable in each case.

**Model Answer:**

- **Classification** is a supervised learning task where the target variable $y$ is **categorical (discrete)**. The model predicts which category/class an input belongs to.  
  **Example:** Predicting whether a tumor is *Malignant* or *Benign* based on medical imaging features. Here $y \in \{0, 1\}$.

- **Regression** is a supervised learning task where the target variable $y$ is **continuous (real-valued)**. The model predicts a numerical value.  
  **Example:** Predicting the *selling price of a house* in Kerala (in lakhs ₹) based on area, location, and number of rooms. Here $y \in \mathbb{R}$.

**(3 marks: 1.5 marks per concept — 0.75 for definition + 0.75 for example each)** ✅

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **supervised learning workflow** in detail, listing all the major steps from data collection to model deployment. Discuss the role of the **train-test split** and why it is essential.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] For a **linear regression** model with hypothesis $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ and cost function $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$, derive the **gradient descent update rule** for $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$. Explain the role of the **learning rate $\alpha$** and what happens if it is too small or too large.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The supervised learning workflow consists of **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Problem Definition** — Clearly identify whether the task is classification or regression, and define the success metric.
2. **Data Collection** — Gather a representative labeled dataset. Quality and quantity of data are paramount.
3. **Data Preprocessing** — Handle missing values, remove duplicates, encode categorical features, scale numerical features (standardization/normalization).
4. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** — Visualize data distributions, detect outliers, understand correlations.
5. **Train-Validation-Test Split** — Partition data into training (typically 60–80%), validation (10–20%), and test (10–20%) sets. The **test set must remain untouched** until final evaluation to provide an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
6. **Model Selection & Training** — Choose an appropriate algorithm, define the loss function, and optimize parameters using techniques like gradient descent.
7. **Model Evaluation & Tuning** — Use metrics (accuracy, F1, RMSE) on the validation set. Tune hyperparameters via cross-validation.
8. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Integrate the model into production. Monitor for data drift and retrain periodically.

**Role of Train-Test Split:** It prevents **data leakage** and provides an honest estimate of how the model will perform on **unseen real-world data**, helping detect **overfitting**.

**(7 marks: 1 mark per major step + 1 mark for the role of train-test split explanation)** ✅

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Hypothesis: $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$
- Cost: $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_0$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$$

> *Logic: Derivative of $(h-y)^2$ w.r.t. $\theta_0$ passes through $h = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$, and $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_0} = 1$. The factor of 2 cancels with $\frac{1}{2}$ in the cost.*

**Step 2:** Compute partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_1$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

> *Logic: $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_1} = x$, which is carried into the chain rule expansion.*

**Step 3:** Gradient Descent Update Rule:

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

> *Logic: Both parameters are updated **simultaneously** (not sequentially) using values from the previous iteration.*

**Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$:**

| Condition | Effect on Training |
|-----------|---------------------|
| $\alpha$ too small | **Slow convergence** — many iterations needed; risk of getting stuck in local minima for non-convex problems |
| $\alpha$ too large | **Overshooting** — algorithm may diverge, fail to converge, or oscillate around the minimum |
| $\alpha$ just right | **Optimal convergence** — fast and stable minimization of $J(\theta)$ |

**(7 marks: 2 marks derivative + 2 marks update rule + 2 marks learning rate + 1 mark conclusion)** ✅

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **loss function** and **cost function**. Derive the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function for linear regression and explain why it is preferred over **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** in many practical scenarios.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **Linear Regression** and **Logistic Regression** in terms of: (i) problem type, (ii) hypothesis form, (iii) loss function, (iv) output range, and (v) one suitable real-world application for each.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definitions:**

- **Loss Function** $L(\hat{y}, y)$: Measures the error for a **single training example**. It quantifies how wrong the prediction $\hat{y}$ is compared to the true label $y$.
- **Cost Function** $J(\theta)$: Measures the **average loss over the entire training set** of $n$ examples. It is the objective function that the learning algorithm minimizes.

**Derivation of MSE:**

Starting from the principle of **least squares** — we want to find parameters $\theta$ that minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$J(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y^{(i)} - h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}))^{2}$$

> *Logic: We square the differences to (a) eliminate sign (errors don't cancel), (b) penalize large errors quadratically, and (c) make the function differentiable and convex.*

Normalizing by the number of samples:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y^{(i)} - h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}))^{2}$$

**Why MSE is preferred over MAE in many cases:**

| Aspect | MSE | MAE |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Differentiability | Smooth, differentiable everywhere (faster optimization) | Not differentiable at $y = \hat{y}$ |
| Outlier Sensitivity | Highly sensitive (squared penalty) | Robust to outliers |
| Mathematical Properties | Convex, unique global minimum | Convex, but slower convergence |
| Gradient Behavior | Large errors → large gradients → fast correction | Constant gradient regardless of error size |

MSE is preferred when:
- Outliers are rare or represent genuine extreme events
- We want faster convergence via gradient-based optimization
- We need a smooth, differentiable objective

**(7 marks: 1 mark each for definitions + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks comparison justification)** ✅

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

| # | Aspect | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| (i) | **Problem Type** | Regression (predicts continuous $y$) | Classification (predicts discrete $y \in \{0,1\}$) |
| (ii) | **Hypothesis Form** | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x$ (linear, unbounded output) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$ (sigmoid-bounded) |
| (iii) | **Loss Function** | Mean Squared Error: $J = \frac{1}{n} \sum (h - y)^2$ | Binary Cross-Entropy: $J = -\frac{1}{n} \sum [y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ |
| (iv) | **Output Range** | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $(0, 1)$ — interpreted as probability |
| (v) | **Real-World Application** | Predicting house prices in Kochi based on area & locality | Predicting whether a loan applicant will default (Yes/No) |

**Key Conceptual Insight:** Logistic regression is essentially linear regression wrapped in a sigmoid activation, transforming a regression output into a probability suitable for binary classification.

**(7 marks: 1 mark per comparison row + 2 marks for the insight at the end)** ✅

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing loss and cost function** — A loss function is for *one* example; the cost function is the *average* over all examples. Examiners *deduct marks* if you use them interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting simultaneous update** — In gradient descent, $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ MUST be updated **simultaneously** using the values from the *previous iteration*. Updating one before the other introduces a bug.
>
> 3. **Misclassifying classification vs regression** — If the question says *"predict"* and gives a *continuous* target, it's regression. If the target is a *category*, it's classification. This is the #1 conceptual trap.
>
> 4. **Not stating the i.i.d. assumption** — When asked about supervised learning assumptions, students often forget to mention the **independent and identically distributed** nature of training and test data.
>
> 5. **Skipping units / ranges in answers** — Always specify the **range** of accuracy (0 to 1), **type** of loss (MSE vs. cross-entropy), and the **output domain** in your answers.
>
> 6. **Missing the bias-variance tradeoff discussion** — Any complete answer on supervised learning *should* mention the tradeoff between model complexity and generalization.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ Supervised learning uses **labeled data** $(x, y)$ pairs
- ✅ Two main sub-types: **Classification** (discrete) and **Regression** (continuous)
- ✅ Hypothesis: $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x$ is the model's prediction function
- ✅ Cost functions: **MSE** for regression, **Cross-Entropy** for classification
- ✅ Gradient Descent update: $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$
- ✅ Learning rate $\alpha$ controls step size — too small = slow; too large = divergence
- ✅ Evaluation metrics — Regression: **MSE, RMSE, MAE, R²**; Classification: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score**
- ✅ Confusion matrix: **TP, FP, TN, FN** form the basis of all classification metrics
- ✅ The **i.i.d. assumption** is fundamental to supervised learning theory
- ✅ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: simple models → high bias/low variance; complex models → low bias/high variance
- ✅ **Overfitting** = model memorizes training data; **Underfitting** = model too simple to capture patterns
- ✅ Key algorithms: **Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, SVM, KNN, Naive Bayes, Random Forest, Neural Networks**
- ✅ **Sigmoid** maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$; **Softmax** generalizes sigmoid to $K$ classes
- ✅ Train-validation-test split is essential to detect overfitting
- ✅ Cross-validation (k-fold) provides robust hyperparameter tuning

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — UNSUPERVISED LEARNING & REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## 🎯 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning (USL)** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an algorithm is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ consisting of **input samples without any corresponding target labels**. The goal is to discover hidden structure, latent representations, groupings, or low-dimensional embeddings inherent in the data.

In contrast to Supervised Learning (which learns a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$), USL learns the **intrinsic geometry** of $\mathcal{X}$ itself. Formally, it can be viewed as either:

- **Density Estimation:** approximating $p(x)$ directly,
- **Representation Learning:** finding a mapping $g: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ to a useful latent space $\mathcal{Z}$, or
- **Clustering:** partitioning $\mathcal{X}$ into $K$ disjoint groups $C_1, \ldots, C_K$.

**Real-World Analogy 🧒:** Imagine a child given a basket of mixed fruits (apples, oranges, bananas) with **no teacher telling which is which**. The child groups them by *color, shape, and size* — apples with apples, bananas with bananas. The child has performed **unsupervised clustering** based on perceived similarity. The "labels" were never told; they were *inferred* from the data's own structure.

**Sub-Categories of USL (per KTU 2024 syllabus):**
1. **Clustering** — partitioning data into groups (K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN).
2. **Dimensionality Reduction** — projecting high-dimensional data to a lower-dimensional manifold (PCA, t-SNE).
3. **Association Rule Mining** — discovering co-occurrence patterns (Apriori Algorithm).

---

### 1.2 Reinforcement Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a goal-directed learning paradigm where an **agent** interacts with an **environment** by performing **actions** and receiving **rewards** (or penalties). Over time, the agent learns an optimal **policy** $\pi^*$ that maximizes the expected cumulative reward.

The mathematical scaffold of RL is the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, formally defined as the tuple:
$$\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ = set of states,
- $\mathcal{A}$ = set of actions,
- $P(s' \vert s, a)$ = transition probability,
- $R(s, a)$ = reward function,
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ = discount factor (typically $\gamma = 0.9$).

**Real-World Analogy 🎮:** Think of training a pet dog. When the dog sits on command, you give it a treat (positive reward). When it chews your shoes, you say "No!" (negative reward). The dog, over hundreds of repetitions, learns the *policy* "sitting → treat; chewing → scolding." It never sees a labeled dataset — it learns purely by **trial-and-error interaction**.

**Key Distinction from Supervised & Unsupervised Learning:**

| Paradigm | Feedback Type | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Labeled (correct answer) | Minimize prediction error |
| Unsupervised | No feedback | Discover structure |
| **Reinforcement** | **Scalar reward (delayed, sparse)** | **Maximize cumulative reward** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes the **Exploration vs Exploitation trade-off** and **Q-Learning** as core algorithms for Module 1.

---

### 1.3 Why These Two Together in Module 1?

Both USL and RL share a common philosophical theme: **learning without explicit labeled supervision**. USL extracts structure from static datasets; RL extracts policy from dynamic interactions. Together they represent the *non-supervised* half of the ML spectrum.

# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means Clustering — Operational Breakdown

**Objective:** Partition $N$ data points into $K$ clusters by minimizing **intra-cluster variance** (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares, WCSS).

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. **Initialize** $K$ cluster centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K\}$ randomly (or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment Step:** For each point $x_i$, assign it to the nearest centroid.
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points assigned to it.
4. **Repeat** steps 2–3 until convergence (centroids stop moving or change is below a threshold $\epsilon$).

**The "Why":** K-Means alternates between two *coupled* optimization problems — (a) given centroids, optimal assignment; (b) given assignments, optimal centroids. This is the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** principle applied to a mixture of isotropic Gaussians.

**Limitation:** Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters; sensitive to initialization → use **K-Means++** for seeding.

---

## 2.2 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

**Objective:** Project $D$-dimensional data onto a $k$-dimensional subspace ($k \ll D$) that preserves maximum variance.

**Mechanism:**
1. Center the data: $\tilde{x}_i = x_i - \bar{x}$.
2. Compute the covariance matrix: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} \tilde{X}^\top \tilde{X}$.
3. Perform eigendecomposition: $\Sigma = V \Lambda V^\top$.
4. Select top $k$ eigenvectors as principal axes.
5. Project: $Z = \tilde{X} W_k$, where $W_k$ contains top $k$ eigenvectors.

**Why PCA works:** The eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ point along directions of maximum variance. By keeping the top $k$, we retain the directions carrying the most information.

---

## 2.3 Apriori Algorithm (Association Rules)

Used for **market-basket analysis**. Discovers rules of the form $X \Rightarrow Y$ where $X, Y$ are item sets.

**Support:** $s(X) = \frac{\text{transactions containing } X}{\text{total transactions}}$

**Confidence:** $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$

**Lift:** $\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)}$ — values > 1 indicate positive correlation.

**Apriori Principle:** *If an itemset is infrequent, all its supersets are infrequent.* This prunes the search space exponentially.

---

## 2.4 Reinforcement Learning — The MDP Framework

### The Bellman Equation (Optimal Value Function):
$$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^*(s') \right]$$

### Q-Function (Action-Value Function):
$$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

### Q-Learning Update Rule (Model-Free, Off-Policy):
$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a) \right]$$

The term in brackets is the **Temporal Difference (TD) error** $\delta_t$.

---

## 2.5 Exploration vs Exploitation — ε-Greedy Strategy

- With probability $\epsilon$, choose a **random** action (exploration).
- With probability $1-\epsilon$, choose the **greedy** action $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ (exploitation).
- $\epsilon$ is often **decayed** over time: $\epsilon_t = \epsilon_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | K-Means Objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | $\mu_k$ = centroid of cluster $C_k$ |
| 2 | Centroid Update | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Mean of assigned points |
| 3 | PCA Reconstruction Error | $E_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^2$ | $\hat{x}_i$ = reconstructed point |
| 4 | Variance Retained (PCA) | $\text{Retained} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{D} \lambda_i}$ | $\lambda_i$ = eigenvalues |
| 5 | Support | $s(X) = \frac{\sigma(X)}{N}$ | $\sigma(X)$ = frequency count |
| 6 | Confidence | $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$ | Conditional probability |
| 7 | Lift | $\text{Lift} = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)}$ | $> 1$ = positive correlation |
| 8 | Bellman Optimality | $V^*(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^*(s')]$ | Recursive value |
| 9 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| 10 | TD Error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$ | Drives learning |
| 11 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative future reward |
| 12 | ε-Greedy Decay | $\epsilon_t = \epsilon_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = decay rate |

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **K-Means** → Customer segmentation in marketing, image compression (vector quantization), anomaly detection pre-processing.
- **PCA** → Face recognition (Eigenfaces), genomics (gene expression), denoising sensor data in IoT.
- **Apriori** → Recommender systems ("Customers who bought X also bought Y"), network intrusion logs.
- **Q-Learning** → Robotics (robotic arm grasping), game AI (AlphaGo), autonomous driving, dynamic pricing in e-commerce.

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 K-Means Convergence Proof Sketch (Exhaustive Derivation)

We want to show the K-Means objective $J$ is non-increasing across iterations.

**Setup:**
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{x_i \in C_k\} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Step 1 — Assignment Step Minimizes $J$ for Fixed Centroids:**

For a given $\mu_k$, assigning $x_i$ to the nearest centroid is equivalent to:
$$C_k^{(t)} = \{x_i : \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \Vert^2, \forall j \neq k\}$$

This is the *argmin* operation. Hence:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 \leq \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k} \mathbb{1}\{x_i \in C_k^{(t-1)}\} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

Therefore $J^{(t)} \leq J^{(t-1)}$ after assignment.

**Step 2 — Centroid Update Minimizes $J$ for Fixed Assignments:**

Given fixed $C_k$, we minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\mu_k$ by setting the partial derivative to zero:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$

Solving:
$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

**Step 3 — Monotonicity of $J$:**

Combining Steps 1 and 2:
$$J^{(t+1)} = \sum_{i} \min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t+1)} \Vert^2 \leq \sum_{i} \min_k \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert^2 = J^{(t)}$$

Since $J \geq 0$ is bounded below, the algorithm **converges** to a (local) minimum. ∎

---

## 3.2 PCA — Eigendecomposition of Covariance Matrix (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Data Centering:**
$$\tilde{X} = X - \mathbf{1} \bar{x}^\top$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ and $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones column vector.

**Step 2 — Covariance Matrix:**
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} \tilde{X}^\top \tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times D}$$

**Step 3 — Eigendecomposition:**
$$\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$$

Sort eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_D \geq 0$.

**Step 4 — Projection Matrix:**
$$W_k = [v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \ldots \mid v_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times k}$$

**Step 5 — Low-Dimensional Embedding:**
$$Z = \tilde{X} W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times k}$$

**Step 6 — Reconstruction (Optional):**
$$\hat{X} = Z W_k^\top + \mathbf{1} \bar{x}^\top$$

**Step 7 — Reconstruction Error:**
$$E_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^2 = \sum_{j=k+1}^{D} \lambda_j$$

This is a beautiful result: **the total reconstruction error equals the sum of the discarded eigenvalues**.

---

## 3.3 Q-Learning Convergence — Worked Numerical Example

**Setup:** A grid-world with 4 states $\{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ and 2 actions $\{a, b\}$. Discount $\gamma = 0.9$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Initial Q-Table (all zeros):**

| | $a$ | $b$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_4$ | 0 | 0 |

**Episode 1 Experience:** $(s_1, a) \rightarrow r=5, s'=s_3$

**Q-Update:**
$$Q(s_1, a) \leftarrow Q(s_1, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_3, a') - Q(s_1, a) \right]$$

Plugging in values:
$$Q(s_1, a) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \left[ 5 + 0.9 \cdot \max(0, 0) - 0 \right] = 0.5 \cdot 5 = 2.5$$

**Updated Q-Table:**

| | $a$ | $b$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 2.5 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_4$ | 0 | 0 |

**Episode 2:** $(s_3, b) \rightarrow r=10, s'=s_4$
$$Q(s_3, b) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \left[ 10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0 \right] = 5.0$$

**Episode 3:** $(s_1, a) \rightarrow r=5, s'=s_3$ (again)
$$Q(s_1, a) \leftarrow 2.5 + 0.5 \left[ 5 + 0.9 \cdot \max(0, 5.0) - 2.5 \right]$$
$$= 2.5 + 0.5 \left[ 5 + 4.5 - 2.5 \right] = 2.5 + 0.5 \cdot 7.0 = 2.5 + 3.5 = 6.0$$

**Observation:** Repeated exposure to the same transition **propagates reward backward** through the Q-table. This is the essence of **Temporal Difference learning** — the value of $s_1$ improves because $s_3$ is now known to be valuable.

**Convergence Theorem (Watkins & Dayan, 1992):** Q-Learning converges to the optimal $Q^*$ with probability 1 if:
- All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often (exploration condition),
- $\alpha_t$ satisfies the Robbins-Monro conditions: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.

---

## 3.4 Apriori — Worked Example

**Transactions:**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | {Bread, Butter, Milk} |
| T2 | {Bread, Butter} |
| T3 | {Butter, Milk} |
| T4 | {Bread, Milk} |
| T5 | {Bread, Butter, Milk} |

**Minimum Support** = 0.4 (i.e., 2 out of 5 transactions).

**Step 1 — Frequent 1-Itemsets:**
- {Bread} → 4/5 = 0.8 ✓
- {Butter} → 4/5 = 0.8 ✓
- {Milk} → 4/5 = 0.8 ✓

**Step 2 — Candidate 2-Itemsets:**
- {Bread, Butter} → 3/5 = 0.6 ✓
- {Bread, Milk} → 3/5 = 0.6 ✓
- {Butter, Milk} → 3/5 = 0.6 ✓

**Step 3 — Candidate 3-Itemsets:**
- {Bread, Butter, Milk} → 2/5 = 0.4 ✓ (just meets threshold)

**Step 4 — Rule Generation from {Bread, Butter, Milk}:**

| Rule | Confidence | Lift |
|---|---|---|
| Bread $\Rightarrow$ Butter, Milk | $\frac{2/5}{4/5} = 0.50$ | $\frac{0.50}{0.80} = 0.625$ |
| Butter $\Rightarrow$ Bread, Milk | $\frac{2/5}{4/5} = 0.50$ | $\frac{0.50}{0.80} = 0.625$ |
| Milk $\Rightarrow$ Bread, Butter | $\frac{2/5}{4/5} = 0.50$ | $\frac{0.50}{0.80} = 0.625$ |
| Bread, Butter $\Rightarrow$ Milk | $\frac{2/5}{3/5} = 0.667$ | $\frac{0.667}{0.80} = 0.833$ |
| Bread, Milk $\Rightarrow$ Butter | $\frac{2/5}{3/5} = 0.667$ | $\frac{0.833}{0.80} = 1.042$ ✅ |
| Butter, Milk $\Rightarrow$ Bread | $\frac{2/5}{3/5} = 0.667$ | $\frac{0.833}{0.80} = 1.042$ ✅ |

Lift $> 1$ indicates the rule is *positively correlated* (non-trivial).

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Clustering — Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start K-Means]) --> initA[Initialize K centroids randomly or via K-Means++]
    initA --> assignA[Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    assignA --> updateA[Update Step: Recompute centroids as cluster means]
    updateA --> checkA{Convergence reached?}
    checkA -->|No| assignA
    checkA -->|Yes| outputA[Output final clusters and centroids]
    outputA --> stopA([Stop])
```

## 4.2 PCA Pipeline — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawX[Raw Data X N x D] --> centerX[Step 1: Mean Center X tilde]
    centerX --> covX[Step 2: Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    covX --> eigenX[Step 3: Eigendecomposition Sigma V Lambda V transpose]
    eigenX --> sortX[Step 4: Sort eigenvalues descending]
    sortX --> pickX[Step 5: Select top k eigenvectors as Wk]
    pickX --> projX[Step 6: Project Z equals X tilde Wk]
    projX --> outX[Low Dimensional Embedding Z N x k]
```

## 4.3 Reinforcement Learning — Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[Environment at state st] -->|observation| agentA[Agent]
    agentA -->|action at| envA
    envA -->|reward rt+1| agentA
    agentA -->|policy update| policyA[Policy pi: S to A]
    policyA --> agentA
```

## 4.4 Q-Learning Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ([Start Episode]) --> obsQ[Observe current state s]
    obsQ --> chooseQ{epsilon greedy action selection}
    chooseQ -->|with prob 1 minus epsilon| greedyQ[Pick action a equals argmax Q s a]
    chooseQ -->|with prob epsilon| randomQ[Pick random action a]
    greedyQ --> execQ
    randomQ --> execQ[Execute action a in environment]
    execQ --> rewardQ[Observe reward r and next state s prime]
    rewardQ --> updateQ[Update Q s a using Q Learning rule]
    updateQ --> checkQ{Episode finished?}
    checkQ -->|No| obsQ
    checkQ -->|Yes| stopQ([End Episode])
```

## 4.5 Apriori Algorithm — Pruning Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startAp([Start Apriori]) --> gen1[Generate Frequent 1 Itemsets L1]
    gen1 --> gen2[Generate Candidate 2 Itemsets C2 from L1]
    gen2 --> scan2[Scan DB: count support]
    scan2 --> prune2{Prune itemsets below min support}
    prune2 -->|Yes| gen2
    prune2 -->|No| gen3[Generate Candidate 3 Itemsets C3 via join]
    gen3 --> scan3[Scan DB and count support]
    scan3 --> prune3{Below min support?}
    prune3 -->|Yes| gen3
    prune3 -->|No| rulesAp[Generate Association Rules from Lk]
    rulesAp --> stopAp([Output Rules])
```

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Paradigm | Feedback Signal | Goal | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervised | Labeled output $y$ for each $x$ | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ | Email spam classification |
| Unsupervised | No labels | Discover hidden structure / groupings | Customer segmentation using K-Means |
| Reinforcement | Scalar reward $r$ from environment | Maximize cumulative reward $G_t$ | Self-driving car learning to navigate |

All three are branches of ML differing in the **type of feedback** available to the learner. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "Exploration vs Exploitation" in Reinforcement Learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Exploration** = trying new actions to discover potentially better rewards, even if a known good action exists.
- **Exploitation** = using current knowledge (greedy policy) to maximize immediate reward.
- The trade-off is fundamental: too much exploration wastes time on bad actions; too much exploitation risks missing the optimal policy.
- A common solution is the **$\epsilon$-greedy** strategy: explore with probability $\epsilon$, exploit with probability $1 - \epsilon$, with $\epsilon$ decayed over training. (3 marks)

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm**. Derive the centroid update rule mathematically. State one limitation of K-Means. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Apriori algorithm** for association rule mining. Define Support, Confidence, and Lift. Given the following transaction database, find all frequent itemsets with **min_support = 0.5**: **(7 marks)**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| 1 | A, B, C |
| 2 | A, B |
| 3 | B, C |
| 4 | A, C |
| 5 | A, B, C |

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(a)

**Algorithm (4 marks):**

K-Means partitions $N$ data points into $K$ clusters by minimizing the WCSS objective:
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

**Steps:**
1. Initialize $K$ centroids $\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment:** $C_k = \{x_i : \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2, \forall j\}$.
3. **Update:** $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.
4. Repeat 2–3 until centroids stabilize.

**Centroid Derivation (2 marks):**

To minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\mu_k$ for fixed $C_k$:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

**Limitation (1 mark):** Sensitive to initialization; assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters; cannot handle non-convex shapes; requires pre-specifying $K$.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(b)

**Apriori Explanation (2 marks):**
Apriori is an iterative algorithm for mining frequent itemsets. It uses the **Apriori Property** — all subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent — to prune the candidate generation process. It works in $k$-passes over the database, generating $C_k$ candidates, counting support, and producing $L_k$ frequent itemsets.

**Metrics (2 marks):**
- $s(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{N}$
- $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$
- $\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)}$

**Worked Example (3 marks):**

Total transactions $N = 5$. Min support = 0.5 → min count = 2.5 → 3 transactions.

**L1 (Frequent 1-Itemsets):**
- A → 4 ✓
- B → 4 ✓
- C → 4 ✓

**C2 / L2:**
- {A,B} → 3 ✓
- {A,C} → 3 ✓
- {B,C} → 3 ✓

**C3 / L3:**
- {A,B,C} → 2 ✗ (below threshold of 3) — pruned.

**Final Frequent Itemsets:** $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{B,C\}$.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** framework with its components. Derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the value function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Q-Learning algorithm** with the update rule. A robot is in a 3-state environment. Initial $Q$-values are all 0. Using $\alpha = 0.5, \gamma = 0.9$, update the Q-values for the following single episode trajectory: $(s_1, a) \to r = 4, s' = s_2$; $(s_2, b) \to r = 6, s' = s_3$; $(s_3, a) \to r = 10, s' = \text{terminal}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(a)

**MDP Components (3 marks):**
An MDP is the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:
- $\mathcal{S}$: set of all states.
- $\mathcal{A}$: set of all actions.
- $P(s' \vert s, a)$: transition probability.
- $R(s, a)$: reward function.
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$: discount factor.

**Markov Property:** The future depends only on the current state, not the history.

**Bellman Derivation (4 marks):**

The value function is defined as the expected discounted return:
$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \vert s_t = s \right]$$

Expanding the first reward:
$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [r_{t+1} + \gamma V^\pi(s_{t+1}) \vert s_t = s]$$

Taking the expectation over actions and transitions:
$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) [R(s,a) + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$$

The **Bellman Optimality Equation** is obtained by choosing the maximizing action:
$$V^*(s) = \max_a \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^*(s') \right]$$

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(b)

**Q-Learning Explanation (2 marks):**
Q-Learning is a **model-free, off-policy** RL algorithm that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ directly from experience. The update rule is:
$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a) \right]$$

**Worked Numerical Update (5 marks):**

All Q-values start at 0.

**Update 1:** $(s_1, a) \to r=4, s'=s_2$
$$Q(s_1, a) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5[4 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_2, \cdot)) - 0] = 0.5 \cdot 4 = 2.0$$

**Update 2:** $(s_2, b) \to r=6, s'=s_3$
$$Q(s_2, b) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5[6 + 0.9 \cdot \max(0, 0) - 0] = 3.0$$

**Update 3:** $(s_3, a) \to r=10, s'=\text{terminal}$
$$Q(s_3, a) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5[10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0] = 5.0$$

**Final Q-Table:**

| State | Action a | Action b |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 2.0 | 0.0 |
| $s_2$ | 0.0 | 3.0 |
| $s_3$ | 5.0 | 0.0 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Reinforcement Learning with Supervised Learning:** RL has *delayed*, *scalar* rewards, not per-instance labels. Marks lost if you treat RL as just another classifier.
> 2. **Forgetting the max operator in Q-Learning:** The off-policy target uses $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$, *not* the action actually taken. This is the key distinction from SARSA.
> 3. **Missing the $\gamma$ in Bellman equation:** Many students write $V^*(s) = \max R(s,a)$ without the discounted future value term.
> 4. **K-Means centroid formula:** Must show the mean derivation, not just state the formula.
> 5. **Apriori pruning:** Failing to show the *Apriori Property* ("all subsets of a frequent itemset are frequent") loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Units / Convergence:** Q-Learning requires all $(s,a)$ pairs to be visited infinitely often for convergence — forgetting this loses credit on theory questions.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets)**

### Unsupervised Learning
- 🔹 USL has **no labels** — goal is to discover intrinsic data structure.
- 🔹 **K-Means** minimizes WCSS using alternating Assignment + Update steps.
- 🔹 Centroid formula: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.
- 🔹 K-Means assumes **spherical, equal-sized** clusters; use **K-Means++** for better seeding.
- 🔹 **PCA** projects data onto eigenvectors of the covariance matrix (directions of max variance).
- 🔹 Reconstruction error = sum of discarded eigenvalues $\sum_{j=k+1}^{D} \lambda_j$.
- 🔹 **Apriori** mines frequent itemsets using the *Apriori Property* for pruning.
- 🔹 **Support** = frequency; **Confidence** = conditional probability; **Lift** > 1 = positive correlation.
- 🔹 **DBSCAN** is a density-based alternative; handles arbitrary cluster shapes; marks noise points.

### Reinforcement Learning
- 🔹 RL = agent interacts with environment, learns policy to maximize cumulative reward.
- 🔹 **MDP** tuple: $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.
- 🔹 **Markov Property:** future is independent of past given present state.
- 🔹 **Bellman Optimality:** $V^*(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^*(s')]$.
- 🔹 **Q-Learning** is model-free and off-policy; uses $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ in target.
- 🔹 **TD error** $\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)$ drives learning.
- 🔹 **Exploration vs Exploitation** balanced via $\epsilon$-greedy with decay.
- 🔹 Convergence requires infinite visits to all $(s,a)$ pairs and Robbins-Monro learning rate.
- 🔹 RL applications: robotics, game AI (AlphaGo), autonomous driving, dynamic pricing.

### Cross-Paradigm Comparison (Memorize)
- 🔹 **Supervised:** labeled data → prediction.
- 🔹 **Unsupervised:** unlabeled data → structure discovery.
- 🔹 **Reinforcement:** reward signals → policy learning via interaction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Preprocessing - Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Preprocessing - Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Preprocessing** is the foundational phase in any Machine Learning (ML) pipeline wherein raw, real-world data is transformed into a clean, consistent, and machine-ingestible format. **Data Cleaning** (a sub-stage of preprocessing) is the systematic process of detecting, correcting, or removing corrupt, inaccurate, incomplete, or irrelevant records from a dataset to improve its overall quality.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEITT721 – Machine Learning**, data cleaning is positioned as the **first critical step** before any exploratory data analysis (EDA), feature engineering, or model training can occur. The quality of input data is governed by the well-known industry maxim:

> *"Better data beats fancier algorithms."* — **Anonymous ML Practitioner**

Mathematically, the cleaning operation can be abstracted as a transformation function:

$$T_{\text{clean}} : \mathcal{D}_{\text{raw}} \longrightarrow \mathcal{D}_{\text{clean}}$$

where $\mathcal{D}_{\text{raw}}$ denotes the noisy/incomplete dataset and $\mathcal{D}_{\text{clean}}$ denotes the refined, model-ready dataset.

### 1.2 The "Why" — Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Preparation Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a gourmet dish. Before you can cook, you must:
> 1. **Wash** the vegetables (remove dirt/noise)
> 2. **Peel** the spoiled outer layers (handle outliers)
> 3. **Discard** rotten produce (drop duplicates/errors)
> 4. **Chop** uniformly (normalize formatting)
> 5. **Measure** precise quantities (handle missing values)
>
> If you skip these steps, no matter how expensive your oven (algorithm) is, the dish will fail. **Data Cleaning is exactly this kitchen preparation for ML.**

### 1.3 The Seven Wastes of Data — Core Problems Addressed

| # | Problem | Description | KTU Board Tag |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Missing Values** | Empty cells (`NaN`, `NULL`, `?`) | High-frequency |
| 2 | **Noisy Data** | Random error/variance in measurements | High-frequency |
| 3 | **Outliers** | Data points deviating drastically | High-frequency |
| 4 | **Inconsistencies** | Same entity represented differently (`"Kerala"` vs `"kerela"`) | Moderate |
| 5 | **Duplicates** | Redundant rows/records | Moderate |
| 6 | **Type Errors** | Numeric stored as string | Low |
| 7 | **Irrelevant Features** | Columns that add no predictive power | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule (KTU Board Emphasis):** A dataset with **80% clean data** will outperform a sophisticated algorithm trained on **20% clean data**. Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO) is the governing principle.

### 1.4 The CRISP-DM and KTU Context

Data cleaning is the **first iterative step** in the **CRoss-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)** cycle, which KTU 2024 endorses as the standard reference model. The cycle is non-linear — cleaning insights often force re-collection or re-definition of the problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Stage Cleaning Pipeline (KTU 2024 Mandate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies the following sub-stages. Memorize them in this exact order for the Part A questions.

**Step 1 — Audit / Data Profiling:**
