Identify the *type*, *location*, and *severity* of impurities using summary statistics ($\mu$, $\sigma$, min, max, cardinality).

**Step 2 — Handling Missing Values:**
Choose between *deletion* (listwise/pairwise) or *imputation* (statistical or model-based).

**Step 3 — Noise Smoothing:**
Apply binning, regression, or clustering-based smoothing to reduce random variance.

**Step 4 — Outlier Detection & Treatment:**
Use statistical (Z-score, IQR) or distance-based (k-NN, LOF) methods to flag anomalies.

**Step 5 — Consistency Normalization:**
Standardize units, formats, casing, and taxonomies.

**Step 6 — Validation:**
Re-profile to confirm the **data quality metrics** (accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness) have improved.

### 2.2 Detailed Sub-Theory

#### 2.2.1 Missing Value Mechanisms (Rubin's Taxonomy)

The KTU syllabus expects students to distinguish between:

- **MCAR (Missing Completely At Random):** Probability of missingness is independent of all data.
$$\Pr(M = 1 \mid X, Y) = \Pr(M = 1)$$
- **MAR (Missing At Random):** Missingness depends only on observed data.
$$\Pr(M = 1 \mid X, Y) = \Pr(M = 1 \mid X_{\text{observed}})$$
- **MNAR (Missing Not At Random):** Missingness depends on the unobserved value itself (most dangerous case).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** If the question states "missingness is related to the missing value itself," the answer is **MNAR**. This is a high-yield KTU Part A question.

#### 2.2.2 Imputation Strategies

- **Mean Imputation (for symmetric, no-outlier numeric data):**
$$\hat{x}_i = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
- **Median Imputation (for skewed numeric data):**
$$\hat{x}_i = \tilde{x} = \text{median}(X)$$
- **Mode Imputation (for categorical data):**
$$\hat{x}_i = \arg\max_{c_j} \text{freq}(c_j)$$

#### 2.2.3 Outlier Detection Mathematics

- **Z-Score Method (parametric, assumes Gaussian):**
$$Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$
A point is flagged if $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ (the empirical **68-95-99.7 rule**).
- **IQR Method (non-parametric, robust):**
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$$
Outlier boundaries:
$$[x_{\text{low}}, x_{\text{high}}] = [Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}, \quad Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}]$$

#### 2.2.4 Noise Reduction via Binning

- **Smoothing by Bin Means:** Replace each value in a bin by the bin's mean.
- **Smoothing by Bin Medians:** Replace each value by the bin median.
- **Smoothing by Bin Boundaries:** Replace each value by the closest boundary (min or max of bin).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam-ready summary**. Bookmark this section.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use-Case | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Imputation | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | MCAR numeric, symmetric | Same unit as $x$ |
| Median Imputation | $\tilde{x} = Q_2$ | Skewed numeric | Robust to outliers |
| Z-Score | $Z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ | Outlier (Gaussian) | Threshold $\vert Z \vert > 3$ |
| IQR | $Q_3 - Q_1$ | Outlier (non-Gaussian) | Threshold $1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ |
| Z-Score Standardization | $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ | Feature scaling | Output: mean $0$, var $1$ |
| Min-Max Normalization | $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ | Bounded scaling | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| Data Quality Ratio | $\text{DQR} = \frac{n_{\text{clean}}}{n_{\text{total}}}$ | Validation | Dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ |
| Missing Ratio | $R_{\text{miss}} = \frac{n_{\text{missing}}}{n_{\text{total}}}$ | Audit | Dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ |
| Smoothing by Bin Mean | $\hat{x}_{\text{bin}} = \frac{1}{\vert B \vert}\sum_{x_i \in B} x_i$ | Noise reduction | Applied per bin $B$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In production ML systems (e.g., **Kerala State IT mission analytics**, **KISAN call-center NLP**, or **flood-prediction models** for the Western Ghats), data cleaning is automated through tools like **Apache Spark**, **Pandas**, **Great Expectations**, and **AWS Deequ**. The preprocessing stage typically consumes **60–80%** of a data scientist's time — a statistic frequently cited in KTU viva voce panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Z-Score Outlier Detection

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Given a univariate dataset $X = \{12, 13, 12, 14, 11, 13, 12, 45, 12, 13\}$, identify the outliers using the Z-score method (threshold $\vert Z \vert > 3$).

**Step 1 — Compute the arithmetic mean $\mu$:**
$$\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

Substituting the values ($n = 10$):
$$\mu = \frac{12 + 13 + 12 + 14 + 11 + 13 + 12 + 45 + 12 + 13}{10}$$

$$\mu = \frac{157}{10} = 15.7$$

**Step 2 — Compute the population standard deviation $\sigma$:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}$$

We compute each squared deviation:
- $(12 - 15.7)^2 = (-3.7)^2 = 13.69$
- $(13 - 15.7)^2 = (-2.7)^2 = 7.29$
- $(12 - 15.7)^2 = 13.69$
- $(14 - 15.7)^2 = (-1.7)^2 = 2.89$
- $(11 - 15.7)^2 = (-4.7)^2 = 22.09$
- $(13 - 15.7)^2 = 7.29$
- $(12 - 15.7)^2 = 13.69$
- $(45 - 15.7)^2 = (29.3)^2 = 858.49$
- $(12 - 15.7)^2 = 13.69$
- $(13 - 15.7)^2 = 7.29$

Sum of squared deviations:
$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 13.69 + 7.29 + 13.69 + 2.89 + 22.09 + 7.29 + 13.69 + 858.49 + 13.69 + 7.29 = 960.10$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{960.10}{10}} = \sqrt{96.01} \approx 9.799$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z-score for the suspicious value $x_8 = 45$:**
$$Z_8 = \frac{x_8 - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{45 - 15.7}{9.799}$$

$$Z_8 = \frac{29.3}{9.799} \approx 2.99$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Since $\vert Z_8 \vert = 2.99 < 3$, technically not flagged under strict $3\sigma$ rule.

**Step 5 — Engineering Judgment:**
However, the value is **$\approx 3\sigma$ away** and clearly an anomaly. In practice, KTU examiners will accept $x_8 = 45$ as an outlier because it is the lone extreme point disrupting the tight cluster $\{11, 12, 13, 14\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Insight:** Some texts use $\vert Z \vert > 2$ or $2.5$. Always **state the threshold** explicitly in the exam for full marks.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: IQR-Based Outlier Detection

**Problem Statement:**
For $X = \{7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 100\}$, find the IQR-based outlier boundaries.

**Step 1 — Sort the data (already sorted for $n=10$):**
$$X_{\text{sorted}} = \{7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 100\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Q_1$ (25th percentile):**
For $n=10$, $Q_1$ is the median of the lower half $\{7, 8, 9, 10, 11\}$:
$$Q_1 = 9$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Q_3$ (75th percentile):**
For the upper half $\{12, 13, 14, 15, 100\}$:
$$Q_3 = 14$$

**Step 4 — Compute IQR:**
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 14 - 9 = 5$$

**Step 5 — Compute boundary fences:**
$$x_{\text{low}} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 9 - 1.5(5) = 9 - 7.5 = 1.5$$

$$x_{\text{high}} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 14 + 1.5(5) = 14 + 7.5 = 21.5$$

**Step 6 — Apply rule:**
A point $x_i$ is an outlier if $x_i < 1.5$ or $x_i > 21.5$.

Since $100 > 21.5$, the value $x = 100$ is flagged as an outlier. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IQR > Z-Score for small datasets?** IQR is non-parametric and resistant to the outlier's *own* influence on $\mu$ and $\sigma$. For skewed or small-$n$ data, always prefer IQR in the exam answer.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Min-Max Normalization Worked Example

**Problem Statement:**
Normalize the values $x = 20$ within the feature $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$ into range $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the extremes:**
$$x_{\min} = 10, \quad x_{\max} = 50$$

**Step 2 — Apply Min-Max formula:**
$$x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}} = \frac{20 - 10}{50 - 10}$$

$$x' = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
The value $20$ lies at the **25th percentile** of the range. Perfect mid-low value.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** Examiners often present scenarios and ask which method to apply. Use this matrix.

| Scenario | Best Method | Why | Marks Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Numeric, symmetric, no outliers | Mean Imputation | Preserves variance | "$E[X]$ preserved" |
| Numeric, skewed | Median Imputation | Robust to skew | "Median = order statistic" |
| Categorical, low cardinality | Mode Imputation | Most frequent class | "MAPE estimator" |
| Numeric, outliers present, $n > 30$ | Z-Score | Parametric, fast | "$3\sigma$ rule" |
| Numeric, outliers present, $n < 30$ or skewed | IQR | Non-parametric | "$Q_3 - Q_1$" |
| Time-series, smooth trend | Bin Smoothing | Local averaging | "Neighborhood mean" |
| Mixed data, complex | KNN / MICE Imputation | Multivariate | "$k=5$ neighbors" |
| Missing $> 40\%$ in a column | Drop Column | Imputation unreliable | "Information loss" |
| Missing $< 5\%$ in a column | Listwise Deletion | Minimal bias | "MAR assumption" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Data Cleaning Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D_raw] --> B[Step 1: Data Profiling and Audit]
    B --> C{Missing Values Detected?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Step 2a: Imputation or Deletion]
    C -- No --> E[Step 3: Outlier Detection]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Outliers Present?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 3a: Cap, Remove, or Transform]
    F -- No --> H[Step 4: Noise Smoothing via Binning]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 5: Consistency and Format Normalization]
    I --> J[Step 6: Duplicate Removal]
    J --> K[Step 7: Validation via DQR Metric]
    K --> L{DQR greater than 0.95?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Clean Dataset D_clean - Ready for EDA]
    L -- No --> B
    M --> N[Module 2: Feature Engineering]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Figure 4.1** — Iterative Data Cleaning Workflow as prescribed by KTU 2024 Module 1.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Missing Value Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Input Column X with NaN] --> Q{Data Type Check}
    Q -- Numeric --> R{Distribution Symmetric?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Mean Imputation]
    R -- No --> T[Median Imputation]
    Q -- Categorical --> U[Mode Imputation]
    Q -- Mixed --> V[Apply MICE or KNN Imputer]
    S --> W[Imputed Column X_hat]
    T --> W
    U --> W
    V --> W
    W --> X[Re-integrate into DataFrame]

    style P fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style W fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style X fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Figure 4.2** — Decision tree for missing value imputation strategies (high-yield KTU diagram).

### 4.3 Outlier Detection Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Dataset X] --> Z1[Compute mu and sigma]
    Z1 --> Z2[Z-Score = x - mu / sigma]
    Z2 --> Z3{abs Z greater than 3?}
    Z3 -- Yes --> Z4[Flag as Outlier]
    Z3 -- No --> Z5[Keep]

    I[Dataset X] --> I1[Sort Ascending]
    I1 --> I2[Compute Q1 and Q3]
    I2 --> I3[Compute IQR = Q3 - Q1]
    I3 --> I4[Boundaries Q1 - 1.5 IQR, Q3 + 1.5 IQR]
    I4 --> I5{x less than low OR x greater than high?}
    I5 -- Yes --> I6[Flag as Outlier]
    I5 -- No --> I7[Keep]

    style Z4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Z5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Figure 4.3** — Side-by-side comparison of Z-Score vs. IQR outlier detection methods.

### 4.4 Data Quality Dimensions Radar (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Quality Dimensions per KTU 2024] --> A1[Accuracy]
    A --> A2[Completeness]
    A --> A3[Consistency]
    A --> A4[Timeliness]
    A --> A5[Validity]
    A --> A6[Uniqueness]

    A1 --> R[Validate against ground truth]
    A2 --> R
    A3 --> R
    A4 --> R
    A5 --> R
    A6 --> R
    R --> S[Generate DQR Score]

    style A fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Figure 4.4** — Six-dimension data quality framework converging into a single DQR metric.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define data preprocessing. List any four tasks involved in data cleaning with a one-line example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data preprocessing is the process of transforming raw, real-world data into a clean, consistent, and machine-readable format suitable for ML model training. It is a prerequisite for all subsequent stages in the CRISP-DM pipeline.

**Four Tasks (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Missing Value Handling:** E.g., filling `NaN` age values with the column mean.
2. **Noise Removal:** E.g., applying binning to smooth sensor temperature readings.
3. **Outlier Treatment:** E.g., removing a salary entry of ₹10,00,00,000 from a junior-engineer dataset.
4. **Consistency Normalization:** E.g., converting `"Kerala"`, `"kerela"`, `"KERALA"` all to a single canonical form.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between MCAR, MAR, and MNAR missing data mechanisms with a real-world example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Definition (1 mark) | Real-World Example (each 0.67 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **MCAR** | Missingness is independent of any data | A survey response lost due to a random server crash. |
| **MAR** | Missingness depends only on observed variables | Men are less likely to fill a depression survey (gender is observed). |
| **MNAR** | Missingness depends on the unseen value itself | High-income individuals skip the "income" field in a tax form. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set 1]*

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **six stages of the data cleaning pipeline** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. For each stage, mention one technique and one metric. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The six stages of the KTU 2024 data cleaning pipeline are:

1. **Data Profiling (1 mark):** Computes summary statistics such as $\mu$, $\sigma$, min, max, cardinality, and $R_{\text{miss}}$. **Technique:** Pandas `.describe()`. **Metric:** Missing ratio $R_{\text{miss}} = n_{\text{missing}} / n_{\text{total}}$.

2. **Missing Value Handling (1.5 marks):** Two strategies — **deletion** (listwise, pairwise) and **imputation** (mean, median, mode, KNN, MICE). **Technique:** Median imputation for skewed data. **Metric:** Post-imputation variance.

3. **Outlier Detection & Treatment (1.5 marks):** Use **Z-score** ($\vert Z \vert > 3$) for Gaussian data or **IQR** ($1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ rule) for skewed data. **Technique:** IQR fences. **Metric:** Outlier proportion.

4. **Noise Smoothing (1 mark):** Apply **binning** (mean, median, boundary), **regression**, or **clustering**. **Technique:** Smoothing by bin means. **Metric:** Reduction in standard deviation.

5. **Consistency Normalization (1 mark):** Standardize units, casing, taxonomy. **Technique:** String lowercasing + regex. **Metric:** Cardinality drop.

6. **Validation (1 mark):** Re-profile and compute the **Data Quality Ratio (DQR)**:
$$\text{DQR} = \frac{n_{\text{clean}}}{n_{\text{total}}} \in [0, 1]$$
Acceptable threshold: $\text{DQR} \geq 0.95$.

**Flowchart:** (Refer to Section 4, Figure 4.1 for the iterative loop diagram — 0 marks deducted if verbal flowchart is described in text form).

---

**(b)** Consider the following dataset of monthly rainfall (in mm) recorded at 5 stations in Idukki:
$$X = \{220, 230, 215, 240, 225, 232, 218, 850, 228, 222\}$$
The value **850 mm** is suspected to be a sensor error. **(i)** Detect this outlier using the **Z-score method** (threshold = 2.5). **(ii)** Replace it with the **median** of the remaining values. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Z-Score Detection (4 marks):**

Step 1: Compute $\mu$ over all 10 values:
$$\mu = \frac{220 + 230 + 215 + 240 + 225 + 232 + 218 + 850 + 228 + 222}{10} = \frac{2880}{10} = 288.0 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Compute $\sigma$ (population):
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}\sum (x_i - 288)^2}$$

Squared deviations:
- $(220-288)^2 = 4624$
- $(230-288)^2 = 3364$
- $(215-288)^2 = 5329$
- $(240-288)^2 = 2304$
- $(225-288)^2 = 3969$
- $(232-288)^2 = 3136$
- $(218-288)^2 = 4900$
- $(850-288)^2 = 315\,844$
- $(228-288)^2 = 3600$
- $(222-288)^2 = 4356$

Sum = $351\,426$. Therefore:
$$\sigma = \sqrt{35142.6} \approx 187.46 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Z-score for $x_8 = 850$:
$$Z_8 = \frac{850 - 288}{187.46} = \frac{562}{187.46} \approx 2.998 \approx 3.0$$

**Decision (1 mark):** Since $Z_8 \approx 3.0 > 2.5$, the value **850 mm is flagged as an outlier**. ✅

**Part (ii) — Median Replacement (3 marks):**

After removing $850$, sort the 9 values:
$$X' = \{215, 218, 220, 222, 225, 228, 230, 232, 240\}$$

The median (5th element) is:
$$\tilde{x} = 225 \text{ mm}$$

Replace $850 \to 225$. The cleaned dataset becomes:
$$X_{\text{clean}} = \{220, 230, 215, 240, 225, 232, 218, 225, 228, 222\}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set 2]*

**(a)** Explain the **Z-score** and **IQR methods** for outlier detection. State the threshold for each, and list **two advantages** and **one limitation** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Z-Score Method (3 marks):**
$$Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$
- **Threshold:** $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ (strict), or $\vert Z_i \vert > 2.5$ (lenient).
- **Advantages:** (i) Fast and simple for large datasets, (ii) Works on standardized scale.
- **Limitation:** Assumes Gaussian distribution; sensitive to outliers themselves (since they inflate $\sigma$).

**IQR Method (3 marks):**
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1, \quad \text{Outlier if } x_i < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} \text{ or } x_i > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$
- **Threshold:** Multiplier of $1.5$ (or $3.0$ for "extreme" outliers — Tukey's fences).
- **Advantages:** (i) Non-parametric and distribution-free, (ii) Robust to extreme values.
- **Limitation:** Less precise for small datasets where quartiles themselves are unstable.

**Comparative Statement (1 mark):** For skewed or small datasets, **IQR is preferred**; for large Gaussian datasets, **Z-score is preferred**.

---

**(b)** A dataset column contains the values: $X = \{4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 100\}$. Apply the following preprocessing steps in order and show the result after each: **(i) IQR-based outlier detection** (identify and remove outliers), **(ii) Min-Max normalization** to range $[0, 1]$, **(iii) Z-score standardization**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — IQR Outlier Removal (3 marks):**

Sorted: $\{4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 100\}$, $n = 6$.

$Q_1$ = median of lower half $\{4, 5, 6\}$ = $5$.
$Q_3$ = median of upper half $\{7, 8, 100\}$ = $8$.

$$\text{IQR} = 8 - 5 = 3$$

Boundaries:
$$x_{\text{low}} = 5 - 1.5(3) = 0.5, \quad x_{\text{high}} = 8 + 1.5(3) = 12.5$$

Since $100 > 12.5$, the value $100$ is an **outlier**. Remove it.

Cleaned: $X_{\text{clean}} = \{4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$.

**Part (ii) — Min-Max Normalization (2 marks):**

$x_{\min} = 4$, $x_{\max} = 8$, range = $4$.

Apply $x' = (x - 4) / (8 - 4)$:
- $4 \to 0.00$
- $5 \to 0.25$
- $6 \to 0.50$
- $7 \to 0.75$
- $8 \to 1.00$

$X_{\text{norm}} = \{0.00, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00\}$.

**Part (iii) — Z-Score Standardization (2 marks):**

Mean of $\{4,5,6,7,8\}$ = $6$.
Variance = $\frac{(4-6)^2 + (5-6)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (7-6)^2 + (8-6)^2}{5} = \frac{4+1+0+1+4}{5} = 2.0$.
$\sigma = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

Apply $z = (x - 6)/\sqrt{2}$:
- $4 \to -1.414$
- $5 \to -0.707$
- $6 \to 0.000$
- $7 \to +0.707$
- $8 \to +1.414$

$X_{\text{std}} = \{-1.414, -0.707, 0.000, 0.707, 1.414\}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the threshold:** Always write the rule explicitly (e.g., "$\vert Z \vert > 3$" or "$1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ rule"). A bare answer loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing population and sample $\sigma$:** Use $n$ (population) unless the question explicitly says "sample." Examiner checks this.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why":** In 14-mark answers, just computing is not enough. Explain *why* you chose mean vs. median imputation — earns the **application mark**.
> 4. **Not showing intermediate steps:** Always show $\mu$, $\sigma$, then $Z$ separately. Grouping loses step-marks.
> 5. **Confusing normalization with standardization:** Normalization → range $[0,1]$; Standardization → mean $0$, variance $1$. Mixing them up costs full marks on sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this for the night before the exam):**

- **Data Cleaning is the FIRST step** in the ML pipeline (before EDA, feature engineering, modeling).
- **Six Stages:** Profile → Missing → Outlier → Noise → Consistency → Validate.
- **MCAR vs. MAR vs. MNAR:** MNAR is the only one where missingness depends on the *unseen* value.
- **Mean Imputation:** Use only for **symmetric, no-outlier** numeric data.
- **Median Imputation:** Use for **skewed numeric** data; it is the 2nd quartile $Q_2$.
- **Mode Imputation:** Use for **categorical** data.
- **Z-Score:** $Z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$, threshold $\vert Z \vert > 3$ (Gaussian assumption).
- **IQR:** $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$, fences at $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$.
- **IQR > Z-Score** for: small $n$, skewed, non-Gaussian data.
- **Binning types:** by means, by medians, by boundaries — all reduce intra-bin variance.
- **Min-Max Normalization** → range $[0, 1]$; formula: $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$.
- **Z-Score Standardization** → mean $0$, std $1$; formula: $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$.
- **Drop a column** when missingness exceeds **40%** (rule of thumb cited in KTU viva).
- **Data Quality Ratio (DQR):** $\text{DQR} = n_{\text{clean}}/n_{\text{total}}$; target $\geq 0.95$.
- **DQR Formula:** Acceptable, Valid, Complete, Consistent, Unique, Timely — six dimensions.
- **CRISP-DM cycle is iterative** — cleaning may loop back to data collection.
- **GIGO Principle:** Garbage-In-Garbage-Out — quality data > fancy algorithms.
- **KTU weightage tip:** Data Preprocessing carries **~15–20% marks** in the ESE Module 1.
- **Always state the assumption** (Gaussian, MCAR, etc.) when applying a method — examiners reward it.
- **Kerala-context examples** (Idukki rainfall, KISAN, flood models) score extra in descriptive answers.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Data Preprocessing: Cleaning Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L4 levels covered)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normalization and Splitting Data - Training Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Normalization and Splitting Data - Training Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Normalization — Formal Definition

**Normalization** (also called *Feature Scaling*) is a data preprocessing step in Machine Learning that transforms the numeric values of features (columns) of a dataset to a common scale, without distorting differences in the ranges of values or losing information. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the two most prominently tested forms are:

- **Min–Max Normalization (Scaling):** rescales features to a fixed range, typically **[0, 1]**.
- **Z-Score Normalization (Standardization):** rescales features so that they have a **mean of 0** and a **standard deviation of 1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Normalization is the process of scaling individual samples (or features) to unit norm, or rescaling feature ranges to a standard scale, performed **after** data cleaning and **before** model training to ensure that no single feature dominates the objective function purely because of its magnitude.

### 1.2 Splitting Data — Formal Definition

**Data Splitting** is the procedure of partitioning a labelled dataset into disjoint subsets used for distinct phases of the learning pipeline. The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus mandates the three-way split:

- **Training Set** — used to fit (train) the model parameters.
- **Validation Set** — used to tune hyperparameters and perform model selection.
- **Test Set** — used to estimate the final, unbiased generalization performance of the chosen model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Training Set Definition (High-Yield for KTU):** A *training set* is the subset of the labelled dataset presented to a learning algorithm during the model-fitting phase. The model iteratively adjusts its internal parameters (weights, biases, tree splits, etc.) by minimizing a loss function evaluated **only on the training data**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Cooking Analogy":**
> Imagine you are preparing a recipe (your ML model). You have ingredients measured in *cups*, *grams*, *litres*, and *teaspoons* (your raw features). If you dump a litre of salt next to a teaspoon of pepper, the salt will dominate the dish *only because of its raw quantity*, not its importance. **Normalization** is the step where you convert all ingredients to a *standard measure* (e.g., grams), so the chef (the model) can judge the real importance of each ingredient.
>
> **Splitting data** is like keeping a *practice set of past exam papers* (training set), a *mock test* (validation set) to fine-tune your strategy, and the *actual final exam* (test set) you will never see during preparation. If you study using the final exam, you cannot honestly judge your preparation.

### 1.4 Why Normalization & Splitting Are Mandatory (KTU Exam Favourite)

- Algorithms relying on **distance metrics** (K-Nearest Neighbors, K-Means, SVM with RBF kernel, PCA, Gradient Descent-based models like Linear/Logistic Regression, Neural Networks) are **highly sensitive to feature scales**.
- Algorithms based on **tree splits** (Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost) are **scale-invariant** but normalization may still improve convergence of any wrapped gradient optimization.
- A model evaluated on the same data it was trained on produces **over-optimistic (biased) accuracy** — splitting provides an honest, out-of-sample evaluation.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Min–Max Normalization

1. **Identify** the minimum value $X_{\min}$ and maximum value $X_{\max}$ of the feature column.
2. **Subtract** $X_{\min}$ from every value $X_i$ to shift the minimum to zero.
3. **Divide** by the range $(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ to compress all values into the interval $[0, 1]$.
4. **Verify** that the new minimum equals 0 and the new maximum equals 1.

> **Why it works:** Subtraction handles *location* (shifts the distribution), and division handles *scale* (stretches/compresses the spread). The transformation is **linear**, so the shape of the original distribution is preserved.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Z-Score Normalization (Standardization)

1. **Compute** the population/sample mean $\mu$ of the feature.
2. **Compute** the population/sample standard deviation $\sigma$.
3. **Subtract** $\mu$ from each value (centering).
4. **Divide** by $\sigma$ (scaling).
5. The resulting feature has $\mu_{new} = 0$ and $\sigma_{new} = 1$.

> **Why it works:** Standardization converts the feature into a dimensionless **z-statistic** measured in *standard-deviation units*. It is preferred when the data contains outliers (min–max is sensitive to extremes) or when the algorithm *assumes* zero-centred data (e.g., PCA, Logistic Regression with regularization).

### 2.3 Operational Logic of Train–Validation–Test Splitting

1. **Shuffle** the dataset randomly (to break any ordering bias, e.g., time-series leakage).
2. **Reserve** a fixed fraction (typically **70 %** for training, **15 %** for validation, **15 %** for testing — the KTU-recommended default).
3. **Train** the model exclusively on the training subset.
4. **Validate** hyperparameters using the validation subset.
5. **Test** the final locked model **once** on the test subset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Validation Alternative:** For small datasets, **k-fold cross-validation** (commonly $k = 5$ or $k = 10$) is used in place of a single split. The training set is partitioned into $k$ equal folds; in each iteration, $k-1$ folds train the model and 1 fold validates it. The average validation score across all $k$ iterations is the final performance estimate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range | Typical Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Min–Max Scaling** | $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $0 \le X' \le 1$ | Neural networks, image pixels, bounded features |
| 2 | **Min–Max with custom range $[a,b]$** | $X' = a + \dfrac{(X - X_{\min})(b - a)}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $a \le X' \le b$ | Rescaling to $[-1, 1]$ for tanh activations |
| 3 | **Z-Score (Standardization)** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\mu_Z = 0,\ \sigma_Z = 1$ | PCA, SVM, regularized regression, outlier-heavy data |
| 4 | **Mean** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i$ | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ | Required for standardization |
| 5 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(X_i - \mu)^2}$ | $\sigma \ge 0$ | Required for standardization |
| 6 | **Training Set Size** | $N_{\text{train}} = p \cdot N$ where $p \in \{0.6, 0.7, 0.8\}$ | $N_{\text{train}} \lt N$ | Common split fractions |
| 7 | **Test Set Size** | $N_{\text{test}} = (1 - p - q) \cdot N$ | $N_{\text{test}} \ge 0$ | $q$ = validation fraction |
| 8 | **k-Fold CV Score** | $CV_k = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Score}_i$ | Unit: same as metric | Robust performance estimate |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** Min–Max scaling is the *de-facto* standard for image pixel inputs to Convolutional Neural Networks (range $[0, 255] \to [0, 1]$), while Z-score standardization is mandatory in sensor-fusion pipelines (IoT, autonomous vehicles) where features have heterogeneous physical units (m/s, °C, Pa, lux).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Min–Max Normalization (Exhaustive)

**Problem:** A dataset has a single feature column with the following 5 values: $\{2,\ 5,\ 8,\ 10,\ 12\}$. Apply Min–Max normalization to scale the values into the range $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Identify extremes.**

$$X_{\min} = \min\{2, 5, 8, 10, 12\} = 2$$

$$X_{\max} = \max\{2, 5, 8, 10, 12\} = 12$$

**Step 2 — Compute the range.**

$$\text{Range} = X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 12 - 2 = 10$$

**Step 3 — Apply the normalization formula $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ to each value.**

For $X = 2$:

$$X'_1 = \frac{2 - 2}{10} = \frac{0}{10} = 0.00$$

For $X = 5$:

$$X'_2 = \frac{5 - 2}{10} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

For $X = 8$:

$$X'_3 = \frac{8 - 2}{10} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.60$$

For $X = 10$:

$$X'_4 = \frac{10 - 2}{10} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.80$$

For $X = 12$:

$$X'_5 = \frac{12 - 2}{10} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.00$$

**Step 4 — Verification (KTU marker expectation).**

$$\min(X') = 0.00 \quad \text{and} \quad \max(X') = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

The normalized vector is $\{0.00,\ 0.30,\ 0.60,\ 0.80,\ 1.00\}$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Z-Score Standardization (Exhaustive)

**Problem:** Using the same dataset $\{2, 5, 8, 10, 12\}$ and $N = 5$, compute the Z-scores.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean $\mu$.**

$$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i = \frac{1}{5}(2 + 5 + 8 + 10 + 12) = \frac{37}{5} = 7.4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared deviations $(X_i - \mu)^2$.**

| $X_i$ | $X_i - \mu$ | $(X_i - \mu)^2$ |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| 2     | $-5.4$      | $29.16$         |
| 5     | $-2.4$      | $5.76$          |
| 8     | $0.6$       | $0.36$          |
| 10    | $2.6$       | $6.76$          |
| 12    | $4.6$       | $21.16$         |

**Step 3 — Sum the squared deviations.**

$$\sum (X_i - \mu)^2 = 29.16 + 5.76 + 0.36 + 6.76 + 21.16 = 63.20$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance and standard deviation.**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum (X_i - \mu)^2 = \frac{63.20}{5} = 12.64$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{12.64} \approx 3.5553$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Z-score formula $Z_i = \dfrac{X_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ to each value.**

For $X_1 = 2$:

$$Z_1 = \frac{2 - 7.4}{3.5553} = \frac{-5.4}{3.5553} \approx -1.519$$

For $X_2 = 5$:

$$Z_2 = \frac{5 - 7.4}{3.5553} = \frac{-2.4}{3.5553} \approx -0.675$$

For $X_3 = 8$:

$$Z_3 = \frac{8 - 7.4}{3.5553} = \frac{0.6}{3.5553} \approx 0.169$$

For $X_4 = 10$:

$$Z_4 = \frac{10 - 7.4}{3.5553} = \frac{2.6}{3.5553} \approx 0.731$$

For $X_5 = 12$:

$$Z_5 = \frac{12 - 7.4}{3.5553} = \frac{4.6}{3.5553} \approx 1.294$$

**Step 6 — Verification.**

$$\mu_Z = \frac{-1.519 - 0.675 + 0.169 + 0.731 + 1.294}{5} = \frac{0.000}{5} \approx 0 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Train–Validation–Test Split (Exhaustive)

**Problem:** A dataset has $N = 1000$ labelled samples. Using a 70 / 15 / 15 split, determine the number of samples in each subset.

**Step 1 — Compute the training set size.**

$$N_{\text{train}} = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the validation set size.**

$$N_{\text{val}} = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the test set size.**

$$N_{\text{test}} = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

$$N_{\text{train}} + N_{\text{val}} + N_{\text{test}} = 700 + 150 + 150 = 1000 = N \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check expected by KTU examiners:** The three fractions must sum to **1.0** exactly, and the three subset sizes must sum to the original $N$ with no remainder (or with a documented $\pm 1$ sample difference for indivisible splits).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Min–Max Normalization | Z-Score Standardization | No Scaling |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Output range** | Fixed $[0, 1]$ (or $[a, b]$) | Unbounded, $\mu = 0,\ \sigma = 1$ | Original scale |
| **Sensitivity to outliers** | **High** | **Low (robust)** | N/A |
| **Preserves original distribution shape** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Affected by new unseen data (train/test shift)** | Yes — recompute min/max | Yes — recompute $\mu, \sigma$ | No |
| **Best suited for** | Neural nets, image data, KNN | SVM, PCA, regularized models | Tree-based models |
| **KTU examiner expectation** | Formula + worked numeric example | Formula + worked numeric example | Justify *why* you skipped it |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Data Preprocessing & Training Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical flow of a supervised learning pipeline, with explicit emphasis on the *normalization* and *data-splitting* stages covered in KTU 2024 Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["RAW DATASET"] --> node2["STEP 1 DATA CLEANING"]
    node2 --> node3["STEP 2 HANDLE MISSING VALUES"]
    node3 --> node4["STEP 3 NORMALIZATION"]
    node4 --> node4a["MIN MAX SCALING OR Z SCORE STANDARDIZATION"]
    node4a --> node5["STEP 4 TRAIN VALIDATION TEST SPLIT"]
    node5 --> node6["TRAINING SET 70 PERCENT"]
    node5 --> node7["VALIDATION SET 15 PERCENT"]
    node5 --> node8["TEST SET 15 PERCENT"]
    node6 --> node9["MODEL TRAINING FIT PARAMETERS"]
    node7 --> node10["HYPERPARAMETER TUNING"]
    node8 --> node11["FINAL UNBIASED EVALUATION"]
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["REPORT METRICS ACCURACY F1 RMSE"]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Sub-Block — The Three-Way Split

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FULL DATASET N SAMPLES"] --> nodeB["SHUFFLE RANDOMLY"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ALLOCATE p PERCENT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["TRAINING SET N TRAIN"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["ALLOCATE q PERCENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["VALIDATION SET N VAL"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["ALLOCATE 1 MINUS p MINUS q PERCENT"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["TEST SET N TEST"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["USED TO LEARN WEIGHTS"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["USED TO TUNE HYPERPARAMETERS"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["USED ONCE FOR FINAL REPORT"]
```

### 4.3 Sub-Block — Normalization Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["INPUT FEATURE COLUMN"] --> nodeQ2{"PRESENCE OF OUTLIERS"}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeQ3["USE Z SCORE STANDARDIZATION"]
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeQ4{"REQUIRED OUTPUT BOUNDED RANGE"}
    nodeQ4 -- YES --> nodeQ5["USE MIN MAX SCALING TO 0 1"]
    nodeQ4 -- NO --> nodeQ6["USE Z SCORE STANDARDIZATION"]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ7["OUTPUT SCALED FEATURE"]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ7
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7
```

> **Reading guide for the diagrams:** Every rectangular block represents a process stage, every diamond block represents a decision gate, and every arrow represents a directional flow of *data* or *control*. The Training Set branch (nodeD → nodeI) is the **only** branch where the model parameters are updated via gradient descent; the Test Set branch (nodeH → nodeK) is executed **exactly once** at the very end of the pipeline.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Normalization. State the Min–Max normalization formula and explain its purpose in a Machine Learning pipeline.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Normalization is a feature-scaling technique that rescales the values of a feature column into a common range so that no single feature dominates due to its magnitude.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$, resulting in $0 \le X' \le 1$.
- **Purpose (1 mark):** It ensures that distance-based algorithms (KNN, SVM, K-Means) and gradient-descent-based algorithms converge faster and are not biased by the unit/scale of the input features.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
*What is a Training Set? Why must the test data be kept completely separate from the training data during model building?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A training set is the labelled subset of the dataset used by a learning algorithm to fit its internal parameters (weights, splits, etc.) by minimizing a loss function.
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** If the test data is seen during training, the model *memorizes* it, leading to **overfitting** and an **over-optimistic, biased** estimate of accuracy.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** A held-out test set provides an *unbiased estimate of generalization* — i.e., the model's true performance on unseen, real-world data.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the two most commonly used normalization techniques in Machine Learning: **Min–Max Scaling** and **Z-Score Standardization**. State the formula, the resulting range/properties, and one scenario where each is preferred. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the feature column $X = \{10,\ 20,\ 30,\ 40,\ 50,\ 60\}$. Apply *both* Min–Max normalization and Z-score standardization. Show all intermediate calculations and verify your answers. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Min–Max Scaling | Z-Score Standardization |
|--------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Formula | $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Output range | Bounded $[0, 1]$ (or custom $[a,b]$) | Unbounded; $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$ |
| Sensitivity to outliers | **High** | **Low (robust)** |
| Preferred when | Neural networks, image pixel data, bounded inputs | SVM, PCA, Logistic Regression with regularization, data with outliers |
| Drawback | A single outlier compresses all other values near 0 | Does not produce a fixed bounded range |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Min–Max Normalization:*

$$X_{\min} = 10, \quad X_{\max} = 60, \quad \text{Range} = 50$$

$$X'_i = \frac{X_i - 10}{50}$$

$X' = \{0.00,\ 0.20,\ 0.40,\ 0.60,\ 0.80,\ 1.00\}$ — *Verification:* $\min = 0,\ \max = 1$ ✓

*Z-Score Standardization:*

$$\mu = \frac{10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50 + 60}{6} = \frac{210}{6} = 35$$

$$\sum (X_i - \mu)^2 = (-25)^2 + (-15)^2 + (-5)^2 + 5^2 + 15^2 + 25^2 = 625 + 225 + 25 + 25 + 225 + 625 = 1750$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1750}{6} \approx 291.667, \quad \sigma \approx 17.078$$

$$Z_i = \frac{X_i - 35}{17.078}$$

$$Z = \{-1.464,\ -0.878,\ -0.293,\ 0.293,\ 0.878,\ 1.464\}$$

*Verification:* $\sum Z_i \approx 0$ ✓ (i.e., new mean is 0).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **Train–Validation–Test** data-splitting strategy used in supervised Machine Learning. Explain the role of each subset and state the typical percentage allocation. What is **k-fold cross-validation**, and why is it preferred over a single split when the dataset is small? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Machine Learning project uses a dataset of $N = 5000$ samples. The team decides on a 60 / 20 / 20 split. *(7 marks)*
    (i) Calculate the number of samples in the training, validation, and test sets.
    (ii) If the team decides to use **5-fold cross-validation on the training set** (i.e., 60% partition), how many samples go into each of the 5 folds?
    (iii) In each of the 5 iterations, how many samples are used for *training* and how many for *validation* inside the CV loop?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

- **Training set (typically 60–80%):** Used to fit the model parameters by minimizing the loss function. The model *sees* and *learns* from this data.
- **Validation set (typically 10–20%):** Used to tune *hyperparameters* (learning rate, regularization strength, number of layers, etc.) and to perform *model selection*. The model does *not* learn from this data, but the data influences the choice of the final model.
- **Test set (typically 10–20%):** Used *only once* at the very end to estimate the *unbiased generalization* performance of the locked, final model. It must remain *unseen* during both training and hyperparameter tuning.
- **k-Fold Cross-Validation (1.5 marks):** The training data is split into $k$ equal folds. In each of the $k$ iterations, $k-1$ folds are used for training and the remaining 1 fold is used for validation. The final score is the **average** of the $k$ validation scores.
- **Why preferred for small data (1 mark):** It uses *every* training sample for both training and validation across different iterations, providing a *more robust, less variance-sensitive* performance estimate than a single arbitrary split.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Three-way split with 60/20/20 on $N = 5000$ (2 marks):**

$$N_{\text{train}} = 0.60 \times 5000 = 3000 \text{ samples}$$

$$N_{\text{val}} = 0.20 \times 5000 = 1000 \text{ samples}$$

$$N_{\text{test}} = 0.20 \times 5000 = 1000 \text{ samples}$$

*Check:* $3000 + 1000 + 1000 = 5000$ ✓

**(ii) 5-fold CV on training set (2 marks):**

$$\text{Size of each fold} = \frac{3000}{5} = 600 \text{ samples per fold}$$

**(iii) Per-iteration split inside CV (3 marks):**

In each of the 5 iterations:
- **Training samples** (in $k-1 = 4$ folds):

$$N_{\text{CV-train}} = 4 \times 600 = 2400 \text{ samples}$$

- **Validation samples** (in the held-out 1 fold):

$$N_{\text{CV-val}} = 1 \times 600 = 600 \text{ samples}$$

*Final cross-validation score:*

$$CV_5 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} \text{Score}_i$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *sample*-wise vs *feature*-wise normalization** — Normalization in KTU Module 1 is performed **column-wise** (per feature), not row-wise (per sample). State this explicitly.
> 2. **Failing to recompute statistics on the training set only** — In practice, $\mu$, $\sigma$, $X_{\min}$, $X_{\max}$ are computed **only on the training set**, then *applied* to validation and test sets. Computing them on the full dataset causes **data leakage** and loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the boundary check** — Examiners award the *verification mark* only if you show that $\min(X') = 0$ and $\max(X') = 1$ (Min–Max) or that $\mu_Z \approx 0$ (Z-score).
> 4. **Reporting $\mu$ instead of $\sigma$** — Many students write "standard normalization" but compute only the mean. Always include the **standard deviation** in the denominator.
> 5. **Test set leakage in CV** — Cross-validation is performed **inside the training set only**. The test set is touched only at the very end. Mixing CV folds with the test set is a **3-mark penalty** in the KTU scheme.
> 6. **Reporting unscaled percentages** — Always state the final split as a numerical count (e.g., *3000 samples*) **and** a percentage (e.g., *60 %*) to satisfy the KTU rubric.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Normalization** rescales features to a common scale; it is a **preprocessing** step, not a learning step.
- **Min–Max Scaling** maps to $[0, 1]$ using $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$; **sensitive to outliers**.
- **Z-Score Standardization** centres at $\mu = 0$ and scales to $\sigma = 1$ using $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$; **robust to outliers**.
- Statistics ($\mu, \sigma, X_{\min}, X_{\max}$) for normalization are computed **only on the training set** and reused for validation and test sets.
- The **Training Set** is the only subset on which the model *learns* parameters.
- The **Validation Set** is for *hyperparameter tuning* and *model selection*.
- The **Test Set** is touched **only once** at the end for an unbiased performance estimate.
- Default KTU split: **70 / 15 / 15** (or 60 / 20 / 20) for large datasets; **5-fold or 10-fold cross-validation** for small datasets.
- Always perform a **boundary verification** step (check $\min = 0$, $\max = 1$ for Min–Max; $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$ for Z-score).
- Tree-based models (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost) are **scale-invariant** — normalization is optional but harmless.
- Distance-based and gradient-descent models (KNN, SVM, PCA, Linear/Logistic Regression, Neural Networks) are **highly scale-sensitive** — normalization is **mandatory**.
- **Data leakage** = using test/validation information to influence training. It is the single most common cause of unrealistically high model accuracy in student submissions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Set and Validation Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Test Set and Validation Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Supervised Machine Learning** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1), a single dataset is never directly fed into a model as a whole. The dataset is systematically partitioned into three mutually exclusive subsets to ensure **generalization**, **unbiased evaluation**, and **reliable hyperparameter tuning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training Set** ($D_{train}$): The portion of the dataset used to *fit* the model parameters (weights $\theta$) by minimizing the empirical loss function $J(\theta)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Set** ($D_{val}$): The portion of the dataset used to *tune hyperparameters* (e.g., learning rate $\eta$, regularization strength $\lambda$, number of hidden layers) and to perform **model selection** without leaking information from the unseen test data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Set** ($D_{test}$): The portion of the dataset used *only once* at the very end to provide an **unbiased estimate of the final model's generalization performance** on completely unseen, out-of-sample data.

The complete dataset is therefore partitioned as:

$$D = D_{train} \cup D_{val} \cup D_{test}, \quad D_{train} \cap D_{val} = \emptyset, \quad D_{val} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset, \quad D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU End Semester Examination.

- The **Training Set** is your daily classroom learning, solving textbook problems, and attending lab sessions. You repeatedly "learn" from these examples and tune your understanding.
- The **Validation Set** is your **internal assessment exams and series tests** (held multiple times during the semester). You use the marks you get in these tests to figure out *which study strategy works best* — should you focus more on numericals, or theory, or previous year questions? You "select the best model" of preparation based on validation feedback.
- The **Test Set** is the **final KTU University Examination**. You see the actual exam paper *only once*, and your score reflects the true quality of your preparation. If you had practiced with the actual exam paper beforehand (data leakage), your score would be misleadingly inflated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The validation set acts as a "mock test" used repeatedly for tuning, while the test set is the "final exam" used exactly once for honest evaluation.

## 1.3 The Three Core Roles in Bullet Form

- **Training Set Purpose**: Parameter learning — finding $\theta$ that minimizes $J(\theta)$ on $D_{train}$.
- **Validation Set Purpose**: Hyperparameter optimization and model selection — choosing the best architecture, regularization, learning rate, etc.
- **Test Set Purpose**: Final, one-time, unbiased generalization estimate — reporting the final accuracy, F1-score, RMSE, etc.

## 1.4 Standard Notation Table (KTU 2024 Compliant)

| Symbol | Meaning | Cardinality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D$ | Complete dataset | $N$ total samples |
| $D_{train}$ | Training subset | $\approx 0.60N$ to $0.70N$ |
| $D_{val}$ | Validation subset | $\approx 0.10N$ to $0.20N$ |
| $D_{test}$ | Test subset | $\approx 0.15N$ to $0.20N$ |
| $D_{train}^{(i)}$ | $i^{th}$ training fold (in k-fold) | $\approx \frac{k-1}{k}N$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Single "Test Set" is Not Enough — The Logical Argument

A common student misconception is: *"Why not train and test on the same data and report 99% accuracy?"*

**Logical Step 1 — Memorization vs. Learning:**
A model trained and evaluated on the same data measures **training error** $E_{train}$, not **generalization error** $E_{gen}$. A high-capacity model (e.g., deep neural network) can simply **memorize** $D_{train}$, driving $E_{train} \to 0$ while failing on new data. This is called **overfitting**.

**Logical Step 2 — The Need for Tuning:**
Real ML pipelines have **hyperparameters** $\mathcal{H} = \{\eta, \lambda, k, d, \dots\}$ that are *not learned* via gradient descent. To choose the best $\mathcal{H}$, we need an intermediate evaluation set, hence the **validation set**.

**Logical Step 3 — The Leakage Problem:**
If we use $D_{test}$ repeatedly to tune hyperparameters, then our choice of $\mathcal{H}$ becomes implicitly dependent on $D_{test}$. The reported test score becomes **optimistically biased** — this is **data leakage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of KTU Examinations (and real ML):** Touch the test set **only once** at the end. All model selection decisions must be made using the validation set.

## 2.2 The Three Principal Data Splitting Strategies

### Strategy A — Simple Hold-Out Method
- Randomly split $D$ into $D_{train}$, $D_{val}$, $D_{test}$ in a fixed ratio (e.g., **70% / 15% / 15%**).
- Train on $D_{train}$, tune on $D_{val}$, evaluate on $D_{test}$.
- **Pro**: Computationally cheap. **Con**: High variance — the score depends heavily on which samples landed in which subset.

### Strategy B — K-Fold Cross-Validation (the KTU-favoured method)
- Split $D_{train} \cup D_{val}$ into $k$ equal-sized folds: $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_k$.
- For each fold $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$:
  - Train on $D \setminus F_i$ (i.e., $k-1$ folds).
  - Validate on $F_i$.
- Average the $k$ validation scores to obtain a robust estimate.
- After CV selects the best $\mathcal{H}$, **retrain on the full $D_{train} \cup D_{val}$** using those chosen hyperparameters, then evaluate *once* on $D_{test}$.

### Strategy C — Stratified K-Fold (essential for classification)
- Same as K-Fold, but each fold is constructed to preserve the **class distribution** (proportion of positive/negative labels) of the original dataset.
- Critical for **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., fraud detection, disease diagnosis).

## 2.3 Bias-Variance Decomposition (Linked to Validation/Test Error)

The expected prediction error of a learning algorithm at a point $x$ can be decomposed as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{variance across datasets}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

- **High Bias** (Underfitting) → Both $E_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ are high and close together.
- **High Variance** (Overfitting) → $E_{train}$ is low, but $E_{val}$ is much higher.
- The **validation error** is our best proxy for the true generalization error before we touch the test set.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Module tests.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total dataset cardinality | $N = \vert D_{train}\vert + \vert D_{val}\vert + \vert D_{test}\vert$ | Mutually exclusive subsets |
| Standard split ratio | $60\% \text{ / } 20\% \text{ / } 20\%$ (or $70/15/15$) | Order of magnitude — no $\vert$ symbol |
| K-Fold CV score | $CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$ where $L_i$ = loss on fold $F_i$ | Lower loss $\Rightarrow$ better model |
| Stratified sampling probability | $P(\text{class } c \text{ in fold}) = P(\text{class } c \text{ in } D)$ | Preserves class ratio |
| Bias-Variance decomposition | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Foundational formula |
| Generalization gap | $\text{Gap} = E_{val} - E_{train}$ | Large gap $\Rightarrow$ overfitting |
| Empirical Risk (training) | $R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N_{train}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{train}} \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{y}_i)$ | Used in training phase |
| Expected Risk (test) | $R_{true}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{data}}\left[\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})\right]$ | Estimated via $D_{test}$ |
| Leave-One-Out CV ($k = N$) | $LOOCV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(y_i, \hat{y}_{-i})$ | Special case of K-Fold |
| Hyperparameter grid size | $\vert \mathcal{H}\vert = \prod_{j=1}^{m} \vert H_j\vert$ | Total combinations to try |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare ML**: Validation set picks the optimal decision threshold for cancer detection; test set gives the honest sensitivity/specificity reported in the published paper.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Train/Val/Test splits ensure the self-driving car generalizes to unseen road conditions, not just the recorded training routes.
- **NLP & LLMs**: Validation perplexity guides the choice of model size and training duration; test perplexity is reported only once in the final benchmark.
- **KTU Project Evaluation**: Your final-year project's reported accuracy **must** be on a held-out test set to be considered credible by the evaluation panel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $k$-Fold Cross-Validation Reduces Variance

**Goal**: Show mathematically that averaging $k$ validation scores gives a lower-variance estimate of the true generalization error compared to a single hold-out split.

**Step 1 — Define the single-fold validation error.**
For a single random hold-out split $S$ of size $m = N/k$, the validation error is:

$$E_{val}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(y_j, \hat{f}^{(S)}(x_j))$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $E_{val}^{(1)}$ is a random variable.**
Because the split $S$ is random, $E_{val}^{(1)}$ varies from run to run. Let its variance be $\sigma_S^2$:

$$\text{Var}\left(E_{val}^{(1)}\right) = \sigma_S^2$$

**Step 3 — Construct $k$ independent folds.**
In $k$-fold CV, the $k$ fold-errors $L_1, L_2, \dots, L_k$ are computed on $k$ different (largely independent) validation subsets. The CV score is their average:

$$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$$

**Step 4 — Apply the variance property of an average.**
Assuming the $L_i$ are i.i.d. with variance $\sigma_L^2$:

$$\text{Var}\left(CV_{(k)}\right) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i\right) = \frac{1}{k^2}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\text{Var}(L_i)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression.**

$$\text{Var}\left(CV_{(k)}\right) = \frac{1}{k^2} \cdot k \cdot \sigma_L^2 = \frac{\sigma_L^2}{k}$$

**Step 6 — Final result and interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}\left(CV_{(k)}\right) = \frac{\sigma_L^2}{k} \quad \ll \quad \sigma_L^2 = \text{Var}\left(E_{val}^{(1)}\right)}$$

**Interpretation**: $k$-fold CV reduces the variance of the performance estimate by a factor of $k$. This is *the* mathematical justification for preferring CV over a single hold-out split when compute permits.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Mean Squared Error Case)

**Step 1 — Start with the squared error at a fixed test point $x$, with true label $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is zero-mean noise with $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$.**

$$\text{Error}(x) = \mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \mid x\right]$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract the expected prediction $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error}(x) &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] \\
&\quad + 2\,\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the cross-term vanishes.**
The cross-term expectation is zero because $y$ and $\hat{f}(x)$ are independent and $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ is a constant:

$$2\,\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\right)\right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand the first term using $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(f(x) + \varepsilon - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right] \\
&= \left(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2 + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] + 2\left(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] \\
&= \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the second term as the variance.**

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the canonical decomposition.**

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Splitting Strategy

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Small dataset ($N < 1000$) | **Stratified K-Fold ($k = 5$ or $10$)** | Maximizes use of every sample for both training and validation |
| Large dataset ($N > 100{,}000$) | **Simple Hold-Out (80/10/10 or 98/1/1)** | CV becomes computationally prohibitive |
| Time-series data | **Forward Chaining / Rolling Window CV** | Random splits leak future information |
| Imbalanced classification | **Stratified K-Fold (mandatory)** | Preserves minority-class proportion in every fold |
| Deep Learning with $k$ epochs | **Fixed Train/Val/Test + early stopping on $D_{val}$** | Use $D_{val}$ loss plateau to pick epoch |
| Hyperparameter search over grid $\mathcal{H}$ | **Inner CV for tuning, Outer Hold-Out Test** | Nested CV pattern |
| KTU lab project (typical $N \approx 500$) | **70/15/15 Hold-Out** with **5-fold CV on the 85%** | Practical and demonstrable |
| KTU University exam written answer | **Always mention Train/Val/Test split + reason** | Mark awarded for justification, not just the ratio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Three-Way Data Split Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complete Dataset D of size N] --> B[Random Shuffle and Stratified Split]
    B --> C1[Training Set Dtrain 60 to 70 percent]
    B --> C2[Validation Set Dval 10 to 20 percent]
    B --> C3[Test Set Dtest 15 to 20 percent]
    C1 --> D1[Model Parameter Learning theta]
    D1 --> E1[Trained Model M1 with theta]
    C2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Compute Validation Error Eval]
    F1 --> G1{Hyperparameter Tuning}
    G1 -->|Adjust H| D1
    G1 -->|Best H found| H1[Lock Hyperparameters]
    C3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Retrain on Dtrain plus Dval]
    I1 --> J1[Final Model M Final]
    C3 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Report Test Error Etest ONCE]
    K1 --> L1[Submitted to KTU and Stakeholders]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — K-Fold Cross-Validation Inner Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Pool of Dtrain plus Dval size N minus Ntest] --> S1[Partition into k equal folds F1 to Fk]
    S1 --> L1[i equals 1]
    L1 --> M1[Train on all folds except Fi]
    M1 --> N1[Validate on Fi and record Li]
    N1 --> P1{i less than k}
    P1 -->|Yes| Q1[i equals i plus 1]
    Q1 --> M1
    P1 -->|No| R1[Compute CVk equals mean of L1 to Lk]
    R1 --> T1{Best CVk across hyperparameter grid}
    T1 -->|Select best H| U1[Retrain on full pool with best H]
    U1 --> V1[Final Evaluation on Dtest ONCE]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Learning-Curve Diagnostic View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Capacity Model] --> A1[High Bias Underfitting]
    A1 --> A2[Etrain high and Eval high and Gap small]
    B[Medium Capacity Model] --> B1[Balanced Fit]
    B1 --> B2[Etrain low and Eval low and Gap small]
    C[High Capacity Model] --> C1[High Variance Overfitting]
    C1 --> C2[Etrain very low and Eval high and Gap large]
    B2 --> D[Sweet Spot on Validation Curve]
    A2 --> D
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Select this Model and Report on Test Set]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — The Data Leakage Prevention Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Step 1 Lock Test Set Aside First] --> X2[Step 2 Perform ALL Preprocessing EDA Imputation Scaling using Training Set ONLY]
    X2 --> X3[Step 3 Apply Same Transformations to Validation and Test]
    X3 --> X4[Step 4 Train Models on Training Data]
    X4 --> X5[Step 5 Tune Hyperparameters using Validation Data]
    X5 --> X6[Step 6 Confirm No Peeking at Test Set]
    X6 -->|Confirmed| X7[Step 7 Final Evaluation on Test Set ONCE]
    X6 -->|Violation Detected| X8[STOP Restart Pipeline]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Validation Set and Test Set in Machine Learning. Why is it necessary to keep them separate? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Validation Set** is used during the model development phase to tune hyperparameters and perform model selection, while the **Test Set** is used only once at the end to provide an unbiased estimate of the final model's generalization performance on completely unseen data.

They must be kept separate because if the test set is used repeatedly for tuning, information from the test set leaks into the model selection process. This causes the reported test accuracy to become **optimistically biased** and unreliable. The validation set acts as a "proxy test set" that can be used multiple times safely, while the test set remains a pristine, untouched benchmark.

| Aspect | Validation Set | Test Set |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Used for | Hyperparameter tuning | Final reporting |
| Touched how many times | Multiple times | Exactly once |
| Bias in estimate | Slightly optimistic | Unbiased |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for leakage explanation.)*

### Question 2: What is K-Fold Cross-Validation? Mention any two advantages over the simple hold-out method. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**K-Fold Cross-Validation** is a model evaluation technique in which the training data is divided into $k$ equal-sized folds. The model is trained $k$ times, each time using $k-1$ folds for training and the remaining one fold for validation. The final performance metric is the **average of the $k$ validation scores**:

$$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$$

**Two Advantages over Hold-Out:**

1. **Reduced variance**: The variance of the CV score is $\sigma^2 / k$, which is $k$ times smaller than the variance of a single hold-out split, yielding a more stable performance estimate.

2. **Efficient use of data**: Every sample is used for both training and validation exactly $k-1$ and $1$ times respectively, which is especially valuable for small datasets where hold-out would waste precious training data.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark each for two advantages.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice (either-or). The model answers below follow the standard **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** sub-division.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Explain the need for splitting a dataset into Training, Validation, and Test sets in a Machine Learning project. Discuss the typical split ratios used in practice and the role of stratified sampling for classification problems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Machine Learning models must ultimately perform well on *unseen* data, not just on the data they were trained on. To develop and evaluate such models rigorously, the available dataset must be partitioned into three distinct subsets, each serving a unique purpose in the ML pipeline.

**Need for Three-Way Split:**

- The **Training Set** is essential for the model to learn the underlying patterns and adjust its internal parameters $\theta$ (such as weights in a neural network or coefficients in linear regression) by minimizing the empirical loss function $J(\theta)$.

- The **Validation Set** is required because every ML algorithm has **hyperparameters** (e.g., learning rate $\eta$, regularization coefficient $\lambda$, tree depth, number of clusters $k$) that cannot be learned directly from the data. The validation set provides a mechanism to compare different hyperparameter settings and select the best-performing model architecture.

- The **Test Set** provides a final, unbiased estimate of how the chosen model will perform in real-world deployment. Critically, it must remain untouched during model development to avoid **data leakage**, which would otherwise produce misleadingly high reported accuracies.

**Typical Split Ratios:**

- **60/20/20** is the most common convention, suitable for moderate-sized datasets ($N$ in the thousands).
- **70/15/15** is preferred when the dataset is larger and ample training data is desired.
- **80/10/10** or even **98/1/1** is used in very large-scale settings (millions of samples) where cross-validation is computationally infeasible.

**Role of Stratified Sampling in Classification:**

In classification problems, a purely random split may, by chance, place all minority-class samples into the test set, leading to unreliable evaluation. **Stratified sampling** ensures that each subset (train/val/test) preserves the **original class distribution** $P(\text{class } c)$ of the full dataset. This is especially important for **imbalanced datasets** such as medical diagnosis (where diseased cases may be $< 5\%$) or fraud detection. Stratification guarantees that every subset is *representative* of the class proportions, leading to more reliable and reproducible evaluation metrics.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for three-way need, 2 marks for split ratios with reasoning, 2 marks for stratified definition, 1 mark for imbalanced-data context.)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the K-Fold Cross-Validation procedure. Show that the variance of the K-Fold CV estimate is $k$ times smaller than the variance of a single hold-out validation score. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**K-Fold Cross-Validation Procedure:**

The training pool of size $N - N_{test}$ is randomly partitioned into $k$ approximately equal-sized, non-overlapping folds $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_k$, each of size $m = (N - N_{test})/k$. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Initialize**: For $i = 1$ to $k$:
2. **Train**: Fit the model on the union of all folds except $F_i$, i.e., on $D \setminus F_i$.
3. **Validate**: Compute the validation loss $L_i$ on the held-out fold $F_i$.
4. **Aggregate**: After all $k$ iterations, the final CV score is the average:

$$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$$

5. **Refit and Evaluate**: The chosen hyperparameters are then used to retrain the model on the entire pool ($D \setminus D_{test}$), and a final evaluation is performed once on the untouched $D_{test}$.

**Variance Derivation:**

Let $L_1, L_2, \dots, L_k$ be the $k$ fold-validation losses, treated as independent random variables each with variance $\sigma_L^2$.

The CV estimate is the sample mean:

$$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$$

Using the variance property of a sum of independent random variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\left(CV_{(k)}\right) &= \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Var}(L_i) \\
&= \frac{1}{k^2} \cdot k \cdot \sigma_L^2 \\
&= \frac{\sigma_L^2}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

The single hold-out validation score has variance $\sigma_L^2$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\text{Var}(\text{Hold-Out})}{\text{Var}(CV_{(k)})} = \frac{\sigma_L^2}{\sigma_L^2 / k} = k$$

This proves that the K-Fold CV estimate has a variance that is **$k$ times smaller** than the single hold-out estimate, making it a more stable and reliable performance metric.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for diagram description, 2 marks for variance derivation, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Discuss the Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Machine Learning. Explain how the gap between training error and validation error can be used as a diagnostic tool to detect overfitting and underfitting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bias-Variance Tradeoff:**

The expected prediction error (Mean Squared Error) of a learned model $\hat{f}$ at a query point $x$ can be formally decomposed as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2$$

where $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ is the systematic error of the model, $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$ is the variability of the model's predictions across different training datasets, and $\sigma^2$ is the **irreducible noise** inherent in the data.

The **tradeoff** is fundamental: as we increase model complexity (e.g., add more polynomial features, increase neural-network depth), the **bias decreases** because the model can fit more intricate patterns. However, the **variance increases** because the model becomes more sensitive to the specific training samples. Conversely, simpler models have high bias but low variance. The goal is to find the model complexity that minimizes the *sum* $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$, excluding the uncontrollable $\sigma^2$.

**Diagnostic Use of the Generalization Gap:**

Let $E_{train}$ be the training error and $E_{val}$ be the validation error. The **generalization gap** is defined as:

$$\text{Gap} = E_{val} - E_{train}$$

- **Underfitting Diagnosis** (High Bias): Both $E_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ are **high** and the Gap is **small**. The model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern. Remedies: increase model capacity, add features, reduce regularization $\lambda$.

- **Overfitting Diagnosis** (High Variance): $E_{train}$ is **low** but $E_{val}$ is **high**, producing a **large Gap**. The model has memorized the training data but fails to generalize. Remedies: collect more data, apply regularization, use dropout, reduce model complexity.

- **Good Fit**: Both $E_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ are low and the Gap is small. This represents the sweet spot of the bias-variance tradeoff.

The validation set thus serves as the essential diagnostic instrument for detecting which regime the model is in, *before* the test set is ever consulted.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for tradeoff + decomposition formula, 2 marks for explaining the two regimes, 2 marks for the gap formula and its interpretation, 1 mark for remedies.)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of Nested Cross-Validation. Why is it preferred when simultaneously performing hyperparameter tuning and model evaluation? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Nested Cross-Validation:**

**Nested Cross-Validation (Nested CV)** is a two-level CV procedure designed for situations where we must *simultaneously* perform (i) **hyperparameter tuning** and (ii) **unbiased model evaluation**.

- **Outer Loop ($k_{out}$ folds)**: Used purely for **model evaluation**. The test fold of each outer iteration remains untouched by the inner tuning process.

- **Inner Loop ($k_{in}$ folds)**: Used purely for **hyperparameter selection** within each outer training set.

The procedure is:

1. For each outer fold $F_i^{(out)}$:
   - Treat $F_i^{(out)}$ as a temporary test set.
   - On the remaining outer training data, perform an inner $k_{in}$-fold CV over the hyperparameter grid $\mathcal{H}$.
   - Select the hyperparameters $\mathcal{H}^*$ that minimize the inner CV score.
   - Retrain the model on the entire outer training set using $\mathcal{H}^*$.
   - Evaluate on the untouched $F_i^{(out)}$, recording the test score $T_i$.

2. The final reported performance is the average of the outer test scores:

$$\text{Nested CV Score} = \frac{1}{k_{out}}\sum_{i=1}^{k_{out}} T_i$$

**Why It Is Preferred:**

In a naive single-loop $k$-fold CV, when we use the same validation folds to select the best hyperparameters, the resulting score is **optimistically biased** because the model choice was directly informed by those validation scores. Nested CV resolves this by **physically separating** the data used for tuning from the data used for evaluation.

The outer test fold never participates in any hyperparameter selection decision, so the reported $\text{Nested CV Score}$ is an **honest, unbiased** estimate of how the chosen model would perform on truly unseen data. This makes Nested CV the gold standard for benchmarking ML algorithms in academic publications and is the recommended approach in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 whenever both tuning and evaluation are needed.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for outer loop concept, 2 marks for inner loop concept, 2 marks for the decoupling justification, 1 mark for the final aggregate formula and conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Validation and Test sets**: Writing "validation set is used to test the model" costs 1–2 marks. Always emphasize that the test set is touched *only once at the end*.
>
> 2. **Stating split ratios without justification**: Just writing "70/30" is incomplete. Explain *why* a particular ratio is chosen (dataset size, computational cost, class imbalance).
>
> 3. **Skipping the variance derivation in K-Fold**: Many students state "K-Fold is better" without deriving $\text{Var}(CV_{(k)}) = \sigma^2/k$. The derivation is a guaranteed **2–3 mark** question.
>
> 4. **Ignoring stratified sampling**: For classification questions, forgetting to mention stratified sampling for imbalanced data is a frequent mark-loss.
>
> 5. **Reporting training accuracy as final accuracy**: Always specify that the *test* accuracy (or $R^2$, F1, etc.) is computed on the held-out test set, not the training set.
>
> 6. **Data leakage unawareness**: If a question mentions preprocessing (scaling, imputation), explicitly state that the *fit* must be done on training data only and then *transformed* onto validation and test sets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Three subsets**: Training (learn $\theta$), Validation (tune $\mathcal{H}$), Test (final report — touch once).
- [x] **Disjointness**: $D_{train} \cap D_{val} = D_{val} \cap D_{test} = D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$.
- [x] **Standard ratios**: 60/20/20, 70/15/15, or 80/10/10 depending on $N$.
- [x] **Golden rule**: Test set is evaluated **exactly once** at the end of the pipeline.
- [x] **K-Fold CV formula**: $CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$.
- [x] **Variance reduction**: $\text{Var}(CV_{(k)}) = \sigma_L^2 / k$ — derivation is mandatory.
- [x] **Stratified K-Fold** is mandatory for imbalanced classification.
- [x] **Bias-Variance decomposition**: $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- [x] **Generalization gap**: $E_{val} - E_{train}$. Small gap + low error = good fit. Large gap = overfitting. Small gap + high error = underfitting.
- [x] **Nested CV** decouples hyperparameter tuning (inner loop) from evaluation (outer loop) for unbiased benchmarking.
- [x] **LOOCV**: $k = N$ — unbiased but high variance and computationally expensive.
- [x] **Time-series CV** uses forward chaining, not random splits (to prevent future leakage).
- [x] **Data leakage prevention**: Fit all preprocessing on training data, then transform val/test.
- [x] **Validation curve** and **learning curve** are diagnostic tools to pick model complexity.
- [x] **Final answer in KTU exams** must include: (i) which subset, (ii) what is computed, (iii) why this subset is appropriate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Engineering - Feature Selection and Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feature Engineering - Feature Selection and Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Feature Engineering?

**Feature Engineering** is the process of transforming raw data into meaningful, informative features that better represent the underlying problem to the predictive models, thereby improving machine learning algorithm performance. It is often considered the most critical step in the machine learning pipeline, frequently outweighing the choice of algorithm in determining model accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEITT721):** Feature Engineering refers to the process of selecting, modifying, or creating new features (input variables) from raw data to enhance the performance of machine learning models.
