It encompasses two primary sub-processes: **Feature Selection** (choosing a subset of existing features) and **Feature Extraction** (deriving new, compact features from existing ones).

## 1.2 The Two Pillars: Selection vs Extraction

The KTU syllabus explicitly distinguishes between these two concepts. Let us first understand them through analogy before formalizing them.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Moving House

Imagine you are moving to a new, smaller apartment. You have **100 boxes** of belongings but only space for **20 boxes**.

**Option A — Feature Selection (Curating what you already have):**
You open every box and *choose* the 20 most essential ones to keep (winter clothes, important documents, laptop). The remaining 80 boxes are left behind. The chosen 20 boxes are *original, untouched items* — you simply picked the best ones.

**Option B — Feature Extraction (Synthesizing new, compact items):**
You take all 100 boxes and *melt them down* into 20 new boxes that capture the *essence* of everything (e.g., one composite "essentials box" containing a representative mix of all categories). The 20 new boxes are *newly engineered* — you transformed the original items.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Selection preserves the **original meaning** of variables. Extraction **transforms** the feature space entirely (e.g., 100 raw pixels → 10 principal components).

### Formal Definitions

| Concept | Definition | Output | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature Selection** | Identifying and retaining only the most relevant subset of original features, eliminating redundant or noisy ones. | A *subset* of original features. | Dropping "Customer ID" and "ZIP Code" from a churn dataset. |
| **Feature Extraction** | Projecting high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional space by constructing new composite features. | A *new set* of transformed features. | Applying **PCA** to reduce 784 image pixels to 50 components. |

## 1.3 The Feature Engineering Pipeline (KTU Context)

In the KTU 2024 OEITT721 syllabus, Feature Engineering is the bridge between *Data Preprocessing* and *Model Building*. The typical flow is:

1. **Raw Data** → 2. **Cleaning** → 3. **Transformation** (Scaling, Encoding) → 4. **Feature Engineering (Selection / Extraction)** → 5. **Model Training**

## 1.4 Why Does It Matter? (Engineering Utility)

- **Reduces Overfitting:** Fewer irrelevant features mean less noise for the model to memorize.
- **Improves Accuracy:** Well-chosen features expose patterns the algorithm can learn.
- **Reduces Training Time:** Smaller feature spaces → faster computation. Critical in real-time systems (e.g., IoT edge devices, autonomous vehicles).
- **Enhances Interpretability:** Engineers and stakeholders can understand decisions better.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curse of Dimensionality:** As the number of features ($d$) grows, the data becomes exponentially sparse. Feature Engineering is the primary engineering solution to combat this curse.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feature Selection: The Three Strategic Families

Feature Selection methods are categorized into three families based on how they interact with the learning algorithm.

### 2.1.1 Filter Methods (Model-Agnostic Ranking)

These methods rank features using a statistical measure, *independent* of any ML algorithm. They are fast and form the first line of defense in KTU lab exams.

**A. Pearson Correlation Coefficient (Linear Dependence)**
Measures the linear relationship between a feature $X$ and target $Y$:

$$r_{XY} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$$

**B. Information Gain (IG) — Entropy-Based Ranking**
Borrowed from Decision Trees. Entropy $H(S)$ measures impurity in a set $S$:

$$H(S) = - \sum_{c \in C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

Information Gain for feature $F$ splitting set $S$ into subsets $S_v$:

$$IG(S, F) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(F)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**C. Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test (Categorical Features)**
Tests independence between a categorical feature and categorical target:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

where $O_{ij}$ = observed frequency, $E_{ij}$ = expected frequency, $r$ = rows, $c$ = columns.

**D. ANOVA F-Statistic (Numerical Features, Categorical Target)**
Measures variance between groups relative to variance within groups:

$$F = \frac{\text{Between-group variability}}{\text{Within-group variability}} = \frac{MS_{\text{between}}}{MS_{\text{within}}}$$

### 2.1.2 Wrapper Methods (Algorithm-Driven Search)

These treat feature selection as a *search problem*, training a model repeatedly with different feature subsets. Slower but highly accurate.

- **Forward Selection:** Start with 0 features. Iteratively add the feature that most improves model performance. Stop when no improvement.
- **Backward Elimination:** Start with all features. Iteratively remove the least important feature. Stop when performance degrades.
- **Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE):** Train model → rank features → remove weakest → repeat until desired count.

### 2.1.3 Embedded Methods (Built-in Regularization)

Feature selection is performed *during* model training itself.

- **L1 Regularization (Lasso):** Adds penalty $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ to the loss function. Forces irrelevant feature weights to **exactly zero**.
- **L2 Regularization (Ridge):** Adds penalty $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$. Shrinks weights but does not zero them out.
- **Tree-based Importance:** Random Forest and XGBoost natively compute feature importance scores based on impurity reduction (Gini Importance) or permutation.

## 2.2 Feature Extraction: Linear & Non-Linear Projections

### 2.2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — The King of Feature Extraction

PCA finds orthogonal axes (principal components) that **maximize variance** in the data. It is the most frequently asked extraction method in KTU papers.

**Core Objective:** Find a projection $\mathbf{w}$ such that the variance of projected data $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$ is maximized.

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$$

where $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is the data covariance matrix. The solution is the **eigenvector** of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ corresponding to the largest **eigenvalue** $\lambda$.

The variance captured by the $k^{th}$ principal component equals its eigenvalue $\lambda_k$.

### 2.2.2 Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) — Supervised Projection

Unlike PCA (unsupervised), LDA finds axes that **maximize class separability** by maximizing the **between-class variance** $S_B$ relative to the **within-class variance** $S_W$.

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the eigenvector of $S_W^{-1} S_B$. LDA can extract at most $c - 1$ components where $c$ = number of classes.

### 2.2.3 t-SNE (Non-Linear Extraction)

Used primarily for **visualization** of high-dimensional data in 2D/3D. Minimizes Kullback–Leibler divergence between probability distributions in high and low dimensions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| Method | Type | Key Formula | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pearson Correlation** | Filter | $r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | Keep if $\vert r \vert > 0.3$ |
| **Information Gain** | Filter | $IG(S,F) = H(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Keep if $IG > \text{threshold}$ |
| **Chi-Square** | Filter | $\chi^2 = \sum \sum \frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | Keep if $p\text{-value} < 0.05$ |
| **ANOVA F-test** | Filter | $F = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W}$ | Keep if $p\text{-value} < 0.05$ |
| **L1 (Lasso)** | Embedded | $\text{Loss} = \sum (y - \hat{y})^2 + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ | Non-zero $\beta$ = kept feature |
| **L2 (Ridge)** | Embedded | $\text{Loss} = \sum (y - \hat{y})^2 + \lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | Shrinks, never zeros |
| **PCA Variance** | Extraction | $\text{Explained Ratio} = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | Keep PCs until ratio $\geq 0.95$ |
| **LDA Objective** | Extraction | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$ | Max class separability |

## 2.4 Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis:** PCA on gene expression data reduces 20,000 genes to 100 components for cancer detection.
- **Image Processing:** Feature extraction converts raw pixels into edge detectors, textures (using Gabor filters, SIFT, HOG).
- **NLP:** TF-IDF is itself a feature extraction technique — converting text into a numerical vector.
- **IoT Sensor Fusion:** Selecting only relevant sensor channels to reduce bandwidth and battery drain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Rule for ESE:** In exam questions involving "select top-k features", always justify *why* you chose that k (e.g., "k=5 to retain 95% variance" or "based on elbow plot").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Information Gain Calculation (Selection)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** Given a dataset $S$ of 14 instances with target variable "Play Tennis" (Yes/No), a feature $F$ = "Outlook" splits $S$ as follows:
- $S_{\text{Sunny}} = 5$ instances (2 Yes, 3 No)
- $S_{\text{Overcast}} = 4$ instances (4 Yes, 0 No)
- $S_{\text{Rainy}} = 5$ instances (3 Yes, 2 No)

Compute the Information Gain of feature "Outlook". Should it be selected?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the entropy of the original set $S$.**

The original set has 9 Yes and 5 No out of 14.

$$H(S) = -p_{\text{Yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} - p_{\text{No}} \log_2 p_{\text{No}}$$

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \left(\frac{5}{14}\right)$$

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \cdot (-0.6365) - \frac{5}{14} \cdot (-1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4093 + 0.5305 = 0.940 \; \text{bits}$$

> *Logic: This is the total impurity before splitting. Higher means more mixed data.*

**Step 2: Compute the entropy of each child subset.**

**For $S_{\text{Sunny}}$** (2 Yes, 3 No out of 5):

$$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -\frac{2}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$$

$$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -\frac{2}{5} \cdot (-1.3219) - \frac{3}{5} \cdot (-0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.971 \; \text{bits}$$

**For $S_{\text{Overcast}}$** (4 Yes, 0 No out of 4):

$$H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = -\frac{4}{4} \log_2 \left(\frac{4}{4}\right) - 0 = 0 \; \text{bits}$$

> *Logic: Pure subset, zero entropy — every overcast day leads to "Play Tennis = Yes".*

**For $S_{\text{Rainy}}$** (3 Yes, 2 No out of 5):

$$H(S_{\text{Rainy}}) = -\frac{3}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)$$

$$H(S_{\text{Rainy}}) = -\frac{3}{5} \cdot (-0.7370) - \frac{2}{5} \cdot (-1.3219) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.971 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 3: Compute the weighted average entropy after the split.**

$$H(S \mid F) = \frac{5}{14} H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) + \frac{4}{14} H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) + \frac{5}{14} H(S_{\text{Rainy}})$$

$$H(S \mid F) = \frac{5}{14}(0.971) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.971)$$

$$H(S \mid F) = 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.694 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Information Gain.**

$$IG(S, F) = H(S) - H(S \mid F) = 0.940 - 0.694 = 0.246 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since $IG = 0.246 > 0$ and is reasonably high (Decision Tree's default in ID3/C4.5 algorithms), the feature **"Outlook" should be selected** as the root split criterion.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: PCA Derivation on a Toy 2D Dataset

**Problem Statement:** Given 2D data points centered at origin: $X = \{ (2,1), (3,2), (4,3), (1,0) \}$. Compute the first principal component and reduce to 1D.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Construct the data matrix and center it.**

The mean of the data is:

$$\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \left( \frac{2+3+4+1}{4}, \frac{1+2+3+0}{4} \right) = (2.5, 1.5)$$

Subtracting the mean to center:

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \\ -1.5 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the covariance matrix.**

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c$$

Computing the individual terms:

$$\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} (-0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2 + (-1.5)^2 & (-0.5)(-0.5) + (0.5)(0.5) + (1.5)(1.5) + (-1.5)(-1.5) \\ \text{same} & \text{same} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 & 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 \\ 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 & 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Dividing by $n-1 = 3$:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/3 & 5/3 \\ 5/3 & 5/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Solve the eigenproblem $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.**

$$\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0 \implies \begin{vmatrix} 5/3 - \lambda & 5/3 \\ 5/3 & 5/3 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

$$(5/3 - \lambda)^2 - (5/3)^2 = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - \frac{10}{3}\lambda = 0 \implies \lambda(\lambda - 10/3) = 0$$

So $\lambda_1 = 10/3 \approx 3.333$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$.

**Step 4: Find the principal eigenvector.**

For $\lambda_1 = 10/3$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 5/3 - 10/3 & 5/3 \\ 5/3 & 5/3 - 10/3 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0} \implies \begin{bmatrix} -5/3 & 5/3 \\ 5/3 & -5/3 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

This gives $w_1 = w_2$. Normalizing: $\mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} (1, 1)^T \approx (0.707, 0.707)^T$.

**Step 5: Project the data to 1D.**

$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \\ -1.5 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.707 \\ 0.707 \\ 2.121 \\ -2.121 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Variance retained.**

$$\text{Explained Variance Ratio} = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{10/3}{10/3 + 0} = 100\%$$

Since the data lies perfectly along the line $y = x$, PCA retains 100% variance with just 1 component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized PCA Algorithm (5 Steps to memorize for ESE):**
> 1. Center the data (subtract mean).
> 2. Compute covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$.
> 3. Compute eigenvectors and eigenvalues of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$.
> 4. Sort eigenvectors in descending order of eigenvalues.
> 5. Project data onto top-$k$ eigenvectors.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: When to Use What?

| Method | Supervised? | Dataset Size | Best For | Computation Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pearson Correlation** | No (Filter) | Small to large | Linear relationships with target | Very low |
| **Information Gain** | Yes (Filter) | Categorical data | Decision tree pre-filtering | Low |
| **L1 Regularization** | Yes (Embedded) | Medium | Sparse feature selection | Medium |
| **RFE** | Yes (Wrapper) | Small to medium | High accuracy needs | Very high |
| **PCA** | No (Extraction) | Large $d$ | Compression, denoising | $O(d^3)$ for eigen |
| **LDA** | Yes (Extraction) | Classification tasks | Maximizing class separation | $O(d^3)$ |
| **t-SNE** | No (Extraction) | Visualization only | 2D/3D plots | $O(n^2)$, slow |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Feature Selection and Feature Extraction with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Choosing a *subset* of the original features that are most relevant. | Creating *new* features by transforming the original feature space. |
| **Output** | A subset of the original feature set. | A completely new, lower-dimensional feature set. |
| **Interpretability** | Retains original feature meaning (high interpretability). | New features lose original meaning (low interpretability). |
| **Example** | From 20 features, selecting the 5 most correlated with target using Pearson correlation. | Applying PCA to 784 MNIST image pixels to extract 50 principal components. |
| **Computational Cost** | Generally low (filter) to high (wrapper). | Moderate to high (matrix decomposition). |

> **Key Sentence (mandatory for full 3 marks):** *"Feature Selection retains the original variables, while Feature Transformation (extraction) constructs new variables that are linear/non-linear combinations of the originals."*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**What is the Curse of Dimensionality? How does Feature Engineering help to overcome it? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The *Curse of Dimensionality*, coined by Richard Bellman, refers to the various phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces (typically $d > 10$). As dimensions increase:
1. Data points become increasingly sparse (volume grows exponentially).
2. Distances between points become less meaningful (all pairwise distances converge).
3. The number of training samples required grows exponentially to maintain density.

**How Feature Engineering helps:**
1. **Dimensionality Reduction:** PCA / LDA reduce $d$-dimensional input to $k \ll d$ dimensions.
2. **Noise Removal:** Irrelevant features that act as noise are eliminated.
3. **Computational Efficiency:** Reduced feature count → faster training and inference.
4. **Better Generalization:** Lower complexity reduces overfitting risk.

> **Key Sentence (mandatory for full 3 marks):** *"Feature Engineering reduces the effective dimensionality $d$ to a manageable $k$, allowing models to generalize better from finite training data."*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** The End Semester Examination (ESE) provides internal choice between two questions from the same module. Both alternatives below are fully solved.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the three categories of Feature Selection methods in detail. For each category, give one algorithm and its working principle. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Feature Selection methods are broadly classified into three families based on their interaction with the learning algorithm.

**(i) Filter Methods (Statistical Pre-Screening) — 2.5 Marks**

Filter methods operate as a preprocessing step, ranking features based on intrinsic statistical properties of the data, *without* invoking any machine learning algorithm.

- **Principle:** Compute a relevance score for each feature independently (or with respect to the target), then select the top-$k$.
- **Algorithm Example — Pearson Correlation Coefficient:**
  - For each feature $X_j$, compute $r(X_j, Y) = \frac{\text{Cov}(X_j, Y)}{\sigma_{X_j} \sigma_Y}$.
  - Select features with $\vert r \vert > \theta$ (typically $\theta = 0.3$).
  - **Advantage:** Very fast, model-agnostic.
  - **Disadvantage:** Ignores feature interactions.

**(ii) Wrapper Methods (Model-Based Search) — 2.5 Marks**

Wrapper methods use a specific ML model's performance as the evaluation criterion to search for the optimal feature subset.

- **Principle:** Treat feature subset selection as a combinatorial optimization problem.
- **Algorithm Example — Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE):**
  1. Train the model on all $d$ features.
  2. Rank features by model coefficients or importance.
  3. Eliminate the lowest-ranked feature.
  4. Repeat steps 1–3 on the reduced set until desired count $k$ is reached.
- **Advantage:** Considers feature interactions; gives best accuracy.
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally expensive ($O(2^d)$ in worst case).

**(iii) Embedded Methods (Integrated Regularization) — 2 Marks**

Feature selection is built into the algorithm's training objective via regularization.

- **Principle:** Add a penalty term to the loss function that discourages complex models.
- **Algorithm Example — L1 (Lasso) Regularization:**
  - Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert \beta_j \vert$
  - The L1 penalty drives coefficients $\beta_j$ of irrelevant features to **exactly zero**, achieving automatic feature selection.
- **Advantage:** Efficient, considers feature interactions.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires tuning of regularization parameter $\lambda$.

> **Concluding Statement (mandatory):** *"Filter methods are fast but coarse; wrapper methods are accurate but slow; embedded methods strike a balance and are the most used in modern ML pipelines."*

---

#### **(b) With a suitable example, derive the working of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for feature extraction. How do you decide the number of components to retain? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Problem Statement (1 Mark)**
Given an $n \times d$ data matrix $\mathbf{X}$ (centered), PCA seeks an orthogonal linear transformation that maps the data to a new coordinate system such that the greatest variance lies along the first axis (PC1), the second greatest on PC2, and so on.

**Step 2: Mathematical Formulation (2 Marks)**

We want a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ (with $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$) that maximizes the variance of the projected data $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \text{Var}(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$$

where $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is the covariance matrix.

Using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3: Solution (2 Marks)**
This is the **eigenvalue equation**. The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the **eigenvector** of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda$. The variance of the projection equals $\lambda$:

$$\text{Var}(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T (\lambda \mathbf{w}) = \lambda$$

**Step 4: Worked Example (1 Mark)**
Consider the toy 2D dataset from Section 3.2: $\mathbf{X}_c = \{(-0.5, -0.5), (0.5, 0.5), (1.5, 1.5), (-1.5, -1.5)\}$.

The covariance matrix was $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/3 & 5/3 \\ 5/3 & 5/3 \end{bmatrix}$, with eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 10/3$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$.

The first principal component is $\mathbf{w}_1 = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})^T$, capturing 100% of variance. Thus 2D data reduces to 1D without information loss.

**Step 5: Choosing the Number of Components (1 Mark)**

Three standard methods:

1. **Cumulative Variance Rule (Scree Plot):** Plot eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ in descending order. Choose $k$ such that:
$$\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95$$
i.e., retain 95% of total variance.

2. **Kaiser Criterion:** Keep all components with $\lambda_i > 1$ (when data is standardized).

3. **Elbow Method:** Plot $\lambda_i$ vs. $i$; choose the point where the curve "bends" (elbow) and levels off.

> **Concluding Statement (mandatory):** *"PCA transforms correlated features into a smaller set of uncorrelated principal components, ordered by the variance they capture, enabling efficient dimensionality reduction."*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Explain the Information Gain and Gini Index criteria used for feature selection in Decision Trees. Compute both for the given dataset. (7 Marks)**

**Dataset:** 10 instances. Target: "Buys_Computer" (Yes=6, No=4). Feature "Income" splits the data into:
- $S_{\text{High}}$ = 4 instances (Yes=4, No=0)
- $S_{\text{Medium}}$ = 4 instances (Yes=2, No=2)
- $S_{\text{Low}}$ = 2 instances (Yes=0, No=2)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Entropy of the Parent Set (1 Mark)**

$$H(S) = -\frac{6}{10} \log_2 \frac{6}{10} - \frac{4}{10} \log_2 \frac{4}{10}$$

$$H(S) = -0.6 \cdot (-0.737) - 0.4 \cdot (-1.322) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.971 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 2: Entropy of Each Child (1 Mark)**

$$H(S_{\text{High}}) = -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\frac{4}{4} - 0 = 0$$

$$H(S_{\text{Medium}}) = -\frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.000 \; \text{bits}$$

$$H(S_{\text{Low}}) = -\frac{0}{2}\log_2\frac{0}{2} - \frac{2}{2}\log_2\frac{2}{2} = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Step 3: Weighted Entropy After Split (1 Mark)**

$$H(S \mid \text{Income}) = \frac{4}{10}(0) + \frac{4}{10}(1.000) + \frac{2}{10}(0) = 0.4$$

**Step 4: Information Gain (1 Mark)**

$$IG(S, \text{Income}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Income}) = 0.971 - 0.400 = 0.571 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 5: Gini Index of the Parent Set (1 Mark)**

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2 = 1 - \left(\frac{6}{10}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{4}{10}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.36 - 0.16 = 0.48$$

**Step 6: Gini Index of Children and Weighted Gini (1 Mark)**

$$\text{Gini}(S_{\text{High}}) = 1 - 1^2 - 0^2 = 0$$

$$\text{Gini}(S_{\text{Medium}}) = 1 - (0.5)^2 - (0.5)^2 = 0.5$$

$$\text{Gini}(S_{\text{Low}}) = 1 - 0 - 1^2 = 0$$

$$\text{Gini}(S \mid \text{Income}) = \frac{4}{10}(0) + \frac{4}{10}(0.5) + \frac{2}{10}(0) = 0.2$$

**Step 7: Gini Reduction (1 Mark)**

$$\Delta \text{Gini} = \text{Gini}(S) - \text{Gini}(S \mid \text{Income}) = 0.48 - 0.20 = 0.28$$

**Step 8: Decision and Concept Summary**

Since $IG = 0.571 > 0$ and $\Delta \text{Gini} = 0.28 > 0$, the feature "Income" is highly useful for splitting and should be selected as the root node.

| Criterion | Formula | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Gain** | $IG = H(S) - H(S \mid F)$ | ID3 algorithm; biased toward high-cardinality features |
| **Gain Ratio** | $GR = \frac{IG}{H_F(S)}$ | C4.5; corrects IG bias |
| **Gini Index** | $\text{Gini} = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | CART; computationally faster than entropy |

> **Concluding Statement (mandatory):** *"Both Information Gain and Gini Index measure the reduction in impurity achieved by splitting on a feature; higher reduction means a more useful feature for classification."*

---

#### **(b) Explain the concept of Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) for feature extraction. Compare it with PCA. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Introduction to LDA (1 Mark)**

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), also known as Fisher's Discriminant Analysis, is a **supervised** dimensionality reduction technique. Unlike PCA (which is unsupervised), LDA uses class label information to find a linear projection that **maximizes class separability**.

**Step 2: Mathematical Formulation (2 Marks)**

Given $c$ classes with means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and within-class covariance matrices $\mathbf{\Sigma}_i$:

**Within-class scatter matrix:**

$$S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c} S_i \quad \text{where} \quad S_i = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T$$

**Between-class scatter matrix:**

$$S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i (\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the global mean and $n_i$ is the count of class $i$.

**Fisher's Criterion (Objective to maximize):**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$ leads to the **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w} \quad \implies \quad S_W^{-1} S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

The optimal projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$ consists of the top eigenvectors of $S_W^{-1} S_B$.

**Step 3: Dimensionality Limit (1 Mark)**

LDA can produce at most $c - 1$ discriminant components (where $c$ = number of classes), because the rank of $S_B$ is at most $c - 1$.

**Step 4: PCA vs LDA Comparison Table (2 Marks)**

| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| **Objective** | Maximize total variance | Maximize class separability |
| **Uses labels** | No | Yes |
| **Max components** | $d$ (original dimensions) | $c - 1$ (number of classes - 1) |
| **Best for** | Dimensionality reduction, denoising, compression | Classification tasks |
| **Scatter optimized** | Total scatter $S_T$ | Between-class / Within-class ratio $S_B/S_W$ |
| **Assumption** | Linear | Equal covariance matrices per class (homoscedasticity) |
| **Failure case** | When class separation is not along max-variance direction | When classes are not linearly separable |

**Step 5: Illustrative Example (1 Mark)**

Consider a 2D dataset with two well-separated classes arranged vertically (high variance along $y$-axis, separation along $x$-axis). PCA would project onto the $y$-axis (max variance) and *destroy* the class separation. LDA correctly projects onto the $x$-axis, preserving the discrimination.

> **Concluding Statement (mandatory):** *"LDA is preferred over PCA when class labels are available and the downstream task is classification, as it explicitly optimizes for class separability rather than mere variance preservation."*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in ESE OEITT721:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Feature Selection" with "Dimensionality Reduction":** Dimensionality reduction is a *broader* concept encompassing both selection and extraction. Do not use them as synonyms.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to center data before PCA:** PCA requires zero-mean data. If you compute covariance on uncentered data, your eigenvalues will be incorrect and will cost you the derivation marks.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" in algorithm explanations:** Examiners reward justification. For example, do not just write "compute eigenvalues" — add "because we need the directions of maximum variance".
> 
> 4. **Mixing up Filter vs Wrapper categorisation:** Filter methods are *independent* of any ML model; wrapper methods *require* training a model repeatedly. Writing the reverse will lose 1–2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting normalization:** When features have different scales (e.g., age in years vs. income in lakhs), PCA becomes dominated by high-variance features. Always standardize (z-score) before PCA unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning the KTU 2024 context:** ESE questions expect references to *why* a method is preferred (computational cost, dataset size, supervised/unsupervised setting). Generic textbook answers lose marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Feature Engineering):**

- **Feature Engineering** is the *bridge* between raw data and ML models; it encompasses Selection, Extraction, Transformation, and Creation of features.
- **Feature Selection** = subset of original features; **Feature Extraction** = new transformed features.
- The **three families** of feature selection are: **Filter** (statistical, fast), **Wrapper** (search-based, accurate), **Embedded** (integrated with model training).
- **Filter examples:** Pearson correlation, Chi-square, ANOVA F-test, Mutual Information, Information Gain.
- **Wrapper examples:** Forward Selection, Backward Elimination, RFE.
- **Embedded examples:** L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge), Tree-based importance.
- **PCA** is the *workhorse* unsupervised extraction method. Steps: **Center → Covariance → Eigen-decomposition → Sort → Project**.
- PCA requires choosing $k$ such that **cumulative variance retained $\geq 95\%$** (Scree plot / Elbow method).
- **LDA** is the *supervised* counterpart; it maximizes the **Fisher ratio** $J = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$.
- LDA can produce at most $c - 1$ components; PCA can produce up to $d$ components.
- **Information Gain** = $H(S) - H(S \mid F)$; **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p \log_2 p$.
- **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$; used in CART; computationally faster than entropy.
- The **Curse of Dimensionality** motivates feature engineering: as $d$ grows, data becomes sparse and distances lose meaning.
- **Always standardize** features before applying PCA to prevent high-scale features from dominating.
- KTU 2024 expects you to *justify* method choice based on: (1) supervised vs unsupervised, (2) dataset size, (3) downstream task (classification vs regression), (4) computational budget.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Models - Linear Regression and Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Models - Linear Regression and Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — LINEAR MODELS: LINEAR REGRESSION & LOGISTIC REGRESSION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Linear Models?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Linear Model** is a supervised learning algorithm that assumes a **linear relationship** between the input features (independent variables) and the target output (dependent variable). The prediction is obtained as a **linear combination of the input features** weighted by learnable parameters, optionally passed through a **link function** to model regression or classification tasks.

Mathematically, the general form of a linear model is:

$$
y = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n + \varepsilon
$$

where $w_0$ is the **bias (intercept)**, $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ are the **weights (coefficients)**, and $\varepsilon$ is the **error term**.

Two principal members of the linear model family are:

| Linear Model | Task Type | Output Range | Link Function |
|--------------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Linear Regression** | Regression (Continuous) | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | Identity |
| **Logistic Regression** | Classification (Categorical) | $[0, 1]$ (Probability) | **Sigmoid (Logistic)** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sliding Ruler" and the "S-Shaped Switch":**

Imagine you are a **furniture shop owner** trying to predict two things:
1. **How much will a custom table cost?** → The cost grows steadily with the wood length used. This is a **continuous quantity**. You simply slide a **straight ruler** along the data points — this is **Linear Regression**.
2. **Will a customer buy the table (Yes/No)?** → The decision is a probability between 0 and 1. As income rises, the probability of buying jumps from low to high in an **S-shaped curve** — this is **Logistic Regression**.

In both cases, you are drawing a "best-fit" function through the data, but the shape of the function differs based on whether you predict a **number** or a **category**.

### 1.3 Linear Regression — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Regression** is a supervised learning algorithm used to model the relationship between one or more independent variables $\mathbf{x}$ and a **continuous dependent variable** $y$ by fitting a **linear equation** to the observed data.

- **Simple Linear Regression (SLR):** Uses a single feature.
- **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR):** Uses multiple features.

The **assumptions** of classical linear regression (Gauss-Markov assumptions) are:
1. **Linearity** — Relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is linear.
2. **Independence** — Observations are independent.
3. **Homoscedasticity** — Constant variance of residuals.
4. **Normality** — Residuals are normally distributed.
5. **No Multicollinearity** — Independent variables are not highly correlated.

### 1.4 Logistic Regression — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistic Regression** is a **classification algorithm** (despite its name) that models the **probability** that an instance belongs to a particular class using the **sigmoid (logistic) function** applied to a linear combination of input features.

It is used primarily for:
- **Binary Classification** (2 classes) — *most common*
- **Multinomial Classification** (more than 2 classes)

The key insight: Logistic Regression outputs a **probability** $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \in [0, 1]$ which is then thresholded (typically at **0.5**) to obtain a class label.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Regression — Theory Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Model Formulation

For **Simple Linear Regression** with a single feature $x$:

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x
$$

where:
- $\hat{y}$ = **predicted value**
- $w_0$ = **intercept (bias)**
- $w_1$ = **slope (weight)**

For **Multiple Linear Regression** with $n$ features $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$:

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}
$$

In vectorized form (with bias absorbed into $\mathbf{w}$):

$$
\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}
$$

#### 2.1.2 Cost Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE)

The **objective** is to find weights $\mathbf{w}$ that minimize the prediction error. The standard cost function is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2
$$

where $m$ = number of training samples, and the factor $\frac{1}{2}$ simplifies the gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MSE?** The MSE is preferred because it is:
> 1. **Differentiable everywhere** (suitable for gradient descent).
> 2. **Penalizes large errors quadratically** (outliers have high impact).
> 3. **Connected to Maximum Likelihood Estimation** under Gaussian noise assumption.

#### 2.1.3 Optimization — Two Approaches

**Approach 1: Gradient Descent (Iterative)**

Update rule:

$$
w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w})}{\partial w_j}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

**Approach 2: Normal Equation (Closed-Form)**

$$
\mathbf{w} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The Normal Equation requires computing the inverse of $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$, which is **$O(n^3)$** in computational cost. For large $n$ (e.g., $n > 10{,}000$), Gradient Descent is preferred.

### 2.2 Logistic Regression — Theory Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function

The **sigmoid function** squashes any real-valued input into the range $(0, 1)$:

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

Properties:
- $\sigma(0) = 0.5$
- $\sigma(z) \to 1$ as $z \to +\infty$
- $\sigma(z) \to 0$ as $z \to -\infty$
- **Derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$

#### 2.2.2 Model Formulation

The linear combination is first computed:

$$
z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b
$$

Then the sigmoid is applied to get the probability:

$$
P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)}}
$$

The **decision rule** is:

$$
\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(z) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logit (Log-Odds) Interpretation:** The quantity $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ is called the **log-odds** or **logit** of the probability:
>
> $$\ln\!\left(\frac{P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{1 - P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$
>
> This means logistic regression is **linear in the log-odds**, not in the probability itself.

#### 2.2.3 Cost Function — Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)

MSE is **not suitable** for logistic regression (leads to non-convex cost). The proper loss is **Binary Cross-Entropy**:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This cost function is **convex** in $\mathbf{w}$, guaranteeing a global minimum. It is derived from **Maximum Likelihood Estimation** under the Bernoulli distribution assumption.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Linear Model (Vectorized)** | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ |
| 2 | **MSE Cost (Linear Reg.)** | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ | Units: (output)$^2$ |
| 3 | **Normal Equation** | $\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form, $O(n^3)$ |
| 4 | **Gradient Descent Update** | $w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| 5 | **Sigmoid Function** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Range: $(0, 1)$ |
| 6 | **Sigmoid Derivative** | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Used in backprop |
| 7 | **Logit (Log-Odds)** | $\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$ | $p = P(y=1)$ |
| 8 | **Log Loss (Logistic Reg.)** | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Convex |
| 9 | **R² Score (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum(y-\hat{y})^2}{\sum(y-\bar{y})^2}$ | Range: $(-\infty, 1]$ |
| 10 | **MAE** | $MAE = \frac{1}{m}\sum \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 11 | **RMSE** | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}$ | Same unit as $y$ |
| 12 | **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range: $[0,1]$ |
| 13 | **Precision** | $Prec = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | "How many predicted positives are correct" |
| 14 | **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $Rec = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | "How many actual positives are caught" |
| 15 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Prec \cdot Rec}{Prec + Rec}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Linear Regression:** Predicting house prices, forecasting electricity load, sensor calibration, trend analysis in IoT, financial forecasting.
- **Logistic Regression:** Spam email detection, medical diagnosis (disease/no-disease), credit card fraud detection, customer churn prediction, sentiment analysis (positive/negative).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

**Step 1 — Define the Hypothesis and Cost Function**

$$
\hat{y}^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)}
$$

$$
J(w_0, w_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Partial Derivative w.r.t. $w_1$**

We expand the squared term using the chain rule. For a single sample:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial w_1} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 = 2 \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial w_1}
$$

Since $\hat{y}^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)}$, we have $\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial w_1} = x^{(i)}$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial w_1} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 = 2 \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}
$$

Averaging over $m$ samples and applying the $\frac{1}{2m}$ factor (cancels the 2):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Partial Derivative w.r.t. $w_0$**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Write the Gradient Descent Update Rule**

$$
w_1 := w_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)}
$$

$$
w_0 := w_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Repeat Until Convergence**

The algorithm iterates until $J(w_0, w_1)$ stabilizes (change < tolerance threshold $\varepsilon$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Normal Equation from MSE Minimization

**Step 1 — Express the Cost in Matrix Form**

The MSE cost for $m$ samples with $n$ features (design matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ including bias column) is:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m} (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Quadratic Form**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} - 2 \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and Set to Zero**

Using the matrix calculus rules $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^T A \mathbf{w} = 2A\mathbf{w}$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{1}{2m} \left( 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} - 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \right) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\mathbf{w}$**

$$
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Logistic Regression Cost via Maximum Likelihood

**Step 1 — Probability Model (Bernoulli Distribution)**

For binary classification, $y \in \{0, 1\}$, the predicted probability is:

$$
P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \hat{y}, \quad P(y=0 \mid \mathbf{x}) = 1 - \hat{y}
$$

This is compactly written as:

$$
P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \hat{y}^{\,y} (1 - \hat{y})^{\,(1-y)}
$$

**Step 2 — Likelihood Function over $m$ Independent Samples**

$$
L(\mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{y}^{\,y^{(i)}} (1 - \hat{y})^{\,(1-y^{(i)})}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood (easier to maximize)**

$$
\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a Minimization Problem (Negate)**

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Gradient of Logistic Loss

**Step 1 — Compute $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ for a Single Sample**

Recall that $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$ where $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$, and $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$.

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{m} \left[ y \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}} - (1-y) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \hat{y}} \right] \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_j}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{m} \left[ \frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1 - \hat{y}} \right] \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot x_j
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the Bracket Term**

$$
\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}} = \frac{y(1-\hat{y}) - (1-y)\hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} = \frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Gradient (Beautiful Cancellation)**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{m} \left( y - \hat{y} \right) x_j
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remarkable Result:** The gradient of the logistic loss has the **same elegant form** as linear regression's gradient: it is the **prediction error** times the **input feature**. This is one of the most beautiful results in machine learning.

**Step 4 — Gradient Descent Update**

$$
\boxed{w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}}
$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Logistic Regression

**Problem:** Given data points $(x_1, x_2, y)$: $(1, 2, 1)$, $(2, 3, 0)$, $(3, 1, 1)$. Initialize $w_0 = 0, w_1 = 0.1, w_2 = -0.1$, $\alpha = 0.5$. Perform **one iteration** of gradient descent.

**Step 1 — Compute Predictions Using Sigmoid**

For sample 1: $z^{(1)} = 0 + 0.1(1) + (-0.1)(2) = -0.1$

$$
\hat{y}^{(1)} = \sigma(-0.1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.1}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.1052} = 0.4750
$$

For sample 2: $z^{(2)} = 0 + 0.1(2) + (-0.1)(3) = -0.1$

$$
\hat{y}^{(2)} = 0.4750
$$

For sample 3: $z^{(3)} = 0 + 0.1(3) + (-0.1)(1) = 0.2$

$$
\hat{y}^{(3)} = \sigma(0.2) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.2}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.8187} = 0.5498
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Errors**

$$
e^{(1)} = 0.4750 - 1 = -0.5250
$$

$$
e^{(2)} = 0.4750 - 0 = +0.4750
$$

$$
e^{(3)} = 0.5498 - 1 = -0.4502
$$

**Step 3 — Update $w_1$**

$$
\Delta w_1 = -\alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{3} e^{(i)} x_1^{(i)}
$$

$$
\Delta w_1 = -0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-0.525)(1) + (0.475)(2) + (-0.4502)(3) \right]
$$

$$
\Delta w_1 = -0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.525 + 0.950 - 1.3506 \right] = -0.5 \cdot \frac{-0.9256}{3} = +0.1543
$$

$$
w_1^{new} = 0.1 + 0.1543 = 0.2543
$$

**Step 4 — Update $w_2$**

$$
\Delta w_2 = -0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-0.525)(2) + (0.475)(3) + (-0.4502)(1) \right]
$$

$$
\Delta w_2 = -0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ -1.050 + 1.425 - 0.4502 \right] = -0.5 \cdot \frac{-0.0752}{3} = +0.0125
$$

$$
w_2^{new} = -0.1 + 0.0125 = -0.0875
$$

**Step 5 — Update $w_0$ (Bias)**

$$
\Delta w_0 = -0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.525 + 0.475 - 0.4502 \right] = -0.5 \cdot \frac{-0.5002}{3} = +0.0834
$$

$$
w_0^{new} = 0 + 0.0834 = 0.0834
$$

**Final Updated Weights:** $w_0 = 0.0834$, $w_1 = 0.2543$, $w_2 = -0.0875$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Linear Regression — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT FEATURE MATRIX X] --> B[WEIGHT VECTOR W]
    B --> C[LINEAR COMBINATION Z = W TRANSPOSE X PLUS BIAS]
    C --> D[PREDICTION Y HAT]
    D --> E[COMPUTE MSE LOSS]
    F[TARGET Y] --> E
    E --> G[GRADIENT OF LOSS WRT W]
    G --> H[UPDATE RULE W NEW = W OLD MINUS ALPHA TIMES GRADIENT]
    H --> B
    I[NORMAL EQUATION PATH] --> J[W INVERSE X TRANSPOSE X TIMES X TRANSPOSE Y]
    J --> D
```

### 4.2 Logistic Regression — Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT FEATURES X] --> B[LINEAR COMBINATION Z EQUALS W TRANSPOSE X PLUS B]
    B --> C[SIGMOID FUNCTION SIGMA Z]
    C --> D[PROBABILITY P EQUALS SIGMA Z]
    D --> E{THRESHOLD 0 POINT 5}
    E -->|P GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 0 POINT 5| F[CLASS 1]
    E -->|P LESS THAN 0 POINT 5| G[CLASS 0]
    H[TARGET Y] --> I[COMPUTE BINARY CROSS ENTROPY]
    D --> I
    I --> J[GRADIENT DESCENT UPDATE]
    J --> B
```

### 4.3 Sigmoid Function — Visual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Z EQUALS MINUS 6] --> B[SIGMA Z APPROXIMATELY 0 POINT 0024]
    C[Z EQUALS MINUS 2] --> D[SIGMA Z APPROXIMATELY 0 POINT 119]
    E[Z EQUALS 0] --> F[SIGMA Z EQUALS 0 POINT 5]
    G[Z EQUALS 2] --> H[SIGMA Z APPROXIMATELY 0 POINT 881]
    I[Z EQUALS 6] --> J[SIGMA Z APPROXIMATELY 0 POINT 9976]
```

### 4.4 Confusion Matrix — Classification Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PREDICTED CLASS] --> B[POSITIVE]
    A --> C[NEGATIVE]
    B --> D[TP TRUE POSITIVE]
    B --> E[FP FALSE POSITIVE TYPE I ERROR]
    C --> F[FN FALSE NEGATIVE TYPE II ERROR]
    C --> G[TN TRUE NEGATIVE]
    D --> H[PRECISION TP DIVIDED BY TP PLUS FP]
    D --> I[RECALL TP DIVIDED BY TP PLUS FN]
    F --> I
    G --> J[SPECIFICITY TN DIVIDED BY TN PLUS FP]
    H --> K[F1 SCORE 2 PRECISION TIMES RECALL DIVIDED BY PRECISION PLUS RECALL]
    I --> K
```

### 4.5 Complete Linear Model Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START COLLECT AND PREPROCESS DATA] --> B[EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS]
    B --> C{REGRESSION OR CLASSIFICATION}
    C -->|REGRESSION| D[LINEAR REGRESSION MODEL]
    C -->|CLASSIFICATION| E[LOGISTIC REGRESSION MODEL]
    D --> F[INITIALIZE WEIGHTS W AND BIAS B]
    E --> F
    F --> G[CHOOSE COST FUNCTION MSE OR LOG LOSS]
    G --> H[OPTIMIZE GRADIENT DESCENT OR NORMAL EQUATION]
    H --> I[VALIDATE ON VALIDATION SET]
    I --> J{PERFORMANCE SATISFACTORY}
    J -->|NO| K[TUNE HYPERPARAMETERS ALPHA DEGREE REGULARIZATION]
    K --> F
    J -->|YES| L[DEPLOY FINAL MODEL]
    L --> M[END]
```
