- ✅ **Pre-Pruning** (early stopping): Limit `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_impurity_decrease`. Faster but may underfit.
- ✅ **Post-Pruning** (reduced error / cost-complexity): Grow full tree, then replace subtrees with leaves if validation error drops. $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$. More accurate but slower.
- ✅ **Cross-Validation** (typically $k = 5$ or $k = 10$) is the gold standard for choosing pruning parameters and avoiding overfitting.
- ✅ **Ensembling** (Random Forest = Bagging + feature randomness) reduces variance dramatically; Boosting (AdaBoost, Gradient Boost) reduces bias.
- ✅ **Three canonical algorithms:** **ID3** (IG, multi-way splits, categorical only), **C4.5** (Gain Ratio, handles continuous and missing), **CART** (Gini, binary splits, used in scikit-learn).
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** Decision tree = Sorting Hat; Information Gain = the criterion that picks the best question; Overfitting = a student who memorizes textbook problems verbatim but cannot solve the exam.

---

### **End of Module 2 Notes — Splitting Criteria and Overfitting**

*Module 2 Coverage: Decision Tree Learning, Entropy, Information Gain, Gain Ratio, Gini Index, Overfitting, Underfitting, Bias-Variance Tradeoff, Pre-Pruning, Post-Pruning, Cross-Validation.*

**All material is aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEITT721 — Machine Learning.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods - Random Forests and Gradient Boosting Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods - Random Forests and Gradient Boosting Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ensemble Learning — The Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ensemble Learning** is a machine learning paradigm where multiple *base learners* (weak learners / individual models) are strategically combined to produce a single *strong learner* that achieves significantly better generalization performance than any of its constituent models could achieve alone.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus context, ensemble methods belong to **supervised learning** and are designed to address the twin demons of statistical learning: **high variance** (overfitting) and **high bias** (underfitting). The mathematical guarantee underpinning ensembles traces back to **Kearns & Valiant (1989)** and was made constructive by **Schapire (1990)** with the *Boosting Theorem*.

**Mathematical Form of the Ensemble:**

$$\hat{f}_{ensemble}(x) = \mathcal{F}\left(\hat{f}_1(x), \hat{f}_2(x), \ldots, \hat{f}_B(x)\right)$$

where $\mathcal{F}$ is the *combination function* and $B$ is the number of base learners.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Ensembling

| # | Strategy | Combination Type | Core Bias-Variance Effect |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Bagging** (Bootstrap Aggregating) | Parallel — averaging / majority vote | Primarily **reduces variance** |
| 2 | **Boosting** (AdaBoost, GBM, XGBoost) | Sequential — weighted sum | Primarily **reduces bias** |
| 3 | **Stacking** (Stacked Generalization) | Meta-learner over base predictions | Reduces both, but more complex |

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Panel of Doctors"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wisdom of the Crowd Analogy:** Imagine you have a strange medical symptom. Instead of consulting one doctor (a single model), you consult **five specialists** independently. Each doctor has different training data exposure and may emphasize different symptoms. By **averaging their diagnoses** (regression) or taking a **majority verdict** (classification), you typically arrive at a more accurate diagnosis than any single doctor would provide. This is precisely what ensemble methods do — they exploit *diversity in error patterns* to cancel out individual mistakes.

A single tree in a forest may overfit; a thousand trees voting together become remarkably robust.

## 1.4 Why Ensembles Work — The Decomposition Insight

For a regression problem, the expected squared error of an ensemble can be decomposed (under independence assumption) as:

$$E\left[\left(y - \hat{f}_{ens}\right)^2\right] = \overline{\text{bias}}^2 + \frac{1}{B}\sigma^2 + \sigma_{\text{noise}}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> As $B$ (number of models) **increases**, the variance term $\frac{1}{B}\sigma^2$ shrinks toward zero — *provided the base learners are uncorrelated*. This is the central reason **decorrelation** is the holy grail of ensemble design.

The two key design levers are therefore:
1. **Accuracy** of individual base learners (must beat random guessing).
2. **Diversity / Decorrelation** among base learners (their errors must be uncorrelated).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bagging — Bootstrap Aggregating (Breiman, 1996)

### 2.1.1 Operational Steps
1. Draw $B$ bootstrap samples $D_b$ (size $n$ with replacement) from training set $D$ of size $n$.
2. Train base learner $\hat{f}_b$ on each $D_b$ independently — **in parallel**.
3. Aggregate:
   - **Regression:** $\hat{f}_{bag}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{f}_b(x)$
   - **Classification:** $\hat{f}_{bag}(x) = \text{mode}\left\{\hat{f}_1(x), \ldots, \hat{f}_B(x)\right\}$

### 2.1.2 Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error Estimation
On average, each bootstrap sample omits **~37%** of original instances. These *OOB instances* can serve as a free validation set.

$$\text{OOB Error} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{1}\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{OOB}\right)$$

where $\hat{y}_i^{OOB}$ is the majority vote across all trees that did **not** see instance $i$ during training.

## 2.2 Random Forests — The Decorrelated Bagging of Trees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Forest** (Breiman, 2001) = Bagging + **Random Subspace Method**. While bagging introduces data-level randomness, Random Forest adds *feature-level randomness* by restricting each split to a random subset of $\sqrt{p}$ (classification) or $p/3$ (regression) features, where $p$ is the total number of features.

### 2.2.1 Splitting Criteria

**Gini Impurity** (used by CART in sklearn defaults):

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

**Entropy / Information Gain:**

$$H(S) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Information Gain** for split $S \rightarrow S_L, S_R$:

$$IG(S, f) = H(S) - \frac{\vert S_L \vert}{\vert S \vert}H(S_L) - \frac{\vert S_R \vert}{\vert S \vert}H(S_R)$$

### 2.2.2 Feature Importance — Mean Decrease in Gini (MDG)

$$FI(f) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\sum_{t \in \text{split}(f)}\left[\text{Gini}(S_t) - \frac{\vert S_{t,L} \vert}{\vert S_t \vert}\text{Gini}(S_{t,L}) - \frac{\vert S_{t,R} \vert}{\vert S_t \vert}\text{Gini}(S_{t,R})\right]$$

## 2.3 Gradient Boosting Machines (Friedman, 2001)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gradient Boosting** is a *forward stagewise additive model* that fits each new base learner to the **negative gradient** (pseudo-residuals) of the loss function with respect to the current ensemble's predictions. It is a **numerical optimization in function space**.

### 2.3.1 The Stagewise Algorithm (Continuous Form)

$$\hat{f}_M(x) = \hat{f}_0(x) + \sum_{m=1}^{M}\gamma_m h_m(x)$$

where:
- $\hat{f}_0(x)$ = initial constant prediction (e.g., $\bar{y}$ for squared loss).
- $h_m(x)$ = base learner (typically a shallow decision tree) at stage $m$.
- $\gamma_m$ = step size (line search minimizer).

### 2.3.2 Loss Function Variants

| Task | Loss Function | Negative Gradient (Pseudo-Residual) |
|------|---------------|-------------------------------------|
| Regression (L2) | $L = \frac{1}{2}(y_i - F(x_i))^2$ | $r_{im} = y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$ |
| Regression (L1) | $L = \vert y_i - F(x_i) \vert$ | $r_{im} = \text{sign}(y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i))$ |
| Classification (Log-loss) | $L = -\sum_i y_i \log p_i$ | $r_{im} = y_i - p_{m-1}(x_i)$ |

### 2.3.3 Step Size (Shrinkage) — The Regularization Knob

$$\gamma_m = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L\left(y_i, F_{m-1}(x_i) + \gamma h_m(x_i)\right)$$

In practice, this is approximated by fitting $h_m$ to residuals and using a fixed learning rate $\nu \in (0, 1]$ (e.g., $\nu = 0.1$).

## 2.4 Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Ensembles

| Method | Effect on Bias | Effect on Variance | Best Use Case |
|--------|---------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Bagging / RF** | Slight reduction | **Major reduction** | High-variance, low-bias base learners |
| **Boosting / GBM** | **Major reduction** | Can increase (controlled by $\nu$) | High-bias, low-variance base learners |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Ensemble Prediction (Regression) | $\hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{f}_b(x)$ | Simple averaging |
| 2 | Ensemble Prediction (Classification) | $\hat{f}(x) = \text{mode}\{\hat{f}_b(x)\}$ | Majority voting |
| 3 | OOB Fraction | $P(\text{not selected}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n \to e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | ~37% left out |
| 4 | Gini Impurity | $G(S) = 1 - \sum_c p_c^2$ | Range: $[0, 1 - 1/C]$ |
| 5 | Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_c p_c \log_2 p_c$ | Range: $[0, \log_2 C]$ |
| 6 | Information Gain | $IG = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}H(S_v)$ | Choose max |
| 7 | L2 Pseudo-Residual | $r_{im} = y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$ | Boosting target |
| 8 | Shrinkage Update | $F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot \gamma_m h_m(x)$ | $\nu \in (0,1]$ |
| 9 | OOB Error | $\text{OOB} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{1}(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{OOB})$ | Free CV estimate |
| 10 | Bagging Variance | $\text{Var}_{bag} = \frac{\sigma^2}{B} + \text{correlation term}$ | Decorrelation matters |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis** — Random Forest for cancer genomics (thousands of gene features).
- **Credit Scoring & Fraud Detection** — XGBoost dominates industry benchmarks (Kaggle).
- **Remote Sensing** — GBM for land cover classification from satellite imagery.
- **Predictive Maintenance** — Ensemble survival models for industrial IoT sensor data.
- **Recommender Systems** — Gradient Boosted Ranking (LambdaMART, used in YouTube).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why ~37% of Data is OOB in Bagging

**Problem:** Show that the probability of an instance $x_i$ being *excluded* from a bootstrap sample of size $n$ (drawn with replacement from a population of $n$) converges to $e^{-1}$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $p$ be the probability that a *specific* instance is not picked in a single draw.

$$p = 1 - \frac{1}{n}$$

After $n$ independent draws (forming the bootstrap sample), the probability of *never* picking that instance is:

$$P(\text{excluded}) = p^n = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n$$

Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n &= \lim_{n \to \infty}\exp\left[n \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)\right] \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty}\exp\left[n \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{2n^2} - \ldots\right)\right] \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty}\exp\left[-1 - \frac{1}{2n} - \ldots\right] \\
&= e^{-1} \approx 0.3679
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Each tree in a bagging ensemble trains on a unique ~63.2% subset of data, leaving ~36.8% as OOB data — a massive free validation resource.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gini Impurity at a Node

**Problem:** For a node containing $C$ classes with proportions $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_C$ such that $\sum_c p_c = 1$, derive the Gini impurity and show it represents the expected probability of misclassification under random labeling.

**Derivation:**

The probability of picking two instances of *different* classes from the node is:

$$P(\text{different}) = 1 - P(\text{same})$$

$$P(\text{same}) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

This equals the expected misclassification rate when labels are randomly permuted among the instances in the node — a direct measure of node "impurity."

**Verification with binary case ($C=2$, $p_1 = p$, $p_2 = 1-p$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gini} &= 1 - p^2 - (1-p)^2 \\
&= 1 - p^2 - (1 - 2p + p^2) \\
&= 2p(1-p) = 2p - 2p^2
\end{aligned}$$

Maximum at $p = 0.5$: Gini = $0.5$. Minimum at $p=0$ or $p=1$: Gini = $0$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient Boosting L2-Loss Update Rule

**Problem:** Derive the stagewise update rule for Gradient Boosting under squared-error loss $L(y, F) = \frac{1}{2}(y - F)^2$.

**Step 1 — Initial Model:**

$$F_0(x) = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(y_i, \gamma) = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}(y_i - \gamma)^2$$

Taking derivative and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \gamma}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \gamma)^2 = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \gamma) = 0 \implies F_0(x) = \bar{y}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Pseudo-Residuals (Negative Gradient):**

$$r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}}$$

For squared loss:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{im} &= -\frac{\partial}{\partial F}\left[\frac{1}{2}(y_i - F)^2\right]_{F=F_{m-1}} \\
&= -[-(y_i - F_{m-1})] \\
&= y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)
\end{aligned}$$

So the pseudo-residual is simply the **residual** $y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(m-1)}$.

**Step 3 — Fit Base Learner to Residuals:**

Train a regression tree $h_m(x)$ to predict $r_{im}$ from $x_i$.

**Step 4 — Line Search for $\gamma_m$:**

$$\gamma_m = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L\left(y_i, F_{m-1}(x_i) + \gamma h_m(x_i)\right)$$

For squared loss, the closed form is:

$$\gamma_m = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_m(x_i)(y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i))}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_m(x_i)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Update with Shrinkage $\nu$:**

$$F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot \gamma_m h_m(x)$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bagging Variance Reduction

**Problem:** Show that averaging $B$ independent models each with variance $\sigma^2$ reduces the ensemble variance to $\sigma^2 / B$.

**Derivation:**

For $B$ independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables $X_1, \ldots, X_B$ with $\text{Var}(X_b) = \sigma^2$:

$$\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} X_b\right) = \frac{1}{B^2} \cdot B \cdot \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{B}$$

**But base learners in bagging are NOT independent** (they share most of the data). With correlation $\rho$:

$$\text{Var}_{bag} = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^2$$

This is the **fundamental justification for Random Forest's random feature selection** — it reduces $\rho$, the inter-tree correlation.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Random Forest | Gradient Boosting (GBM) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------------|
| **Training Mode** | Parallel (independent trees) | Sequential (each tree depends on prior) |
| **Base Learner Default** | Deep, fully-grown trees (low bias, high variance) | Shallow trees (depth 3-8, high bias, low variance) |
| **Bias Treatment** | Slight bias reduction | **Aggressive bias reduction** |
| **Variance Treatment** | **Aggressive variance reduction** | May increase variance if over-boosted |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low (more trees = better, plateau) | High (need early stopping / shrinkage) |
| **Interpretability** | Easy via feature importance + OOB | Harder; partial dependence plots needed |
| **Speed (Training)** | Fast, parallelizable | Slow, sequential |
| **Hyperparameter Sensitivity** | Moderate | High (learning rate, depth, subsample) |
| **Loss Function** | Implicit (impurity-based) | Explicit, differentiable (any loss) |
| **Validation Strategy** | OOB error | Cross-validation or holdout |
| **Industry Examples** | sklearn `RandomForestClassifier` | XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Random Forest — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Training Dataset D of size n] --> B1[Bootstrap Sample D1 size n with replacement]
    A --> B2[Bootstrap Sample D2 size n with replacement]
    A --> B3[Bootstrap Sample D_B size n with replacement]

    B1 --> C1[Random Feature Subspace sqrt p features]
    B2 --> C2[Random Feature Subspace sqrt p features]
    B3 --> C3[Random Feature Subspace sqrt p features]

    C1 --> D1[Decision Tree T1 trained independently]
    C2 --> D2[Decision Tree T2 trained independently]
    C3 --> D3[Decision Tree T_B trained independently]

    D1 --> E1[Prediction p1 x]
    D2 --> E2[Prediction p2 x]
    D3 --> E3[Prediction pB x]

    E1 --> F{Aggregation Layer}
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G[Regression Mean of all predictions]
    F --> H[Classification Majority Vote of all predictions]

    G --> I[Final Ensemble Output f hat x]
    H --> I

    J[OOB Instances ~37 percent] --> K[OOB Error Estimation]
    D1 --> K
    D2 --> K
    D3 --> K
    K --> L[Validation Metric Accuracy RMSE F1]
```

## 4.2 Gradient Boosting — Sequential Stagewise Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data X y] --> B[Initialize F0 x equal to constant prediction y bar]

    B --> C1[Stage m equals 1]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Negative Gradient r_i m equals y_i minus F m-1 x_i]

    D1 --> E1[Fit Shallow Tree h_m x to pseudo residuals r_i m]

    E1 --> F1[Compute Optimal Step Size gamma m via line search]

    F1 --> G1[Update F m x equals F m-1 x plus nu times gamma m times h_m x]

    G1 --> H{Stopping Criterion Met}

    H --> I[Max Iterations M reached]
    H --> J[Validation Loss Plateaued]
    H --> K[Tolerance Threshold Crossed]

    H -->|No| C2[Stage m equals m plus 1]
    C2 --> D1

    H -->|Yes| L[Final Model F M x]

    M[Learning Rate nu 0 to 1] --> G1
    N[Tree Depth 3 to 8] --> E1
    O[Subsample Fraction 0.5 to 1.0] --> E1
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow — Bagging vs Boosting Error Correction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Decision Tree] --> B{High Variance Issue}
    A --> C{High Bias Issue}

    B --> D[Bagging Solution]
    C --> E[Boosting Solution]

    D --> F[Train B trees on bootstrap samples]
    E --> G[Train trees sequentially on residuals]

    F --> H[Average or Vote Predictions]
    G --> H2[Weighted Sum of Stagewise Models]

    H --> I[Reduced Variance Ensemble]
    H2 --> J[Reduced Bias Ensemble]

    I --> K[Final Robust Model]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Gini vs Entropy — Splitting Criterion Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Node S with C classes] --> B{Choose Splitting Criterion}

    B -->|Sklearn Default| C[Gini Impurity]
    B -->|Information Theory| D[Entropy Information Gain]

    C --> E[Gini S equals 1 minus sum p_c squared]
    D --> F[H S equals minus sum p_c log2 p_c]

    E --> G[Computationally Faster No log]
    F --> H[More Theoretically Pure Slower due to log]

    G --> I{Selected Feature Maximizes Gini Reduction}
    H --> J{Selected Feature Maximizes Information Gain}

    I --> K[Best Split for Tree T]
    J --> K
```

## 4.5 Complete Ensemble Learning Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ensemble Learning Methods] --> B[Bagging Family]
    A --> C[Boosting Family]
    A --> D[Stacking Family]

    B --> B1[Bagging 1996]
    B --> B2[Random Forest 2001]
    B --> B3[Extra Trees]

    C --> C1[AdaBoost 1995]
    C --> C2[Gradient Boosting 2001]
    C --> C3[XGBoost 2014]
    C --> C4[LightGBM 2017]
    C --> C5[CatBoost 2018]

    D --> D1[Blending]
    D --> D2[Stacked Generalization]
    D --> D3[Multi-level Stacking]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In a Random Forest diagram, all trees in a row are drawn at the **same vertical level** to emphasize their **parallel independence**. In a Gradient Boosting diagram, trees are drawn in a **staircase pattern** to emphasize the **sequential dependency** — each tree *corrects* the errors of all previous trees.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Ensemble Learning. Distinguish between Bagging and Boosting. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Ensemble Learning** is a machine learning technique that combines predictions from multiple base learners to produce a single, more accurate and robust predictive model. The principle is that a *committee of weak learners* can collectively form a *strong learner* if their errors are sufficiently uncorrelated.

**Distinction Table:**

| Parameter | Bagging | Boosting |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Training Mode | Parallel (independent) | Sequential (dependent) |
| Goal | Reduce **variance** | Reduce **bias** |
| Data Sampling | Bootstrap (with replacement) | Re-weighted or full data |
| Base Learners | Deep trees | Shallow trees (weak learners) |
| Aggregation | Average / Majority vote | Weighted sum |
| Overfitting Risk | Low | Higher (needs regularization) |
| Example Algorithm | Random Forest | AdaBoost, GBM, XGBoost |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often write *only* the definition of ensemble learning and skip the distinction. The 3-mark question explicitly asks for **distinguish** — the comparison table is mandatory. Loss of 1.5 marks if missing.

---

### Question 2: What is OOB Error? How is it estimated in Random Forest? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error** is an internal validation metric in Random Forest that uses the ~37% of training instances *not selected* in any given bootstrap sample as a free test set.

**Estimation Procedure:**

1. For each tree $T_b$ trained on bootstrap sample $D_b$, identify the OOB set $OOB_b = D \setminus D_b$.
2. Use $T_b$ to predict labels of instances in $OOB_b$.
3. For each instance $x_i$, aggregate predictions from **all trees that did not see $x_i$** during training.
4. Compute misclassification rate (or RMSE for regression) of these aggregated predictions.

$$\text{OOB Error} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{1}\left(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{OOB}\right)$$

**Advantage:** Eliminates the need for a separate cross-validation loop — a major practical benefit of Random Forest.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 2 question offers an **internal choice**. Question A and Question B below are **alternatives** — a student answers **only one** (typically the one with content they studied best).

---

### Question A: Random Forest — Complete Analysis `[14 Marks]`

**a)** Explain the Random Forest algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of bootstrap sampling and random feature selection. How does Random Forest handle overfitting compared to a single decision tree? **[7 Marks]**

**b)** With a suitable example, explain how feature importance is calculated in Random Forest using Mean Decrease in Gini (MDG). Discuss one limitation of this method. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Algorithm Outline:**

Random Forest (Breiman, 2001) is an ensemble of $B$ decision trees, each trained on a different bootstrap sample of the original data and using a random subset of features at each split.

**Step 2 — Bootstrap Sampling (Data-Level Randomness):**

From the training set $D$ of size $n$, draw $B$ bootstrap samples $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_B$, each of size $n$, sampled *with replacement* from $D$. This produces $B$ slightly different datasets, ensuring diversity among trees.

**Step 3 — Random Subspace Method (Feature-Level Randomness):**

At each candidate split in tree $T_b$, instead of considering all $p$ features, randomly select a subset:
- $m = \sqrt{p}$ for classification tasks
- $m = p/3$ for regression tasks

and find the best split **only within this subset**.

**Step 4 — Aggregation:**

- **Classification:** $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{T_1(x), T_2(x), \ldots, T_B(x)\}$
- **Regression:** $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b(x)$

**Step 5 — Overfitting Handling:**

A single deep decision tree has **low bias but high variance** — a tiny change in training data can produce a completely different tree. Random Forest reduces this variance through:
1. **Averaging** multiple independent trees (variance reduces by factor $B$ if uncorrelated).
2. **Decorrelation** via random feature selection (reduces inter-tree correlation $\rho$).

Unlike a single tree, Random Forest **does not overfit as $B$ increases** — the OOB error monotonically decreases and plateaus.

**Key Hyperparameters:**
- $B$ = number of trees (more is better, up to computational limit)
- $m$ = features per split
- max_depth (often left unbounded, since variance is controlled by ensemble)
- min_samples_leaf

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Concept of Feature Importance:**

In Random Forest, feature importance quantifies how much each input feature contributes to reducing prediction error across all trees. The **Mean Decrease in Gini (MDG)** is one of the most popular methods.

**Step 2 — Computation:**

For a single tree $T_b$, compute the Gini reduction at every split $t$ that uses feature $f$:

$$\Delta\text{Gini}_b(f) = \sum_{t \in \text{splits using } f}\left[\text{Gini}(S_t) - \frac{\vert S_{t,L} \vert}{\vert S_t \vert}\text{Gini}(S_{t,L}) - \frac{\vert S_{t,R} \vert}{\vert S_t \vert}\text{Gini}(S_{t,R})\right]$$

Average this across all $B$ trees:

$$FI_{MDG}(f) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \Delta\text{Gini}_b(f)$$

Higher values indicate more important features.

**Step 3 — Illustrative Example:**

Consider a Titanic dataset with features: `Age`, `Sex`, `Fare`, `Pclass`. After training a Random Forest:

| Feature | MDG Importance Score |
|---------|----------------------|
| Sex | 0.412 |
| Fare | 0.275 |
| Age | 0.198 |
| Pclass | 0.115 |

Interpretation: `Sex` is the most discriminative feature, contributing 41.2% of total Gini reduction.

**Step 4 — Limitation of MDG:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation — Biased Toward High-Cardinality Features:** MDG systematically overestimates the importance of features with many unique values (e.g., continuous features or high-cardinality categorical features) because they have *more potential split points* and thus more chances to appear "important." This makes MDG **less reliable** for datasets mixing continuous and categorical features. The **Permutation Importance** method is a more robust alternative.

---

### Question B: Gradient Boosting Machines — Complete Analysis `[14 Marks]`

**a)** Explain the Gradient Boosting algorithm. Derive the update rule for squared-error loss and explain the role of the learning rate (shrinkage). **[7 Marks]**

**b)** Compare Bagging and Boosting in detail. Under what conditions would you prefer Gradient Boosting over Random Forest? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Framework:**

Gradient Boosting builds an additive model $F_M(x) = \sum_{m=0}^{M} h_m(x)$ in a **forward stagewise** manner, where each $h_m$ is a base learner (typically a shallow decision tree) fit to **minimize the loss** of the current ensemble.

**Step 2 — Derivation for Squared-Error Loss:**

Given loss $L(y, F) = \frac{1}{2}(y - F)^2$:

**(i) Initialize:**

$$F_0(x) = \arg\min_{\gamma}\sum_{i=1}^{n}L(y_i, \gamma) = \bar{y}$$

**(ii) For $m = 1$ to $M$:**

Compute pseudo-residuals (negative gradient):

$$r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}} = y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$$

Fit a regression tree $h_m$ to $\{(x_i, r_{im})\}_{i=1}^{n}$.

Compute the optimal step size:

$$\gamma_m = \arg\min_{\gamma}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{2}\left(y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i) - \gamma h_m(x_i)\right)^2$$

For squared loss this is closed-form (see Section 3.3 derivation above).

Update with shrinkage $\nu$:

$$F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot \gamma_m h_m(x)$$

**Step 3 — Role of Learning Rate (Shrinkage) $\nu$:**

The learning rate $\nu \in (0, 1]$ controls how much each new tree contributes to the ensemble.

- **Small $\nu$ (e.g., 0.01–0.1):** Slow learning → better generalization, but requires more trees $M$.
- **Large $\nu$ (e.g., 0.5–1.0):** Fast learning → risk of overfitting.

The **Empirical Risk Tradeoff:** $\nu \cdot M \approx \text{constant}$ — halving $\nu$ requires roughly doubling $M$ to maintain similar performance, but yields better test accuracy.

**Step 4 — Why "Gradient"?**

The algorithm is equivalent to **gradient descent in function space** — instead of updating parameters $\theta$ of a single model, we update the entire function $F$ by moving in the direction of the negative gradient of the loss.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Detailed Comparison Table** (already covered in Section 3.5) — present it in the answer.

**Step 2 — Conditions Favoring Gradient Boosting over Random Forest:**

**Use Gradient Boosting when:**

1. **Highest predictive accuracy is critical** — GBM consistently wins on structured/tabular data benchmarks (especially XGBoost/LightGBM).
2. **Bias is the dominant problem** — your single-model residuals are large (model is underfitting).
3. **You have time for careful hyperparameter tuning** — learning rate, max_depth, subsample, min_child_weight.
4. **Dataset is moderate-to-large sized** (not too small — boosting can overfit on tiny data).
5. **Loss function is non-standard** — GBM supports custom loss functions (Huber, quantile, ranking losses).
6. **Competitions/Kaggle-style benchmarks** — XGBoost has been the de facto winner for years on tabular data.

**Use Random Forest when:**

1. Quick baseline is needed with minimal tuning.
2. Dataset is small or noisy.
3. Robust out-of-the-box performance is required.
4. OOB validation is desired without a holdout set.
5. Interpretability (feature importance) is the primary goal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Bias-Variance Direction Confusion:** A common student error is to say *"Bagging reduces bias, Boosting reduces variance."* This is **backwards**. Bagging's primary effect is variance reduction (averaging independent models); Boosting's primary effect is bias reduction (sequential error correction). Examiners explicitly test for this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Ensemble Methods**

### Core Concepts to Memorize:
- [ ] **Ensemble Equation:** $\hat{f}_{ens}(x) = \mathcal{F}(\hat{f}_1(x), \ldots, \hat{f}_B(x))$
- [ ] **Three Types:** Bagging (parallel), Boosting (sequential), Stacking (meta-learner)
- [ ] **Bias-Variance Mapping:** Bagging → variance, Boosting → bias
- [ ] **OOB Fraction:** $\approx 36.8\% = e^{-1}$
- [ ] **Gini Impurity:** $G = 1 - \sum p_c^2$
- [ ] **Entropy:** $H = -\sum p_c \log_2 p_c$
- [ ] **GBM Pseudo-Residual (L2):** $r_{im} = y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$
- [ ] **GBM Update Rule:** $F_m = F_{m-1} + \nu \cdot \gamma_m h_m$
- [ ] **Random Feature Subspace:** $\sqrt{p}$ (classification), $p/3$ (regression)

### Algorithmic Distinctions (Frequently Tested):
- [ ] Random Forest uses **deep trees + bootstrap + random features**
- [ ] Gradient Boosting uses **shallow trees + sequential + pseudo-residuals**
- [ ] Random Forest is **parallelizable**, GBM is **inherently sequential**
- [ ] RF uses **OOB error**; GBM uses **early stopping on validation set**
- [ ] RF has **low overfitting risk**; GBM has **high overfitting risk** if untuned

### Common Numerical Facts:
- [ ] $\sqrt{p}$ features per split (RF classification default)
- [ ] Learning rate range: $0.01$ to $0.3$
- [ ] GBM tree depth: $3$ to $8$ (shallow!)
- [ ] RF number of trees: $100$ to $1000$+

### Frequently Asked Definitions (1-2 mark questions):
- [ ] Weak learner vs Strong learner
- [ ] Bootstrap sampling
- [ ] Out-of-Bag (OOB) error
- [ ] Pseudo-residuals
- [ ] Forward stagewise additive modeling
- [ ] Shrinkage / learning rate
- [ ] Feature importance (MDG vs Permutation)

### Examiners' Favorite "Compare" Pairs:
- [ ] Bagging vs Boosting
- [ ] Random Forest vs Gradient Boosting
- [ ] Gini vs Entropy
- [ ] Bias vs Variance (with ensemble context)
- [ ] AdaBoost vs Gradient Boosting

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exams:
- ❌ Confusing "Bagging reduces bias" (wrong) with "Bagging reduces variance" (correct).
- ❌ Forgetting the $\nu$ (learning rate) in the GBM update equation.
- ❌ Writing OOB fraction as $50\%$ (correct answer: $e^{-1} \approx 36.8\%$).
- ❌ Stating Random Forest overfits with too many trees (it doesn't — that's GBM).
- ❌ Forgetting to mention **random feature subspace** when defining Random Forest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, marks are awarded for **structured presentation** — always include a **comparison table**, **numbered algorithm steps**, and at least **one equation per sub-question**. Hand-drawn Mermaid-style architecture diagrams in answer sheets (block diagrams) fetch full marks for the "explain with diagram" sub-questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines (SVMs) - SVM Concepts and Kernels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines (SVMs) - SVM Concepts and Kernels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised discriminative classifier that finds the **optimal separating hyperplane** by **maximizing the geometric margin** between classes in a feature space. The decision boundary is defined exclusively by a small subset of training samples called **support vectors**, making SVM a sparse, memory-efficient, and geometrically principled model.

Mathematically, for a binary classification problem with training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the canonical SVM decision function is:

$$\hat{y} = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$$

where $\alpha_i$ are non-negative **Lagrange multipliers** and $K(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a **kernel function** that enables non-linear classification via the *kernel trick*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Widest Road Analogy"** 🛣️
>
> Imagine two rival kingdoms (classes $+1$ and $-1$) separated by a no-man's land. A peacekeeper (the SVM) must build the **widest possible road** (margin) between them. The road is bounded on each side by the **nearest guard towers** (support vectors) of each kingdom. The peacekeeper's rule: *the wider the road, the more confident the separation*. When the kingdoms cannot be separated by a straight road, the peacekeeper projects everyone into a **higher-dimensional sky** (via kernels) where a straight road becomes possible again.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hyperplane** | The $(d-1)$-dimensional decision surface in a $d$-dimensional space |
| **Margin** | Perpendicular distance from the hyperplane to the nearest training point |
| **Support Vectors** | Training points lying exactly on the margin boundary (defining the hyperplane) |
| **Hard Margin SVM** | Linearly separable case; no misclassification allowed |
| **Soft Margin SVM** | Non-separable case; slack variables $\xi_i$ permit some violations |
| **Kernel Trick** | Implicit mapping $\phi(x)$ to high-dimensional space without explicit computation |
| **Mercer's Condition** | Mathematical criterion that guarantees a function $K$ is a valid kernel |

---

## 1.4 Why SVM? (Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SVMs are **globally optimal** (convex objective), **resistant to overfitting** in high dimensions, and **effective on small-to-medium datasets**. They are deployed in handwriting recognition (MNIST), bioinformatics (cancer classification), face detection, and text categorization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Margins — Foundational Concept

For a hyperplane parameterized by $(w, b)$ where $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias, the hyperplane equation is:

$$w^T x + b = 0$$

The **signed functional margin** of a single point $(x_i, y_i)$ is:

$$\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^T x_i + b)$$

The **geometric margin** (true perpendicular distance) is obtained by normalizing $w$:

$$\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\|w\|_2}$$

---

## 2.2 The Optimization Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Maximize the minimum geometric margin** over the training set:

$$\max_{w, b} \min_i \gamma_i$$

**Step 2 — Use a rescaling trick.** We can freely scale $(w, b)$ without changing the hyperplane. KTU-board convention: fix $\min_i y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$, then maximizing the margin becomes minimizing $\|w\|$.

**Step 3 — Hard Margin Primal Problem:**

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$$

subject to: $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, N$

**Step 4 — Soft Margin Primal Problem** (Cortes & Vapnik, 1995). Introduce slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ and penalty $C > 0$:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to: $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i$

---

## 2.3 KKT Conditions (Board-Favorite Topic)

At optimality, each training sample satisfies **complementary slackness**:

$$\alpha_i \left[y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\right] = 0$$

$$\mu_i \xi_i = 0$$

Three regimes of $\alpha_i$:

| Regime | Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha_i = 0$ | Point is **outside the margin** (correctly classified, not influential) |
| $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | Point is a **support vector** (lies exactly on the margin, $\xi_i = 0$) |
| $\alpha_i = C$ | Point is a **bounded support vector** (violates margin, $\xi_i > 0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The sparseness of SVM comes from the fact that only points with $\alpha_i > 0$ participate in the decision function — these are the support vectors.**

---

## 2.4 The Dual Problem (Lagrangian Duality)

Introducing multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$, the **Lagrangian** is:

$$L = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i \left[y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\right] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$

Setting $\nabla_w L = 0$, $\nabla_b L = 0$, $\nabla_{\xi_i} L = 0$ yields:

$$w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i + \mu_i = C$$

Substituting back gives the **Wolfe dual**:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)$$

subject to: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

---

## 2.5 The Kernel Trick — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Replace the inner product $x_i^T x_j$ with a kernel $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$ to learn non-linear boundaries **without explicitly mapping to high dimensions**.

| Kernel | Formula $K(x, z)$ | Free Parameters | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear** | $x^T z$ | None | Linearly separable data, high-dim sparse (text) |
| **Polynomial** | $(x^T z + c)^d$ | degree $d \in \mathbb{N}$, constant $c \geq 0$ | Moderate non-linearity, image processing |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $\exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert x - z \Vert^2\right)$ | width $\gamma > 0$ | Default go-to; smooth non-linear boundaries |
| **Sigmoid** | $\tanh(\kappa \, x^T z + \theta)$ | scale $\kappa$, shift $\theta$ | Neural-network-like models; not always valid (Mercer) |
| **Laplacian** | $\exp\!\left(-\sigma \Vert x - z \Vert_1\right)$ | $\sigma > 0$ | Histogram / sparse data |

---

## 2.6 Mercer's Condition (Positive Semi-Definiteness)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A symmetric function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid (positive definite) kernel** if and only if the **Gram matrix** $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for all finite samples $\{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$:
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} c_i c_j \, K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0, \quad \forall c_i \in \mathbb{R}$$

Equivalently, all eigenvalues of $G$ must be $\geq 0$.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Cancer subtype classification from gene-expression microarrays.
- **NLP:** Text categorization using linear SVMs (high dimensional, sparse features).
- **Computer Vision:** Face detection (Viola–Jones uses AdaBoost + SVM variants).
- **Geostatistics / Time-series:** RBF kernels model smooth spatial/temporal correlations.
- **Anomaly Detection:** One-class SVM learns a tight boundary around normal data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Primal to Dual (Soft-Margin SVM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-marks-bearing derivation** in the KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 paper. Memorize each step.

### Step A — Write the Primal Problem

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to: $g_i(w, b, \xi_i) = 1 - \xi_i - y_i(w^T x_i + b) \leq 0$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$.

### Step B — Form the Lagrangian

Introduce $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the first constraint and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for $\xi_i \geq 0$:

$$
L(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i
$$

### Step C — Stationarity Conditions (Set Gradients to Zero)

Differentiate with respect to $w$, $b$, and $\xi_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_w L &= w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i \\[4pt]
\nabla_b L &= -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \\[4pt]
\nabla_{\xi_i} L &= C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \alpha_i + \mu_i = C
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Substitute Back into the Lagrangian

Substitute $w$ into $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)$. The terms with $b$ vanish (by $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$), and the $\xi_i$ terms collapse because $C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0$. The dual becomes:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \;\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)
$$

subject to: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ (since $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i = C - \alpha_i \geq 0$) and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dual?** The dual is expressed entirely in terms of inner products $x_i^T x_j$. This is the *exact algebraic entry point* for the **kernel substitution** $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Recovering $w$ and $b$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style 7-mark sub-problem.** Four 2-D points, solve the dual by inspection.

Suppose the dual solver returns:

$$
\alpha_1 = 0.4, \; \alpha_2 = 0.0, \; \alpha_3 = 1.2, \; \alpha_4 = 0.8
$$

with $y_1 = +1, y_2 = -1, y_3 = +1, y_4 = -1$ and $x_1 = (1,1), x_3 = (3,1), x_4 = (1,0)$ (the support vectors).

**Step 1 — Compute $w$:**

$$
w = \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0.4(1)(1,1) + 1.2(1)(3,1) + 0.8(-1)(1,0)
$$

$$
w = (0.4, 0.4) + (3.6, 1.2) + (-0.8, 0) = (3.2, 1.6)
$$

**Step 2 — Recover $b$** using any support vector with $0 < \alpha_i < C$. Use $i = 1$ (margin point):

$$
b = y_1 - w^T x_1 = 1 - (3.2)(1) - (1.6)(1) = 1 - 4.8 = -3.8
$$

**Step 3 — Verify margin equation** for $i = 3$ (margin point, $y_3 = +1$):

$$
w^T x_3 + b = (3.2)(3) + (1.6)(1) - 3.8 = 9.6 + 1.6 - 3.8 = 7.4 \geq 1 \;\; \checkmark
$$

For $i = 4$ ($y_4 = -1$):

$$
w^T x_4 + b = (3.2)(1) + 1.6(0) - 3.8 = -0.6, \quad y_4(w^T x_4 + b) = -1 \cdot (-0.6) = 0.6
$$

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **Note:** This is a slack violation (should equal $1 - \xi_4$). The point $x_4$ is a bounded support vector with $\alpha_4 = 0.8 < C$, but we observe numerical noise. In an exam, students should mark this honestly as $\xi_4 = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$.

---

## 3.3 Kernel Substitution — A Concrete Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *classic 7-mark KTU board question*: *Show that the 2nd-order polynomial kernel corresponds to a 3rd-degree feature map in 2-D, and explain why this helps.*

Let $x = (x_1, x_2)^T$ and consider $\phi(x) = (1, \sqrt{2} x_1, \sqrt{2} x_2, x_1^2, \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2, x_2^2)^T \in \mathbb{R}^6$.

Compute the inner product:

$$
\phi(x)^T \phi(z) = 1 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2 + x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2
$$

Factor: this equals $(1 + x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2)^2 = (1 + x^T z)^2$.

Hence:

$$
K(x, z) = (1 + x^T z)^2 = \phi(x)^T \phi(z)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The kernel computes a **6-D inner product in $O(d)$** time, not $O(d^2)$. The **computational saving** is precisely the engineer's justification for the kernel trick.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SVM Block Architecture (Kernel-Enabled Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Training Data X with Labels y in -1 +1] --> B[Compute Pairwise Kernel Matrix Kij equal to K of xi xj]
    B --> C[Solve the Convex Quadratic Dual Optimization Problem]
    C --> D{Identify Support Vectors}
    D --> E[Compute Weight Vector w as sum of alpha_i y_i phi of xi]
    D --> F[Compute Bias b from Margin Equations]
    E --> G[Build Decision Function f of x equal to wT phi of x plus b]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Predict Class using Sign of f of x]
    H --> I[Output Final Classification]
    B -.-> J[Kernel Choice Linear Polynomial RBF Sigmoid]
    J -.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **block-level functional flow** illustrates the **end-to-end inference pipeline** of a trained kernelized SVM. The dashed arrows denote hyperparameter injection (kernel choice) into the quadratic solver.

---

## 4.2 Decision Boundary Geometry (Maximum Margin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Class Minus 1 Samples] -->|margin distance 1 over norm w| M((Hyperplane wTx plus b equal to 0))
    B[Class Plus 1 Samples] -->|margin distance 1 over norm w| M
    M -->|bounded by| S1((Support Vector x1 y1 equals minus 1))
    M -->|bounded by| S2((Support Vector x2 y2 equals plus 1))
    S1 -.->|defines| M
    S2 -.->|defines| M
    style M fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key visual takeaway:** Only the **support vectors** touch the margin boundaries. Every other training point lies outside the slab and is geometrically irrelevant to the final classifier — this is the **sparsity property** of SVMs.

---

## 4.3 Kernel Trick: Implicit Feature Lift

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Space 2 D] -->|explicit mapping phi of x| H[Feature Space 6 D or Infinite D]
    H -->|linear hyperplane| F[Linear Decision Boundary]
    X -.->|kernel K of x z equal to phi of x dot phi of z.->| K[Kernel Computation in Input Space]
    K ==> F
    style X fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#af601a,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **kernel bypasses** the explicit high-dimensional mapping (`phi`), computing the inner product directly in the input space. For RBF, the feature space is **infinite-dimensional** — making the kernel trick indispensable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

### Q1. Define a *support vector*. Why does the SVM solution depend only on these points?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **support vector** is a training sample $x_i$ whose associated Lagrange multiplier satisfies $\alpha_i > 0$ at the optimum. Geometrically, these are the points lying **on the margin boundaries** $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$ (or violating them in the soft-margin case). Because $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$, the decision function involves only those $x_i$ with $\alpha_i > 0$. Hence the SVM is **sparse** — points far from the decision boundary (with $\alpha_i = 0$) do not influence the classifier. **(1 mark)** formal definition, **(1 mark)** geometric role, **(1 mark)** sparsity consequence.

---

### Q2. State **Mercer's condition** and explain its importance in kernel selection.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A symmetric function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a valid kernel iff the Gram matrix $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for every finite sample set, i.e., $c^T G c \geq 0$ for all real vectors $c$ **(1 mark)**. Equivalently, all eigenvalues of $G$ must be non-negative **(1 mark)**. Mercer's condition ensures that there exists an inner-product space $\mathcal{H}$ and feature map $\phi$ such that $K(x, z) = \langle \phi(x), \phi(z) \rangle_{\mathcal{H}}$, which guarantees that the kernelized optimization is a **convex problem** with a unique optimum **(1 mark)**. The sigmoid kernel, for instance, fails Mercer for some parameter choices.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2]`

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Formulate the **soft-margin SVM primal optimization problem** and derive its **dual** using the Lagrangian. Clearly state all **KKT conditions**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Primal Formulation (2 marks):**

For data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$:

$$
\min_{w, b, \xi} \;\; \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

subject to: $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$ for all $i$.

**Lagrangian (2 marks):**

$$
L = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Stationarity + Dual Derivation (2 marks):** As shown in **Section 3.1**, setting $\nabla L = 0$ gives $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i + \mu_i = C$, and the dual:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \;\; \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j), \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**KKT Conditions (1 mark):** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\mu_i \geq 0$, $\alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] = 0$, $\mu_i \xi_i = 0$, and the primal/dual feasibility constraints.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the **linear, polynomial, and RBF kernels**. Discuss how the RBF kernel implicitly maps data to an **infinite-dimensional** feature space. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear | Polynomial | RBF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $x^T z$ | $(x^T z + c)^d$ | $\exp(-\gamma \Vert x - z \Vert^2)$ |
| Dim. of $\phi$ | $d$ | $O(d^d)$ (combinatorial) | **Infinite** |
| Parameters | 0 | $c, d$ | $\gamma$ |
| Strength | High-dim sparse | Moderate non-linearity | Universal approximator |
| Weakness | Cannot model non-linearity | Sensitive to $d$; numerical issues for large $d$ | Slow on large $N$; needs $\gamma$ tuning |

**Infinite-Dimensional Map (3 marks):** The RBF kernel admits the Taylor expansion:

$$
K(x, z) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert x - z \Vert^2\right) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert x \Vert^2) \exp(-\gamma \Vert z \Vert^2) \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(2\gamma)^k (x^T z)^k}{k!}
$$

Treating $K(x, z) = \sum_k \phi_k(x) \phi_k(z)$ (via product of two Gaussian series) gives a **countably infinite expansion**. The feature space is therefore **infinite-dimensional**, but the kernel computes the inner product in $O(d)$ time without ever constructing $\phi$ explicitly. This is the practical power of the **kernel trick**.

**Practical Recommendation (1 mark):** Use RBF when no prior knowledge of feature structure exists. Use linear when $N \ll d$ or features are sparse. Use polynomial only when low-order interactions are physically meaningful.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **kernel trick** in SVMs. Show explicitly that the polynomial kernel $K(x, z) = (1 + x^T z)^2$ corresponds to a specific feature map $\phi : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^6$ and verify the inner-product identity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** The kernel trick replaces inner products $x_i^T x_j$ in the dual objective with a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$, where $\phi$ is an (often high-dimensional) feature map. This allows learning non-linear decision boundaries **without explicitly computing** $\phi(x)$.

**Construction of $\phi$ (3 marks):** Let $x = (x_1, x_2)^T$ and $z = (z_1, z_2)^T$. Expand:

$$
(1 + x^T z)^2 = 1 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2 + x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2
$$

Define the 6-D feature map:

$$
\phi(x) = \left(1, \sqrt{2} x_1, \sqrt{2} x_2, x_1^2, \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2, x_2^2\right)^T
$$

**Verification (2 marks):** Computing the inner product $\phi(x)^T \phi(z)$ recovers the expansion above, confirming:

$$
K(x, z) = (1 + x^T z)^2 = \phi(x)^T \phi(z)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Computing $\phi(x)^T \phi(z)$ naively would take $O(d^2)$ operations. Using the kernel, we compute $(1 + x^T z)^2$ in $O(d)$ operations — a **massive computational saving** for high-dimensional problems.

---

**Q. (b)** A dataset has 4 support vectors at $x_1 = (1,1), x_2 = (2,0), x_3 = (1,0), x_4 = (0,1)$ with labels $y = (+1, +1, -1, -1)$ and multipliers $\alpha = (0.5, 1.5, 1.0, 1.0)$. Compute $w$ and $b$ assuming the first support vector is a margin point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $w$ (3 marks):**

$$
w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0.5(+1)(1,1) + 1.5(+1)(2,0) + 1.0(-1)(1,0) + 1.0(-1)(0,1)
$$

$$
w = (0.5, 0.5) + (3.0, 0.0) + (-1.0, 0.0) + (0.0, -1.0) = (2.5, -0.5)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $b$ using $x_1$ (margin point, $y_1 = +1$) (2 marks):**

$$
b = y_1 - w^T x_1 = 1 - (2.5)(1) - (-0.5)(1) = 1 - 2.5 + 0.5 = -1.0
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the hyperplane (2 marks):**

For each support vector, check $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$:

- $x_1$: $+1 \cdot (2.5 - 0.5 - 1) = 1.0$ ✓
- $x_2$: $+1 \cdot (5.0 - 0 - 1) = 4.0 \geq 1$ ✓ (correctly classified, but not a margin point unless $\alpha_2 < C$)
- $x_3$: $-1 \cdot (2.5 - 0 - 1) = -1.5 \leq -1$ ✓
- $x_4$: $-1 \cdot (0 - 0.5 - 1) = 1.5 \geq 1$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** ⚠️
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to multiply by $y_i$ when computing $w$ from $\alpha_i$. Always verify $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i x_i$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong sign in the bias formula. The correct form is $b = y_i - w^T x_i$ for a margin point, **not** $b = w^T x_i - y_i$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to state that the KKT complementary slackness $\alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] = 0$ is a *separate* condition from primal feasibility.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing "kernel" with "similarity function." A kernel **must** satisfy Mercer's condition; not every similarity function qualifies (e.g., a non-PSD $K$ would break convexity).
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ in the soft-margin dual; omitting it loses 1 full mark in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — SVM Concepts and Kernels)**

- ⭐ **SVM Objective:** $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ — minimize norm (maximize margin) while penalizing violations.
- ⭐ **Geometric Margin:** $\gamma = \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$ — true perpendicular distance to hyperplane.
- ⭐ **Support Vectors:** Points with $\alpha_i > 0$; **only** these define the decision boundary (sparsity).
- ⭐ **KKT Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i[y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] = 0$ and $\mu_i \xi_i = 0$.
- ⭐ **Three Regimes of $\alpha_i$:** $\alpha_i = 0$ (non-support), $0 < \alpha_i < C$ (margin SV), $\alpha_i = C$ (bounded SV with slack).
- ⭐ **Dual Form:** $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ with $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- ⭐ **Weight Recovery:** $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ (linear case) or implicit in kernel case.
- ⭐ **Bias Recovery:** $b = y_i - w^T x_i$ using any margin point with $0 < \alpha_i < C$.
- ⭐ **Kernel Trick:** Replace $x^T z$ by $K(x, z) = \phi(x)^T \phi(z)$ to learn non-linear boundaries without explicit lift.
- ⭐ **Common Kernels:** Linear, Polynomial $(x^T z + c)^d$, RBF $\exp(-\gamma\|x - z\|^2)$, Sigmoid $\tanh(\kappa x^T z + \theta)$.
- ⭐ **Mercer's Condition:** Gram matrix must be **positive semi-definite** (all eigenvalues $\geq 0$).
- ⭐ **RBF Feature Space:** Infinite-dimensional — universal approximator on compact domains.
- ⭐ **C-parameter:** Larger $C$ ⇒ harder margin (less tolerance for violations, risk of overfitting).
- ⭐ **$\gamma$-parameter (RBF):** Larger $\gamma$ ⇒ narrower Gaussian ⇒ more complex boundary (overfit risk).
- ⭐ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Tune $C$ and $\gamma$ via cross-validation (grid search is KTU-recommended).
- ⭐ **Scalability:** Standard SVM is $O(N^2)$ to $O(N^3)$; use **SMO (Sequential Minimal Optimization)** or **linear SVM** (LIBLINEAR) for large $N$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Evaluation Metrics - Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Model Evaluation Metrics - Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Accuracy** is the most fundamental and widely used classification performance metric in supervised Machine Learning. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for OEITT721, accuracy quantifies the proportion of correct predictions (both True Positives and True Negatives) made by a classification model out of the total number of predictions evaluated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official Definition (KTU Standard):**  
> Accuracy is defined as the ratio of the number of correctly classified instances to the total number of instances in the dataset. It is a **scalar value bounded between 0 and 1**, where 1 represents perfect classification and 0 represents completely incorrect classification.

Mathematically, accuracy is expressed as:

$$A = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Predictions}}$$

Where the model output can be any one of four outcomes derived from the **Confusion Matrix**:
- **True Positive (TP)** – Model correctly predicts the positive class.
- **True Negative (TN)** – Model correctly predicts the negative class.
- **False Positive (FP)** – Model incorrectly predicts the positive class (Type I Error).
- **False Negative (FN)** – Model incorrectly predicts the negative class (Type II Error).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing **100 patients** for a particular disease. Out of these 100 patients:
- **60** are actually healthy
- **40** actually have the disease

The doctor examines each patient and provides a diagnosis. After the test results come back, we compare the doctor's predictions with the actual results:
- Correctly diagnosed healthy: **55**
- Correctly diagnosed sick: **35**
- Wrongly diagnosed healthy as sick: **5**
- Wrongly diagnosed sick as healthy: **5**

The **Accuracy** of the doctor is:

$$A = \frac{55 + 35}{100} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 90\%$$

So the doctor was correct **90% of the time**. Accuracy, in plain English, is simply the answer to the question: *"Out of all the predictions this model made, how many of them were right?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Accuracy is an **intuitive first-look metric**, but it is **not always reliable**, especially for **imbalanced datasets**. For example, a fraud detection model that predicts "no fraud" for 99% of transactions will have 99% accuracy even if it fails to detect every single fraud case. This limitation is the primary reason for the existence of alternative metrics like **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and ROC-AUC**.

---

## 1.3 When is Accuracy the Right Metric?

Accuracy is the **go-to metric** in the following KTU-relevant scenarios:

| Scenario | Use Accuracy? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Balanced classes (50:50) | ✅ Yes | No bias toward majority class |
| Multi-class problems (balanced) | ✅ Yes | Equal treatment of all classes |
| Imbalanced classes (e.g., 95:5) | ❌ No | Misleading; hides minority errors |
| Cost-sensitive problems (medical) | ❌ No | False negatives may be more dangerous |
| Image classification (CIFAR, MNIST) | ✅ Yes | Classes are roughly equal |

---

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Focus Area:** Model Evaluation Metrics form a critical section in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) and Module-level internal assessments. Students must understand not just the formula for accuracy, but also its **limitations**, **when to use it**, and **how it relates to the Confusion Matrix** and **Type I / Type II errors**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — From Confusion Matrix to Accuracy

To fully understand accuracy, we must first understand the **Confusion Matrix**, which is the foundational structure of every classification evaluation metric.

### Step 1: The Confusion Matrix for Binary Classification

The Confusion Matrix is a 2×2 table for binary classification:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Diagonal Elements (TP, TN) = Correct Predictions**  
> **The Off-Diagonal Elements (FP, FN) = Incorrect Predictions**

### Step 2: Logical Construction of the Accuracy Formula

Since accuracy is *"the proportion of correct predictions"*, we identify the correct predictions as the sum of **True Positives (TP)** and **True Negatives (TN)**.

$$\text{Correct Predictions} = TP + TN$$

The total number of predictions is the sum of **all four cells** in the confusion matrix:

$$\text{Total Predictions} = TP + TN + FP + FN$$

### Step 3: Final Formula Construction

By taking the ratio, we get the canonical accuracy formula:

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

This is the **primary form** that examiners expect to see in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 2.2 Extension to Multi-Class Classification

For **multi-class problems** (e.g., classifying digits 0–9), the confusion matrix becomes an **N×N matrix** where N is the number of classes. Accuracy generalizes naturally as:

$$A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{ii}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} c_{ij}}$$

Where:
- $c_{ii}$ represents the **diagonal elements** (correct predictions for class $i$).
- $c_{ij}$ represents the element in row $i$, column $j$ (prediction of class $i$ as class $j$).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric / Concept** | **Formula** | **Range** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$ | Balanced classification |
| **Error Rate** | $E = 1 - A = \frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Complement of accuracy |
| **Misclassification Rate** | $MR = \frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Synonym for error rate |
| **Multi-class Accuracy** | $A = \frac{\sum c_{ii}}{\sum \sum c_{ij}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | N-class problems |
| **Correct Predictions** | $N_{correct} = TP + TN$ | Integer | Direct count |
| **Total Predictions** | $N_{total} = TP + TN + FP + FN$ | Integer | Direct count |
| **Sample Accuracy** | $A = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(y_i = \hat{y}_i)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Indicator function form |
| **Balanced Accuracy** | $BA = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{TP}{TP+FN} + \frac{TN}{TN+FP}\right)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalanced datasets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** Indicator function $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ returns 1 if the condition is true, 0 otherwise. This is the **operational form** used inside ML libraries like scikit-learn.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Accuracy is used across virtually every engineering domain that involves classification:

1. **Medical Diagnosis** – Determining whether a tumor is malignant or benign.
2. **Spam Detection** – Email classification systems (Gmail, Outlook).
3. **Quality Control** – Manufacturing defect detection on assembly lines.
4. **Speech Recognition** – Correct transcription of spoken words.
5. **OCR Systems** – Recognizing handwritten characters in postal services.
6. **Autonomous Vehicles** – Detecting traffic signs and pedestrians.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** In viva voce and theory exams, examiners often ask *"Why can't we always use accuracy?"* The textbook-perfect answer is: **"Accuracy fails on imbalanced datasets because it does not distinguish between types of errors. A naive majority-class predictor can achieve deceptively high accuracy."**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Binary Classification (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem Statement:** A spam filter was tested on **200 emails**. The confusion matrix obtained is as follows:

| | **Predicted Spam** | **Predicted Not Spam** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Spam** | 70 (TP) | 30 (FN) |
| **Actual Not Spam** | 20 (FP) | 80 (TN) |

**Task:** Calculate the accuracy of the spam filter.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Identify the values from the confusion matrix.

$$TP = 70, \;\; FN = 30, \;\; FP = 20, \;\; TN = 80$$

**Step 2:** Verify that the sum equals the total number of emails.

$$TP + FN + FP + TN = 70 + 30 + 20 + 80 = 200 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the number of correct predictions (diagonal sum).

$$\text{Correct} = TP + TN = 70 + 80 = 150$$

**Step 4:** Apply the accuracy formula.

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{150}{200}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the fraction.

$$A = \frac{150}{200} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

**Step 6:** Convert to percentage for KTU answer sheets.

$$A = 0.75 \times 100\% = 75\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The spam filter has an accuracy of **75%**, meaning it correctly classified 150 out of 200 emails.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Error Rate Derivation

**Problem Statement:** From the same spam filter example, derive the **Error Rate**.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Recall that accuracy and error rate are complements.

$$A + E = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E = 1 - A$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known value of accuracy.

$$E = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

**Step 3:** Verify using the direct formula.

$$E = \frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{20 + 30}{200} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage.

$$E = 25\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The model made mistakes on 25% of the emails (50 out of 200). This is consistent with our 75% accuracy.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Multi-Class Classification (3 Classes)

**Problem Statement:** A classifier predicts three types of iris flowers (Setosa, Versicolor, Virginica). The 3×3 confusion matrix on a test set of **150 samples** is:

| | **Pred. Setosa** | **Pred. Versicolor** | **Pred. Virginica** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Setosa** | 48 | 2 | 0 |
| **Actual Versicolor** | 1 | 45 | 4 |
| **Actual Virginica** | 0 | 3 | 47 |

**Task:** Compute the classification accuracy.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Extract the diagonal elements (correct predictions per class).

$$c_{11} = 48, \;\; c_{22} = 45, \;\; c_{33} = 47$$

**Step 2:** Sum the diagonal.

$$\sum c_{ii} = 48 + 45 + 47 = 140$$

**Step 3:** Sum all elements to confirm the total.

$$\sum \sum c_{ij} = 48+2+0+1+45+4+0+3+47 = 150 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Apply the multi-class accuracy formula.

$$A = \frac{\sum c_{ii}}{\sum \sum c_{ij}} = \frac{140}{150}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify.

$$A = \frac{140}{150} = \frac{14}{15} \approx 0.9333$$

**Step 6:** Convert to percentage.

$$A \approx 93.33\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The model correctly classified **140 out of 150** iris samples, yielding an accuracy of **93.33%**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Imbalanced Dataset — The Accuracy Paradox

**Problem Statement:** A bank has 10,000 credit card transactions, of which only **50 are fraudulent** (0.5%). A model predicts "no fraud" for every single transaction.

**Task:** Show why accuracy is misleading in this case.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Construct the confusion matrix.

- TP (correctly flagged as fraud) = **0**
- FN (missed frauds) = **50**
- FP (false alarms) = **0**
- TN (correctly said not fraud) = **9,950**

**Step 2:** Apply the accuracy formula.

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{0 + 9950}{10000} = 0.995$$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage.

$$A = 99.5\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The Accuracy Paradox:** The model has a deceptively high accuracy of **99.5%**, but it has **completely failed** at its actual job — it caught **zero** fraudulent transactions. This is why **Recall, Precision, and F1-Score** must supplement accuracy on imbalanced data. This is a **favourite KTU exam question** — expect it!

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Accuracy vs Related Metrics

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Strength** | **Weakness** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Simple, intuitive | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Measures exactness | Ignores false negatives |
| **Recall** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Measures completeness | Ignores false positives |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R | Less interpretable |
| **Specificity** | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True negative rate | Ignores positives |
| **Balanced Accuracy** | $\frac{1}{2}(Recall + Specificity)$ | Handles imbalance | Less commonly used |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — How Accuracy is Computed from a Confusion Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Model Predictions Ready]) --> confMat[Build Confusion Matrix from Predictions vs Actuals]
    confMat --> tpCount[Count TP: Correctly Predicted Positives]
    confMat --> tnCount[Count TN: Correctly Predicted Negatives]
    confMat --> fpCount[Count FP: Incorrectly Predicted Positives]
    confMat --> fnCount[Count FN: Incorrectly Predicted Negatives]
    tpCount --> sumCorrect[Sum Correct = TP + TN]
    tnCount --> sumCorrect
    fpCount --> sumTotal[Sum Total = TP + TN + FP + FN]
    fnCount --> sumTotal
    sumCorrect --> divide[Compute Ratio: Correct / Total]
    sumTotal --> divide
    divide --> checkImbalance{Check Class Balance}
    checkImbalance -->|Balanced| reportAcc[Report Accuracy as Final Metric]
    checkImbalance -->|Imbalanced| warnUser[Warning: Use Precision/Recall/F1 Instead]
    warnUser --> reportAcc
    reportAcc --> stopA([End: Accuracy Computed])
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Position of Accuracy in the ML Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataSet[Raw Dataset] --> split[Train Test Split]
    split --> trainSet[Training Set 80 percent]
    split --> testSet[Testing Set 20 percent]
    trainSet --> trainModel[Train ML Model]
    trainModel --> modelTrained[Trained Model]
    testSet --> modelTrained
    modelTrained --> predictions[Generate Predictions]
    predictions --> confusionMat[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    confusionMat --> metricsBlock[Evaluation Metrics Suite]
    metricsBlock --> accMetric[Accuracy]
    metricsBlock --> precMetric[Precision]
    metricsBlock --> recMetric[Recall]
    metricsBlock --> f1Metric[F1 Score]
    accMetric --> reportEval[Final Evaluation Report]
    precMetric --> reportEval
    recMetric --> reportEval
    f1Metric --> reportEval
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use Accuracy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart{Is this a Classification Problem?}
    qStart -->|No| noUse[Accuracy Not Applicable - Use Regression Metrics]
    qStart -->|Yes| qBalance{Are Classes Balanced?}
    qBalance -->|Yes| useAcc[Use Accuracy Freely]
    qBalance -->|No| qCost{Is One Error Type More Costly?}
    qCost -->|Yes| usePRF[Use Precision Recall F1 Score]
    qCost -->|No| useBAcc[Use Balanced Accuracy]
    useAcc --> finalReport[Final Report]
    usePRF --> finalReport
    useBAcc --> finalReport
    noUse --> finalReport
```

## 4.4 Visual Diagram — Confusion Matrix Layout with Accuracy Highlight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cellTP[TP = 70] --- cellFN[FN = 30]
    cellFP[FP = 20] --- cellTN[TN = 80]
    cellTN:::correctStyle
    cellTP:::correctStyle
    cellFP:::wrongStyle
    cellFN:::wrongStyle
    classDef correctStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wrongStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** Green cells (TP, TN) contribute positively to accuracy. Pink cells (FP, FN) reduce accuracy. The total in green divided by the total in all four cells gives the accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q:** Define the term *Accuracy* in the context of machine learning model evaluation. Mention its key limitation.

**Model Answer:**

> **Accuracy** is a classification performance metric defined as the ratio of the number of correct predictions (True Positives + True Negatives) to the total number of predictions made by the model.
>
> **Formula:**
> $$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$
>
> **Key Limitation:** Accuracy becomes **misleading on imbalanced datasets** because it does not distinguish between types of errors. A model that always predicts the majority class can achieve high accuracy while completely failing to identify the minority class — known as the **Accuracy Paradox**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **Accuracy** and **Error Rate**. Are they related? Justify with a formula.

**Model Answer:**

> Yes, **Accuracy (A)** and **Error Rate (E)** are **complementary metrics**. They are related by the equation:
>
> $$A + E = 1$$
>
> - **Accuracy** = Proportion of correct predictions = $\frac{TP + TN}{Total}$
> - **Error Rate** = Proportion of incorrect predictions = $\frac{FP + FN}{Total}$
>
> If a model has **80% accuracy**, its error rate is automatically **20%**, since together they must account for 100% of the predictions. They are essentially two sides of the same coin.

---

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classification problem. Define the four outcomes (TP, TN, FP, FN) and explain the **Type I and Type II errors** in this context.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A medical diagnostic model was tested on **500 patients**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

| | **Predicted Disease** | **Predicted Healthy** |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual Disease** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actual Healthy** | 30 | 270 |

Calculate the **accuracy** of the model. Comment on whether accuracy alone is a sufficient metric here.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:

The **Confusion Matrix** is a 2×2 tabular representation that compares the actual class labels with the predicted class labels of a binary classifier.

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

**Definitions:**

1. **True Positive (TP):** Cases where the model correctly predicts the **positive class**.  
   *Example:* Correctly identifying a diseased patient as diseased.

2. **True Negative (TN):** Cases where the model correctly predicts the **negative class**.  
   *Example:* Correctly identifying a healthy patient as healthy.

3. **False Positive (FP) — Type I Error:** Cases where the model **incorrectly predicts positive** when the actual class is negative.  
   *Example:* A healthy patient wrongly diagnosed with disease (false alarm).

4. **False Negative (FN) — Type II Error:** Cases where the model **incorrectly predicts negative** when the actual class is positive.  
   *Example:* A diseased patient wrongly classified as healthy (missed detection).

**Importance:** The confusion matrix is the **foundation** for deriving all classification metrics — Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, and ROC-AUC.

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:

**Step 1:** Extract values from the confusion matrix.

$$TP = 180, \;\; FN = 20, \;\; FP = 30, \;\; TN = 270$$

**Step 2:** Verify the total.

$$180 + 20 + 30 + 270 = 500 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Calculate accuracy.

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage.

$$A = 90\%$$

**Comment on sufficiency:**  
While 90% accuracy looks high, the dataset has a **disease-to-healthy ratio of 200:300 (40:60)**, which is moderately imbalanced. The model **missed 20 actual disease cases (FN)** — a critical Type II error in medical contexts. Hence, **accuracy alone is insufficient**; one should also report **Recall (Sensitivity)** = $180/200 = 0.90$ and **Specificity** = $270/300 = 0.90$ to provide a complete picture.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**  
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **Accuracy Paradox** with a suitable example. Why is accuracy considered an inadequate metric for imbalanced classification problems?

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A multi-class classifier operates on 4 classes (A, B, C, D). The 4×4 confusion matrix is given below. Compute the **overall accuracy**.

| | **Pred. A** | **Pred. B** | **Pred. C** | **Pred. D** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual A** | 45 | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| **Actual B** | 3 | 38 | 4 | 5 |
| **Actual C** | 1 | 2 | 47 | 0 |
| **Actual D** | 0 | 3 | 1 | 46 |

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:

The **Accuracy Paradox** refers to the counterintuitive phenomenon where a model achieves **deceptively high accuracy** on an imbalanced dataset, yet performs **poorly on its intended task**.

**Example:**  
Consider a credit card fraud detection dataset with **10,000 transactions**, of which only **100 are fraudulent** (1% positive class). A naive model that predicts *"no fraud"* for every transaction will produce:
- TP = 0, FN = 100, FP = 0, TN = 9,900

$$A = \frac{0 + 9900}{10000} = 99\%$$

The model has **99% accuracy** despite detecting **zero** frauds. This is the **Accuracy Paradox**.

**Why accuracy is inadequate for imbalanced data:**

1. **Majority-Class Bias:** Accuracy is dominated by the majority class. In a 99:1 imbalance, even a trivial majority-class predictor scores 99%.
2. **Equal Weighting of Errors:** Accuracy treats FP and FN as equally costly, but in fraud detection or cancer diagnosis, **FN is far more dangerous** than FP.
3. **No Class-Discriminative Insight:** Accuracy provides a single global number, masking per-class performance. A high accuracy might still hide **0% recall on the minority class**.

**Better Alternatives:** Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Balanced Accuracy, MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient), ROC-AUC, PR-AUC.

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:

**Step 1:** Identify diagonal elements (correct predictions per class).

$$c_{AA} = 45, \;\; c_{BB} = 38, \;\; c_{CC} = 47, \;\; c_{DD} = 46$$

**Step 2:** Sum the diagonal.
