$$\sum c_{ii} = 45 + 38 + 47 + 46 = 176$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total of all elements (sum of all entries in the 4×4 matrix).

Row A total: $45 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 50$  
Row B total: $3 + 38 + 4 + 5 = 50$  
Row C total: $1 + 2 + 47 + 0 = 50$  
Row D total: $0 + 3 + 1 + 46 = 50$  

$$\text{Total} = 50 + 50 + 50 + 50 = 200$$

**Step 4:** Apply the multi-class accuracy formula.

$$A = \frac{\sum c_{ii}}{\sum \sum c_{ij}} = \frac{176}{200}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify.

$$A = 0.88 = 88\%$$

> **Final Answer:** The overall classification accuracy is **88%**, meaning 176 out of 200 samples were correctly classified.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to percentage** — Always express accuracy as a decimal AND percentage in your final answer.
> 2. **Mixing up TP/TN/FP/FN** — Remember: the *first word* (True/False) refers to whether the prediction was correct, the *second word* (Positive/Negative) refers to what was predicted.
> 3. **Not verifying the sum** of confusion matrix cells equals the total dataset size. If it doesn't, you've miscounted.
> 4. **Ignoring the Accuracy Paradox** — KTU examiners *love* to ask "Is accuracy always the best metric?" If you don't mention imbalanced data, you'll lose 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Diagonal Sum Error in Multi-class** — Students often sum the wrong diagonal direction. Diagonal = top-left to bottom-right. Use a ruler if needed!
> 6. **Skipping units** — Write "75%" or "0.75", not just "75" alone.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ — the most basic classification metric.
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$.
- **Perfect classifier:** $A = 1$; **Worst classifier:** $A = 0$.
- **Error Rate** = $1 - A$ — the complement of accuracy.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the prerequisite for computing accuracy.
- **TP, TN** = correct predictions; **FP, FN** = incorrect predictions.
- **Multi-class accuracy** generalizes via the diagonal sum over total sum.
- **Accuracy Paradox:** High accuracy ≠ Good model on imbalanced data.
- **Alternatives for imbalanced data:** Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC-AUC.
- **Balanced Accuracy** = average of per-class recall — better for skewed classes.
- **Always report multiple metrics** in your KTU answer sheets for full marks.
- **Indicator function form:** $A = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(y_i = \hat{y}_i)$ — useful for programming questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Precision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of supervised learning and classification evaluation under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education)** syllabus, **Precision** is formally defined as the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positive observations. It answers the critical question: *"Of all the instances that the model labeled as positive, how many were actually positive?"*

Mathematically, Precision (also known as **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)**) is expressed as:

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

Where:
- **TP (True Positives):** The model correctly predicted the positive class.
- **FP (False Positives):** The model incorrectly predicted the positive class (Type I Error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** Precision is a **class-specific metric** evaluated only over the positive class. It quantifies the **reliability** of a positive prediction made by a classifier. A high precision score indicates that the classifier returns substantially more relevant results than irrelevant ones.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **quality control inspector** in a biscuit factory. The inspector examines 100 biscuits and flags 10 as defective. Of these 10 flagged biscuits, only 8 are actually defective (the other 2 are fine, but mistakenly flagged).

- **Predicted Positives** = 10 (biscuits flagged)
- **True Positives (TP)** = 8 (correctly identified defective)
- **False Positives (FP)** = 2 (wrongly flagged fine biscuits)

**Precision** here means: *"When the inspector says a biscuit is defective, how often is he right?"* The answer is 8/10 = **0.8 or 80%**. High precision means the inspector rarely cries wolf.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in Machine Learning?**
> In applications like **spam detection, medical diagnosis (cancer detection), and fraud detection**, a False Positive can be highly costly. Precision directly measures and minimizes the cost of such false alarms.

### 1.3 Boundary Conditions

- **Best Case (Ideal Classifier):** Precision = 1.0 (100%). Every positive prediction is correct. $FP = 0$.
- **Worst Case (Worst Classifier):** Precision = 0.0 (0%). No positive prediction is correct. Every predicted positive is a False Positive.
- **Range:** $0 \leq \text{Precision} \leq 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision is undefined when $TP + FP = 0$**, i.e., when the model makes no positive predictions at all. In KTU board examinations, students must explicitly state this boundary condition to secure full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Precision does not exist in isolation; it is derived from the **Confusion Matrix**, which is the cornerstone of all classification evaluation metrics in KTU Module 3.

| **Actual \ Predicted** | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

Precision focuses exclusively on the **column-wise analysis of predicted positives**:

1. **Identify the Positive Class:** The class of interest (e.g., "spam", "disease", "fraud").
2. **Sum Predicted Positives:** Add $TP + FP$. This is the **total number of instances the model claimed were positive**.
3. **Extract True Positives:** From those predictions, count the ones that were genuinely positive ($TP$).
4. **Compute the Ratio:** Divide $TP$ by the total predicted positives.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **What It Measures** | **Range** | **Unit** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reliability of positive predictions | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| **Recall (Sensitivity / TPR)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| **FPR (Fall-out)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False alarm rate | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Correctly identified negatives | $[0, 1]$ | Dimensionless |
| **Precision in %** | $\text{Precision} \times 100$ | Percentage form | $[0, 100]$ | Percent (\%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** In board exams, you may be asked to compute Precision given a confusion matrix. Always convert the matrix to a **2x2 table** first, then apply the formula. Avoid using absolute value bars in your final answer table — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 2.3 Why Precision? — Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Consequence of a False Positive** | **Is Precision Critical?** |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Email Spam Filter** | A legitimate email lands in spam (user misses important mail) | High |
| **Cancer Diagnosis** | Healthy patient undergoes painful, risky biopsies | Critical |
| **Fraud Detection (Banking)** | Genuine transaction blocked; customer inconvenience | High |
| **Recommender Systems** | Irrelevant product recommended; loss of user trust | Moderate |
| **Autonomous Vehicle (Pedestrian Detection)** | False alarm triggers sudden braking; rear-end collision | Critical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Precision-Recall Trade-off (KTU Favourite Question):** Increasing precision typically decreases recall and vice versa. A model with $Precision = 1.0$ means it makes very few positive predictions, but the ones it makes are highly reliable. This is a **fundamental trade-off** in the bias-variance landscape of classification.

### 2.4 The Macro vs. Micro vs. Weighted Average

For **multi-class classification** (e.g., classifying digits 0-9 in MNIST), precision is computed per class and then aggregated:

- **Macro Precision:** Arithmetic mean of per-class precisions. Treats all classes equally.
  
$$ P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} P_i $$

- **Micro Precision:** Computed globally by aggregating all $TP$ and $FP$ across classes.
  
$$ P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\sum_i TP_i}{\sum_i (TP_i + FP_i)} $$

- **Weighted Precision:** Weighted by the number of true instances per class.
  
$$ P_{\text{weighted}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} w_i \cdot P_i, \quad w_i = \frac{\text{Support}_i}{\text{Total Support}} $$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam-Style):**
A binary classifier was tested on a dataset of 1000 samples. The resulting confusion matrix is given below. Compute the **Precision** of the classifier for the positive class "Disease".

| **Actual \ Predicted** | **Predicted Positive (Disease)** | **Predicted Negative (Healthy)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive (Disease)** | 90 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative (Healthy)** | 30 (FP) | 870 (TN) |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Positive Class:**
The problem explicitly states that "Disease" is the positive class of interest. All metrics will be computed relative to this class.

**Step 2 — Extract Values from the Confusion Matrix:**

$$ TP = 90, \quad FP = 30, \quad FN = 10, \quad TN = 870 $$

**Step 3 — Verify the Total Sample Count:**

$$ \text{Total} = TP + FP + FN + TN $$

$$ \text{Total} = 90 + 30 + 10 + 870 = 1000 \checkmark $$

**Step 4 — Apply the Precision Formula:**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Values:**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{90}{90 + 30} $$

**Step 6 — Simplify the Denominator:**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{90}{120} $$

**Step 7 — Final Reduction:**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$

**Step 8 — Convert to Percentage (Optional, Often Required by KTU):**

$$ \text{Precision (\%)} = 0.75 \times 100 = 75\% $$

**Interpretation:** When this classifier predicts a patient has the disease, it is correct **75% of the time**. This means **25% of positive predictions are false alarms**, which may be clinically unacceptable in a high-stakes diagnosis context.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Class Worked Example

**Problem:** A 3-class classifier (Classes: A, B, C) produces the following per-class counts. Compute **Macro Precision, Micro Precision, and Weighted Precision**.

| **Class** | **TP** | **FP** | **FN** | **Support (Actual Positives)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | 50 | 10 | 5 | 55 |
| **B** | 40 | 20 | 10 | 50 |
| **C** | 30 | 5 | 15 | 45 |

**Step 1 — Compute Per-Class Precision:**

$$ P_A = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 $$

$$ P_B = \frac{40}{40 + 20} = \frac{40}{60} = 0.6667 $$

$$ P_C = \frac{30}{30 + 5} = \frac{30}{35} = 0.8571 $$

**Step 2 — Macro Precision (Simple Average):**

$$ P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{3}(P_A + P_B + P_C) $$

$$ P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{3}(0.8333 + 0.6667 + 0.8571) $$

$$ P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{3}(2.3571) = 0.7857 $$

**Step 3 — Micro Precision (Global Aggregation):**

$$ P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\sum_i TP_i}{\sum_i (TP_i + FP_i)} $$

$$ P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{50 + 40 + 30}{(50+10) + (40+20) + (30+5)} $$

$$ P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{120}{60 + 60 + 35} = \frac{120}{155} = 0.7742 $$

**Step 4 — Weighted Precision (Weighted by Support):**

$$ \text{Total Support} = 55 + 50 + 45 = 150 $$

$$ w_A = \frac{55}{150} = 0.3667, \quad w_B = \frac{50}{150} = 0.3333, \quad w_C = \frac{45}{150} = 0.3000 $$

$$ P_{\text{weighted}} = (0.3667)(0.8333) + (0.3333)(0.6667) + (0.3000)(0.8571) $$

$$ P_{\text{weighted}} = 0.3055 + 0.2222 + 0.2571 = 0.7848 $$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Precision Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Dataset with Ground Truth Labels] --> B[Trained ML Classifier]
    B --> C{Prediction Made}
    C -->|Positive| D[Predicted Positive Pool]
    C -->|Negative| E[Predicted Negative Pool]
    D --> F{Match with Actual Label?}
    F -->|Yes| G[True Positives TP]
    F -->|No| H[False Positives FP]
    E --> I{Match with Actual Label?}
    I -->|Yes| J[True Negatives TN]
    I -->|No| K[False Negatives FN]
    G --> L[Precision Calculation Engine]
    H --> L
    L --> M[Compute TP / TP+FP]
    M --> N[Output Precision Score 0 to 1]
    N --> O{Precision High?}
    O -->|Yes Above Threshold| P[Reliable Positive Predictions]
    O -->|No Below Threshold| Q[High False Alarm Rate]
    P --> R[Deploy in High-Stakes Applications]
    Q --> S[Retrain with Cost-Sensitive Learning]
```

### 4.2 Confusion Matrix Visual Anchor

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ACT[ACTUAL CLASS]
        AP[Actual Positive]
        AN[Actual Negative]
    end
    subgraph PRED[PREDICTED CLASS]
        PP[Predicted Positive]
        PN[Predicted Negative]
    end
    AP -->|Model Said Positive| TP1[True Positive TP]
    AP -->|Model Said Negative| FN1[False Negative FN]
    AN -->|Model Said Positive| FP1[False Positive FP]
    AN -->|Model Said Negative| TN1[True Negative TN]
    TP1 -.->|Forms Numerator of Precision| PREC[Precision = TP / TP+FP]
    FP1 -.->|Forms Denominator of Precision| PREC
    PREC --> RES[Output 0 to 1]
```

### 4.3 Precision-Recall Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Threshold Tuning] --> Y{Adjust Decision Threshold}
    Y -->|Lower Threshold| Z1[More Positives Predicted]
    Y -->|Higher Threshold| Z2[Fewer Positives Predicted]
    Z1 --> R1[Recall Increases]
    Z1 --> P1[Precision Decreases]
    Z2 --> R2[Recall Decreases]
    Z2 --> P2[Precision Increases]
    P2 --> IDEAL[High Precision Few False Alarms]
    P1 --> COV[High Recall Catches All Positives]
    IDEAL --> SWEET[Optimal Operating Point via F1]
    COV --> SWEET
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: When to Prioritize Precision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Classification Problem] --> Q1{Is False Positive Cost High?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is False Negative Cost Low?}
    Q1 -->|No| REC[Optimize for Recall]
    Q2 -->|Yes| PRIOR[Prioritize Precision]
    Q2 -->|No| F1OP[Optimize F1-Score]
    PRIOR --> EX1[Example Cancer Screening Confirmatory Test]
    REC --> EX2[Example Initial Cancer Screening]
    F1OP --> EX3[Example Balanced NLP Classification]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Precision in the context of a confusion matrix.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Precision** is a classification evaluation metric defined as the ratio of **True Positives (TP)** to the total number of instances predicted as positive ($TP + FP$).

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

It measures the **reliability** of positive predictions made by a classifier. A high precision value indicates that the model has a low false positive rate, making it suitable for applications where false alarms are costly (such as medical diagnosis or spam filtering). **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Question 2: How does Precision differ from Recall?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Precision** | **Recall** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Question Answered** | Of all predicted positives, how many are correct? | Of all actual positives, how many were caught? |
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| **Focus** | Quality of positive predictions | Quantity / coverage of actual positives |
| **Also Known As** | Positive Predictive Value (PPV) | Sensitivity, True Positive Rate (TPR) |

The two metrics are often in **inverse relationship** due to the precision-recall trade-off. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of Precision and Recall with a suitable example. Derive the formulas from the confusion matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Precision and Recall:**

**Precision** and **Recall** are two fundamental evaluation metrics in binary classification, derived from the **confusion matrix**. The confusion matrix is a 2x2 table that summarizes the model's predictions against the actual ground truth:

| **Actual \ Predicted** | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

**Derivation of Precision:**

Precision focuses on the **predicted positive column**. We want to find, out of all the instances the model predicted as positive, how many were truly positive. Therefore:

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Positive Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Positive Predictions}} $$

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

**Derivation of Recall:**

Recall focuses on the **actual positive row**. We want to find, out of all the instances that were truly positive, how many the model successfully identified. Therefore:

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Positive Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Actual Positives}} $$

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$

**Example:**

Consider a **spam email classifier** tested on 200 emails. The confusion matrix is:

| **Actual \ Predicted** | **Spam (Positive)** | **Not Spam (Negative)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Spam (Actual)** | 80 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Not Spam (Actual)** | 10 (FP) | 90 (TN) |

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} = 0.8889 = 88.89\% $$

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 = 80\% $$

**Interpretation:** When the classifier flags an email as spam, it is correct **88.89% of the time**, and it catches **80%** of all actual spam emails. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** A classifier produces the following confusion matrix for a medical diagnosis system where "Malignant" is the positive class:

| **Actual \ Predicted** | **Malignant (Positive)** | **Benign (Negative)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Malignant (Actual)** | 150 | 50 |
| **Benign (Actual)** | 20 | 780 |

Compute: **(i)** Precision, **(ii)** Recall, **(iii)** F1-Score, **(iv)** Accuracy. Also state which metric is most important here and justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Values:**

$$ TP = 150, \quad FN = 50, \quad FP = 20, \quad TN = 780 $$

**Total Samples:**

$$ N = 150 + 50 + 20 + 780 = 1000 $$

**(i) Precision:**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{150}{150 + 20} = \frac{150}{170} = 0.8824 = 88.24\% $$

**(ii) Recall:**

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 50} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 = 75\% $$

**(iii) F1-Score:**

$$ F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} $$

$$ F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8824 \times 0.75}{0.8824 + 0.75} $$

$$ F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6618}{1.6324} = 2 \cdot 0.4054 = 0.8108 = 81.08\% $$

**(iv) Accuracy:**

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{150 + 780}{1000} = \frac{930}{1000} = 0.93 = 93\% $$

**Most Important Metric — Justification:**

In a **malignant cancer diagnosis** context, the cost of a **False Negative** (missing a cancer case, i.e., telling a sick patient they are healthy) is far more catastrophic than a False Positive. Therefore, **Recall** is the most critical metric here. A low recall (75%) means **50 actual cancer patients were missed**, which is clinically dangerous. While Precision (88.24%) is also desirable, **Recall must be maximized** to ensure no malignant case slips through. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the limitations of Accuracy as an evaluation metric. Explain with an example how Precision provides a more meaningful measure in imbalanced datasets. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Accuracy:**

1. **Misleading on Imbalanced Data:** Accuracy can be deceptively high when one class dominates. For instance, in a fraud detection dataset where only 0.1% of transactions are fraudulent, a naive classifier that predicts "not fraud" for every transaction achieves 99.9% accuracy, yet is **useless**.

2. **Ignores Class-Specific Errors:** Accuracy treats False Positives and False Negatives equally, which is inappropriate in cost-sensitive applications (e.g., medical diagnosis where FN is more dangerous than FP).

3. **No Insight into Class Distribution:** A 95% accuracy could mean the model excels at one class but fails miserably at the other.

**Why Precision is More Meaningful:**

In imbalanced datasets, Precision focuses the evaluation entirely on the **minority (positive) class** of interest.

**Example:**

A credit card fraud detection model is tested on 10,000 transactions (99 legitimate, 1 fraudulent → 100 total for simplicity).

| **Actual \ Predicted** | **Fraud (Positive)** | **Legit (Negative)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Fraud** | 1 (TP) | 0 (FN) |
| **Legit** | 5 (FP) | 94 (TN) |

**Accuracy:**

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{1 + 94}{100} = 0.95 = 95\% $$

This appears excellent, but the model flagged 5 legitimate transactions as fraud.

**Precision:**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{1}{1 + 5} = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667 = 16.67\% $$

Precision reveals the model's true weakness: only 16.67% of its fraud alerts are correct, exposing the 83.33% false alarm rate that Accuracy masked. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** What is the Precision-Recall Trade-off? Explain with a precision-recall curve how varying the decision threshold affects both metrics. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of the Trade-off:**

The **Precision-Recall Trade-off** is a fundamental phenomenon in binary classification stating that as we adjust the model's **decision threshold**, Precision and Recall move in **opposite directions**. You generally cannot maximize both simultaneously.

**Mechanism — Threshold Variation:**

The decision threshold (typically 0.5) determines the cutoff probability for classifying an instance as positive.

- **Lowering the threshold** (e.g., 0.5 → 0.3): The model becomes more liberal in predicting positives.
  - More positives are predicted → $FP$ increases → **Precision drops**
  - More actual positives are caught → $FN$ decreases → **Recall increases**

- **Raising the threshold** (e.g., 0.5 → 0.8): The model becomes more conservative.
  - Fewer positives are predicted → $FP$ decreases → **Precision increases**
  - More actual positives are missed → $FN$ increases → **Recall decreases**

**The Precision-Recall Curve:**

A PR-curve is plotted with **Recall on the x-axis** and **Precision on the y-axis** as the threshold varies from 0 to 1. The **Area Under the PR Curve (AUPRC)** summarizes the model's performance.

**Ideal Scenario:** A perfect classifier would have Precision = 1 across all recall values (a horizontal line at the top). In reality, the curve slopes downward.

**Practical Selection:**

Use the **F1-Score** to find the optimal threshold that balances both:

$$ F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} $$

The threshold that **maximizes F1** is the optimal operating point for balanced applications. For high-stakes precision-critical applications (e.g., legal document classification), one may deliberately operate at a higher threshold at the cost of recall. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy:** A common blunder. Always remember: Precision = $TP / (TP + FP)$ uses only the **positive column** of the confusion matrix.
> 2. **Forgetting to state boundary conditions:** When $TP + FP = 0$, Precision is **undefined**, not zero. Examiners award marks for this nuance.
> 3. **Ignoring the positive class declaration:** Always explicitly state which class is "positive" before computing metrics. The examiner will deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 4. **Conflating Macro and Micro averaging:** For multi-class problems, **Macro Precision** is the simple average; **Micro Precision** aggregates globally. Do not mix them.
> 5. **Skipping units/percentage conversion:** KTU often expects the final answer in **percentage form** (e.g., 75%) even if the formula yields a decimal.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Precision** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$; it measures the **quality** of positive predictions.
- ✅ **Range:** $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$; **Ideal** value = 1.0; **Undefined** when $TP + FP = 0$.
- ✅ Precision is also called **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)**.
- ✅ **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** As Precision increases, Recall decreases and vice versa.
- ✅ In **imbalanced datasets**, Precision is a far more reliable metric than Accuracy.
- ✅ **Macro Precision** = simple average across classes; **Micro Precision** = global aggregation; **Weighted Precision** = support-weighted average.
- ✅ **High-stakes applications** (cancer, fraud, spam) demand **high Precision** to minimize costly false positives.
- ✅ Always state the **positive class** explicitly before computing Precision in KTU exams.
- ✅ Use the **F1-Score** to balance Precision and Recall when both errors are equally costly.
- ✅ The **PR-Curve** (Precision-Recall Curve) is the KTU-recommended visualization for imbalanced binary classification problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Recall in Machine Learning

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recall (R)** is a fundamental **classification evaluation metric** that quantifies the proportion of **actual positive instances** that a classifier has **correctly identified** as positive. Formally, it is the ratio of **True Positives (TP)** to the **total number of actual positives** (i.e., True Positives + False Negatives).

Mathematically, the KTU 2024 Scheme defines Recall as:

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Synonyms used in KTU syllabus & standard ML literature:**

| Term | Notation | Context |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Recall** | R | Standard ML evaluation |
| **Sensitivity** | Sn | Medical / Biostatistics |
| **True Positive Rate (TPR)** | TPR | ROC Curve analysis |
| **Hit Rate** | HR | Information Retrieval |
| **Completeness** | — | Search Engine metrics |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🏥 **Think of a Disease Diagnosis Scenario:**
> Imagine a hospital is screening **1000 patients** for a serious disease. Out of these, **100 actually have the disease** (the **actual positives**). The screening test correctly flags **85 of them** as sick, but **misses 15** who actually have the disease.
>
> **Recall = 85 / 100 = 85%**
>
> This means: *"Out of all the sick people, our test successfully identified 85% of them. It missed 15%."*

**Key Intuition:**
- **High Recall** → The classifier is **leaving very few positive cases un-detected** (fewer false negatives).
- **Low Recall** → The classifier is **missing many positive cases** (high false negatives — dangerous in medical/diagnosis scenarios).

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Mnemonic to remember:**
> **"Recall = How much of the real positives did we RECALL (catch)?"** — It measures *completeness* of detection, not *correctness* of detection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of Recall

Recall is derived from the **Confusion Matrix**, a 2×2 contingency table that summarizes a binary classifier's predictions against the ground truth.

| Actual \ Predicted | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Actual Positive** | **True Positive (TP)** ✅ | **False Negative (FN)** ❌ |
| **Actual Negative** | **False Positive (FP)** ⚠️ | **True Negative (TN)** ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Recall uses **only 2 cells** of this matrix: **TP** and **FN** (the *Actual Positive* row).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The mathematical intuition behind Recall can be broken down as follows:

1. **Identify the ground truth positives** — Look at the dataset and find all samples that truly belong to the positive class: $P = TP + FN$.
2. **Identify what the classifier caught** — Among these positives, count those correctly predicted: $TP$.
3. **Compute the ratio** — $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$.
4. **Bound check** — Since $0 \leq TP \leq (TP + FN)$, Recall is bounded: $0 \leq R \leq 1$.

## 2.3 Why Recall Matters — Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Why High Recall is Critical |
|--------|-----------------------------|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Missing a cancer patient (FN) is life-threatening |
| **Spam Detection** | Lower priority — missing one spam is acceptable |
| **Fraud Detection** | Missing fraud = direct financial loss |
| **Security Threats** | Missing a terrorist threat has severe consequences |
| **Search Engines** | Users expect to see all relevant results |
| **Defect Detection (Manufacturing)** | Missing a defective product = customer hazard |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> 📋 **All formulas required for KTU 2024 ESE on evaluation metrics:**

| # | Metric | Formula | Range | KTU Focus |
|---|--------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Recall (R)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Precision (P)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Accuracy (A)** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **FPR (Fall-out)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐ |
| 7 | **Error Rate** | $\dfrac{FP+FN}{Total}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐ |
| 8 | **TPR (Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} = R$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | **Prevalence** | $\dfrac{TP+FN}{Total}$ | $[0, 1]$ | ⭐ |

## 2.5 The Precision-Recall Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Recall and Precision are **inversely related** in most practical classifiers. As Recall increases, Precision typically decreases, and vice versa.

**Mechanism:**
- **Lowering the classification threshold** → More samples classified as positive → **Recall ↑, Precision ↓**
- **Raising the classification threshold** → Fewer samples classified as positive → **Recall ↓, Precision ↑**

## 2.6 When to Choose Recall Over Precision

| Scenario | Preferred Metric | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Cancer detection | **Recall** | Missing a positive is fatal |
| Search engine result | **Recall** | User wants all relevant results |
| Spam filtering | **Precision** | Blocking legit mail is unacceptable |
| Video recommendation | **Precision** | User dislikes irrelevant suggestions |
| Rare disease screening | **Recall** | Critical to catch all cases |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recall from First Principles

We begin with the **Bayesian Decision Theory** perspective, where a classifier's objective is to minimize expected loss.

### Step 1: Define the Loss Function

For a binary classification problem with classes $\{+1, -1\}$, define the loss matrix:

$$
L = \begin{bmatrix} L_{++} & L_{+-} \\ L_{-+} & L_{--} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $L_{+-}$ represents the loss of predicting **negative** when the actual class is **positive** (this is a **False Negative**).

### Step 2: Define Recall in Terms of Conditional Probability

Recall is the probability that the classifier predicts **positive** given that the actual class is **positive**:

$$
R = P(\hat{Y} = + \mid Y = +)
$$

### Step 3: Expand using the Confusion Matrix Counts

$$
R = \frac{\text{Count}(\hat{Y} = + \cap Y = +)}{\text{Count}(Y = +)}
$$

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

### Step 4: Verify the Bounds

- **Lower bound:** When $TP = 0$, then $R = 0$ (no positives identified).
- **Upper bound:** When $FN = 0$, then $R = 1$ (all positives identified perfectly).

$$
\therefore \quad 0 \leq R \leq 1
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A spam classifier is tested on **500 emails**. The Confusion Matrix is given below:

| Actual \ Predicted | Spam (Positive) | Not-Spam (Negative) |
|--------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Spam (Positive)** | **40** | **20** |
| **Not-Spam (Negative)** | **10** | **430** |

**Identify the values:**
- $TP = 40$
- $FN = 20$ (Actual spam, predicted as not-spam — these are the *missed* spams)
- $FP = 10$ (Actual not-spam, predicted as spam)
- $TN = 430$

### Step-by-Step Calculation of Recall:

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
R = \frac{40}{40 + 20}
$$

$$
R = \frac{40}{60}
$$

$$
R = 0.6667 = 66.67\%
$$

### Interpretation:

> "The classifier correctly identified **66.67%** of all actual spam emails. It **missed 33.33%** of the spam emails (the False Negatives)."

### Verification — Computing Related Metrics:

**Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.6667}{0.80 + 0.6667}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{1.0667}{1.4667} = 0.7273
$$

**Accuracy:**

$$
A = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{40 + 430}{500} = \frac{470}{500} = 0.94 = 94\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> 🔍 **Notice the Accuracy Paradox here:** Although accuracy is 94% (looks great), Recall is only 66.67% — meaning 1 in 3 spam emails slip through! This is why **Recall is essential in imbalanced datasets.**

---

## 3.3 Multi-Class Generalization of Recall

For a **multi-class classification** problem with $C$ classes, Recall is computed **per class** (one-vs-rest approach):

$$
R_i = \frac{TP_i}{TP_i + FN_i}, \quad \text{for each class } i \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}
$$

Two common aggregation methods:

**Macro-Averaged Recall (unweighted mean):**

$$
R_{macro} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} R_i
$$

**Micro-Averaged Recall (weighted by class frequency):**

$$
R_{micro} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{C} TP_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{C} (TP_i + FN_i)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Recall. Why is it considered more important than Accuracy in imbalanced datasets?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Recall (R)** is a classification evaluation metric defined as the ratio of True Positives to the total number of actual positive instances:
>
> $$
> R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
> $$
>
> It measures the **completeness** of a classifier — i.e., what fraction of actual positives it successfully identifies.
>
> In **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% negative, 1% positive), a trivial classifier that always predicts "negative" achieves **99% accuracy** but **0% Recall**. This is the **Accuracy Paradox**. Recall exposes this failure by penalizing missed positives, making it the **preferred metric** in medical, fraud, and rare-event detection scenarios. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Precision and Recall with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| **Definition** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| **Question Answered** | *"Of those predicted positive, how many are correct?"* | *"Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?"* |
| **Focus** | Quality of positive predictions | Coverage of positive instances |
| **Real-world Use** | Spam filter (don't block legit mail) | Cancer detection (don't miss patients) |

> **Example:** In a **spam filter**, high Precision means legitimate emails are not wrongly marked as spam. In **cancer detection**, high Recall means no cancer patient is missed. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given the following Confusion Matrix for a binary classifier:**

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 50 | 10 |
| **Actual Negative** | 5 | 35 |

**Compute: (i) Recall, (ii) Precision, (iii) F1-Score, (iv) Accuracy, (v) Specificity. Comment on the classifier's performance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Extract values from the Confusion Matrix:**

- $TP = 50$
- $FN = 10$
- $FP = 5$
- $TN = 35$
- Total $= 50 + 10 + 5 + 35 = 100$

**(i) Recall:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**(ii) Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} = 0.9091 = 90.91\%
$$

**(iii) F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.9091 \times 0.8333}{0.9091 + 0.8333}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{1.5151}{1.7424} = 0.8695
$$

**(iv) Accuracy:**

$$
A = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{50 + 35}{100} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85 = 85\%
$$

**(v) Specificity (TNR):**

$$
S = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{35}{35 + 5} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

**Comment:** The classifier has a balanced performance. Precision (90.91%) is higher than Recall (83.33%), indicating the classifier is slightly conservative — it makes fewer false alarms but misses a few actual positives. F1-Score of 86.95% confirms strong overall performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the Precision-Recall trade-off with a threshold-based example. How does adjusting the threshold affect Recall?**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** The Precision-Recall trade-off arises because a classifier outputs a **probability score**, and we apply a **decision threshold** (default = 0.5) to convert it to a class label.

**Threshold-based Example:**

Consider a classifier predicting whether an email is spam. The table below shows 5 emails with their spam probability scores:

| Email | Spam Probability | Threshold = 0.7 | Threshold = 0.3 |
|-------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| E1 | 0.95 | Spam ✅ | Spam ✅ |
| E2 | 0.80 | Spam ✅ | Spam ✅ |
| E3 | 0.60 | Not Spam ❌ | Spam ✅ |
| E4 | 0.45 | Not Spam ❌ | Spam ✅ |
| E5 | 0.20 | Not Spam ❌ | Not Spam ❌ |

Suppose E1, E2, E3 are **actually spam**, and E4, E5 are **not spam**.

**At Threshold = 0.7:**
- $TP = 2$ (E1, E2)
- $FN = 1$ (E3)
- $FP = 0$
- **Recall = 2/3 = 66.67%**, **Precision = 2/2 = 100%**

**At Threshold = 0.3:**
- $TP = 3$ (E1, E2, E3)
- $FN = 0$
- $FP = 1$ (E4)
- **Recall = 3/3 = 100%**, **Precision = 3/4 = 75%**

**Conclusion:** Lowering the threshold from 0.7 to 0.3 increased Recall from 66.67% to 100% but reduced Precision from 100% to 75%. This demonstrates the inverse relationship between Precision and Recall as the threshold varies. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**For a 3-class classification problem, the per-class confusion matrix values are:**

| Class | TP | FN | FP | TN |
|-------|----|----|----|-----|
| Class A | 30 | 5 | 3 | — |
| Class B | 25 | 8 | 4 | — |
| Class C | 20 | 10 | 2 | — |

**Compute the per-class Recall, Macro-Recall, and Micro-Recall.**

**Model Solution:**

**Per-Class Recall:**

**Class A:**

$$
R_A = \frac{30}{30 + 5} = \frac{30}{35} = 0.8571
$$

**Class B:**

$$
R_B = \frac{25}{25 + 8} = \frac{25}{33} = 0.7576
$$

**Class C:**

$$
R_C = \frac{20}{20 + 10} = \frac{20}{30} = 0.6667
$$

**Macro-Recall (simple average):**

$$
R_{macro} = \frac{1}{3}(R_A + R_B + R_C) = \frac{1}{3}(0.8571 + 0.7576 + 0.6667)
$$

$$
R_{macro} = \frac{2.2814}{3} = 0.7605 = 76.05\%
$$

**Micro-Recall (global average using summed TP and FN):**

$$
R_{micro} = \frac{\sum TP}{\sum (TP + FN)} = \frac{30 + 25 + 20}{(30+5) + (25+8) + (20+10)}
$$

$$
R_{micro} = \frac{75}{35 + 33 + 30} = \frac{75}{98} = 0.7653 = 76.53\%
$$

> **Insight:** Micro-Recall (76.53%) is slightly higher than Macro-Recall (76.05%) because the larger class (A) is weighted more in the micro average. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Discuss the "Accuracy Paradox" with an example. How does Recall address this issue?**

**Model Solution:**

**The Accuracy Paradox:** When datasets are highly **imbalanced**, the Accuracy metric can be misleadingly high, hiding a model's poor performance on the minority (positive) class.

**Numerical Example:**

Consider a **credit card fraud detection** dataset with **10,000 transactions**, where only **100 (1%)** are fraudulent.

| | Predicted Fraud | Predicted Not-Fraud |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Fraud** | 5 (TP) | 95 (FN) |
| **Actual Not-Fraud** | 0 (FP) | 9,900 (TN) |

> Here, the classifier predicts **everything as "not fraud"** — a trivial model.

**Accuracy:**

$$
A = \frac{5 + 9900}{10000} = \frac{9905}{10000} = 99.05\%
$$

**Deceptively excellent!** But the model has **missed 95% of fraud cases!**

**Recall (which exposes the problem):**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{5}{5 + 95} = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05 = 5\%
$$

**Catastrophically low!** Recall correctly reveals that the model is useless for fraud detection.

**Conclusion:** In imbalanced scenarios, **Recall (and F1-Score) are far more informative than Accuracy**. They force the model to be evaluated on its ability to detect the rare but important positive class. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TP+FP with TP+FN** — Students often swap the denominator. Recall uses $TP + FN$ (actual positives row), NOT $TP + FP$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to percentage** — Always state final answer as both decimal and percentage.
> 3. **Mixing Macro and Micro Recall** — Macro is a simple unweighted mean; Micro is a weighted global average.
> 4. **Ignoring the Accuracy Paradox** — KTU specifically tests this concept; always mention imbalanced dataset scenarios.
> 5. **Not stating units/range** — Always explicitly state $0 \leq R \leq 1$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Recall**

- 📌 **Definition:** $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — measures *completeness* of positive detection.
- 📌 **Synonyms:** Sensitivity, TPR, Hit Rate — all mathematically identical.
- 📌 **Range:** $0 \leq R \leq 1$ (higher is better).
- 📌 **Confusion Matrix Anchor:** Uses only the **Actual Positive row** (TP and FN).
- 📌 **Trade-off:** Inverse relationship with Precision; controlled by classification threshold.
- 📌 **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of P and R — $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$.
- 📌 **Multi-class:** Use **Macro-Recall** (equal weight) or **Micro-Recall** (frequency-weighted).
- 📌 **Critical Use Case:** Medical diagnosis, fraud detection, security — where **missing positives is costly**.
- 📌 **Accuracy Paradox:** In imbalanced data, high accuracy ≠ good model — Recall is the real judge.
- 📌 **Boundary Cases:** $R = 1$ means no false negatives (perfect detection); $R = 0$ means all positives missed.
- 📌 **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** Maps to **CO3 (Model Evaluation)** and **Bloom's Level: Understand / Apply / Analyze**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F1 Score
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** F1 Score

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **classification model evaluation**, the **F1 Score** (also called **F-Measure** or **Dice Similarity Coefficient** in information retrieval) is the **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall**. It is a single scalar metric that balances the trade-off between *false positives* and *false negatives*, providing a more holistic view of classifier performance than accuracy alone — especially on **imbalanced datasets**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard)**
> The **F1 Score** is defined as the **weighted harmonic mean** of Precision (P) and Recall (R), where the harmonic mean assigns equal weight to both metrics. Mathematically:
> $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \equiv \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$
> It ranges in the closed interval **[0, 1]**, where **1.0** indicates perfect classification and **0.0** indicates total failure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **fisherman casting a net** in a lake to catch only **red fish** (positives) and avoid the **blue fish** (negatives).

- **Precision** = *"Of all the fish my net pulled up, how many were actually red?"* → It measures **purity** of your catch.
- **Recall** = *"Of all the red fish in the lake, how many did my net actually catch?"* → It measures **completeness** of your catch.
- **F1 Score** = *"How good is my net overall?"* → It gives a **single, fair score** that punishes you heavily if *either* purity *or* completeness is low.

> [!NOTE] **Why Harmonic Mean and Not Arithmetic Mean?**
> Suppose Precision = 1.00 and Recall = 0.01 (terrible model, but high precision). The **arithmetic mean** = 0.505 (decent looking!), but the **harmonic mean** = 0.0198 (correctly reveals the disaster). The harmonic mean is **punishing** to outliers and is therefore the mathematically honest way to combine rates/proportions.

## 1.3 Foundational Building Blocks (must be mastered first)

| Symbol | Meaning | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TP** (True Positives) | Correctly predicted positives | Count |
| **TN** (True Negatives) | Correctly predicted negatives | Count |
| **FP** (False Positives) | Negatives wrongly predicted as positives (Type I Error) | Count |
| **FN** (False Negatives) | Positives wrongly predicted as negatives (Type II Error) | Count |
| **Precision (P)** | TP / (TP + FP) | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] |
| **Recall (R)** | TP / (TP + FN) | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] |
| **Accuracy** | (TP + TN) / Total | Ratio ∈ [0, 1] |

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Misconception**
> F1 Score is **NOT** the arithmetic mean of Precision and Recall. Memorizing $F_1 = \frac{P+R}{2}$ is a frequent KTU valuation mistake that costs full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation Logic

The F1 Score emerges from a deliberate mathematical choice:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the two competing error costs.** Precision penalizes *false alarms* (FP), Recall penalizes *missed detections* (FN). Any single metric favors one type of error.
2. **Step 2 — Choose an aggregator that is "pessimistic".** A simple average (arithmetic mean) hides one weak metric. The **harmonic mean** $H(x,y) = \frac{2xy}{x+y}$ collapses to 0 if either $x$ or $y$ approaches 0.
3. **Step 3 — Apply equal weighting.** F1 weighs Precision and Recall **equally** ($\beta = 1$). This is the neutral, default choice when no business domain favors one over the other.
4. **Step 4 — Rewrite in terms of TP/FP/FN.** Substituting $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ yields the elementary form that survives a confusion matrix.
5. **Step 5 — Generalize to F-β family.** F-β allows tunable weighting: $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2)\frac{PR}{R + \beta^2 P}$. Here **β > 1** weights Recall more, **β < 1** weights Precision more.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | When to Use | Unit / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Cost of False Alarm is high (e.g., spam filter) | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Cost of Missed Detection is high (e.g., cancer diagnosis) | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **F1 Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced Precision-Recall requirement | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **F1 (Elementary Form)** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | Direct use from confusion matrix counts | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **F-β Generalized** | $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{R + \beta^2 \cdot P}$ | Asymmetric cost of FP vs FN | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **Micro-F1** | Aggregate TP, FP, FN globally, then compute | Multi-class, balanced corpus | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **Macro-F1** | Average of per-class F1 scores | Multi-class, imbalanced corpus | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **Weighted-F1** | Weighted by class support | Multi-class with known class priors | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **Accuracy (for contrast)** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Only when classes are balanced | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility**
> F1 Score is the **de-facto standard** for evaluating:
> - **Information Retrieval** (search engines, RAG systems)
> - **Named Entity Recognition (NER)**
> - **Medical Diagnosis AI** (where both false alarms and missed cases matter)
> - **Anomaly / Fraud Detection** in highly imbalanced fraud-vs-legit transactions
> - **Recommendation Systems** measuring user-relevance trade-offs

## 2.3 Critical Boundary Conditions

- **Boundary A — Perfect Model:** $P = 1$ and $R = 1 \Rightarrow F_1 = 1$.
- **Boundary B — Total Failure:** $P = 0$ or $R = 0 \Rightarrow F_1 = 0$.
- **Boundary C — Symmetry:** $F_1(P, R) = F_1(R, P)$. Swapping P and R does NOT change the score.
- **Boundary D — Concavity:** $F_1 \le \frac{P+R}{2}$ always (harmonic mean ≤ arithmetic mean).
- **Boundary E — Monotonicity:** F1 increases monotonically in **both** P and R.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of F1 from Precision and Recall

**Given:** 
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \qquad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$$

**Step 1 — Write the harmonic mean definition of two positive numbers a and b:**
$$H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the common denominator in the fraction's denominator:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}}$$

**Step 4 — Invert and simplify:**
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 5 — Now substitute the definitions of P and R in terms of TP, FP, FN:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}$$

**Step 7 — Factor TP in the denominator:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot \big[(TP+FN) + (TP+FP)\big]} = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot [2TP + FP + FN]}$$

**Step 8 — Cancel one TP:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

A spam classifier is tested on **200 emails**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

|  | Predicted Spam | Predicted Not-Spam |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Spam** | 40 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Not-Spam** | 5 (FP) | 145 (TN) |

**Step 1 — Compute Precision (P):**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40 + 5} = \frac{40}{45} = 0.8889$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recall (R):**
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8000$$

**Step 3 — Compute F1 using the harmonic-mean form:**
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8889 \cdot 0.8000}{0.8889 + 0.8000}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator calculation:**
$$0.8889 \cdot 0.8000 = 0.7111$$
$$2 \cdot 0.7111 = 1.4222$$

**Step 5 — Denominator calculation:**
$$0.8889 + 0.8000 = 1.6889$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**
$$F_1 = \frac{1.4222}{1.6889} \approx 0.8421$$

**Step 7 — Verify using the elementary TP/FP/FN form:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 40}{2 \cdot 40 + 5 + 10} = \frac{80}{95} \approx 0.8421 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Cross-check with arithmetic mean for contrast:**
$$\text{Arithmetic Mean} = \frac{0.8889 + 0.8000}{2} = 0.8444$$
> Notice: F1 (0.8421) < Arithmetic Mean (0.8444). This satisfies the **concavity property** of the harmonic mean.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Same Example, Different Metrics)

| Metric | Value | Interpretation | Penalizes Which Error? |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Accuracy | (40+145)/200 = 0.9250 | Deceptively high due to class imbalance | Neither — masks imbalance |
| Precision | 0.8889 | 88.89% of spam-flagged mails were truly spam | FP |
| Recall | 0.8000 | Caught 80% of all actual spam | FN |
| **F1 Score** | **0.8421** | Balanced single-number summary | Both equally |
| F2 Score (β=2) | 0.8148 | Lower, because Recall-dominant formula punishes misses more | FN (heavier) |
| F0.5 Score (β=0.5) | 0.8621 | Higher, because Precision-dominant formula rewards fewer false alarms | FP (heavier) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Position of F1 in the Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Classifier] --> B[Run on Test Set]
    B --> C[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    C --> D1[True Positives TP]
    C --> D2[True Negatives TN]
    C --> D3[False Positives FP]
    C --> D4[False Negatives FN]
    D1 --> E1[Precision Calculation]
    D3 --> E1
    D1 --> E2[Recall Calculation]
    D4 --> E2
    E1 --> F1Node[F1 Score Aggregator]
    E2 --> F1Node
    F1Node --> G1[Scalar Score in 0 to 1]
    G1 --> H1{Balanced Classes?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Use Accuracy or F1]
    H1 -- No --> I2[Prefer F1 over Accuracy]
    F1Node --> J1[Fbeta Family for Weighted Need]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: When to Choose F1 vs Other Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Start: Choose Evaluation Metric] --> K2{Is the dataset balanced?}
    K2 -- Yes --> K3[Accuracy is acceptable]
    K2 -- No --> K4{Cost of False Positives vs False Negatives?}
    K4 -- FP cost high --> K5[Optimize Precision, F0.5]
    K4 -- FN cost high --> K6[Optimize Recall, F2]
    K4 -- Equal cost --> K7[Use F1 Score]
    K7 --> K8{Multi-class problem?}
    K8 -- Yes --> K9[Use Macro-F1 or Weighted-F1]
    K8 -- No --> K10[Use standard binary F1]
```

## 4.3 Visual Schema: F-β Family Branching from F1

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Fbeta Family] --> L2[F0.5 — Precision Heavy]
    L1 --> L3[F1 — Balanced Harmonic Mean]
    L1 --> L4[F2 — Recall Heavy]
    L3 --> L5[2 x P x R divided by P plus R]
    L2 --> L6[1.25 x P x R divided by R plus 0.25 P]
    L4 --> L7[5 x P x R divided by R plus 4 P]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the F1 Score. Why is the harmonic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean to combine Precision and Recall?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** F1 Score is the harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R), defined as $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$. It provides a single balanced evaluation metric for classification models.
- **Harmonic vs Arithmetic (1 Mark):** The harmonic mean assigns equal weight to P and R and is **bounded above** by the arithmetic mean. It heavily penalizes models where one metric is low.
- **Illustrative Example (1 Mark):** For P=1.0, R=0.01, arithmetic mean = 0.505 (misleading), but F1 = 0.0198 (correctly reflects poor model).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Micro-F1 and Macro-F1 in multi-class classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Micro-F1 (1.5 Marks):** Computed by aggregating TP, FP, and FN **across all classes first**, then applying the F1 formula. It is dominated by frequent/large classes and is suitable for balanced corpora.
- **Macro-F1 (1.5 Marks):** Computed by calculating F1 **independently for each class** and then taking the **simple arithmetic average**. It treats every class equally and is preferred for imbalanced multi-class problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]

**(a)** Explain the terms Precision, Recall, and F1 Score with suitable formulas. Discuss why Accuracy is a misleading metric for **imbalanced datasets** and how F1 addresses this limitation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following confusion matrix for a binary classifier, compute the Precision, Recall, Accuracy, and F1 Score. Also compute the F2 Score and comment on its value relative to F1. **(7 Marks)**

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Actual Positive** | 50 | 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | 10 | 120 |

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**
- **Precision definition (1 Mark):** $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$. Measures the exactness of positive predictions.
- **Recall definition (1 Mark):** $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$. Measures the completeness of positive detection.
- **F1 definition (1 Mark):** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PR}{P+R}$. Harmonic mean of P and R.
- **Why Accuracy fails on imbalance (2 Marks):** In a 99:1 imbalanced dataset, predicting all as majority class yields 99% accuracy but zero predictive value on the minority class. Accuracy is biased toward the majority class because TN dominates the numerator.
- **F1 fixes this (2 Marks):** F1 ignores TN entirely, focusing only on TP/FP/FN. Thus it captures the model's ability to identify the minority class correctly, providing a fair evaluation.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

From the matrix: **TP = 50, FN = 20, FP = 10, TN = 120**, Total = 200.

**Step 1 — Precision (1 Mark):**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{50}{50+10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2 — Recall (1 Mark):**
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{50}{50+20} = \frac{50}{70} = 0.7143$$

**Step 3 — Accuracy (1 Mark):**
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{200} = \frac{50+120}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.8500$$

**Step 4 — F1 Score (1.5 Marks):**
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8333 \cdot 0.7143}{0.8333 + 0.7143} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5952}{1.5476} = \frac{1.1904}{1.5476} \approx 0.7692$$

**Step 5 — F2 Score (Recall-weighted) (1.5 Marks):**
$$F_2 = \frac{(1+2^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{R + 2^2 \cdot P} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.8333 \cdot 0.7143}{0.7143 + 4 \cdot 0.8333} = \frac{2.9762}{4.0475} \approx 0.7353$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 Mark):** $F_2 = 0.7353 < F_1 = 0.7692$. This is because F2 weighs Recall more heavily, and the model's Recall (0.7143) is lower than its Precision (0.8333). F2 penalizes the lower Recall more, dragging the score down. This is the correct behavior when missed positives are more costly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]

**(a)** Derive the F1 Score formula starting from the harmonic mean definition. Express the final form in terms of TP, FP, and FN. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a medical diagnosis system screening a rare disease, the confusion matrix is: TP=8, FP=2, FN=12, TN=978. Compute Precision, Recall, F1, and discuss whether F1 is sufficient here or if F-β with a specific β is recommended. Justify with a numerical example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Harmonic mean definition (1 Mark):**
$$H(x, y) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{y}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute P and R (1 Mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Common denominator (1 Mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P + R}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (1 Mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$ (1 Mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}$$

**Step 6 — Factor and cancel (1 Mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot [2TP + FP + FN]} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result (1 Mark):** 
$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Total = 8 + 2 + 12 + 978 = **1000**.

**Step 1 — Precision (1 Mark):**
$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{8}{8+2} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8000$$

**Step 2 — Recall (1 Mark):**
$$R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{8}{8+12} = \frac{8}{20} = 0.4000$$

**Step 3 — Accuracy (1 Mark):**
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{8+978}{1000} = 0.9860 \text{ (deceptively high!)}$$

**Step 4 — F1 Score (1 Mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 8}{2 \cdot 8 + 2 + 12} = \frac{16}{30} = 0.5333$$

**Step 5 — F2 Score (β=2, Recall-weighted) (1 Mark):**
$$F_2 = \frac{5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.4}{0.4 + 4 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{1.6}{3.6} = 0.4444$$

**Step 6 — Discussion (2 Marks):**
- Accuracy = 98.6% is misleading because the model missed 12 actual patients (FN) — a life-threatening error in a medical context.
- F1 = 0.5333 already reveals poor performance, but it still weights Precision and Recall **equally**.
- In medical screening, **missed diagnoses (FN) are far more costly** than false alarms (FP), since a false alarm leads to a second test, but a missed case leads to untreated disease.
- **Recommendation:** Use **F2 Score (β=2)** or even **F5 (β=5)**, which will push the model to optimize Recall. Reporting F1 alone is **insufficient** in safety-critical healthcare applications.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Formula Confusion:** Writing $F_1 = \frac{P+R}{2}$ (arithmetic mean) → **Lose 100% of marks** on the F1 derivation step. Always show $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PR}{P+R}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to label units / range:** F1 is dimensionless but bounded in [0,1]. Examiners specifically check for the **range statement** in the definition.
> 3. **Confusing F1 with F-β:** Students often write "F1 = 2PR/(P+R)" for an F2 problem. Verify whether the question asks for F1, F0.5, or F2.
> 4. **Multi-class slip-up:** In multi-class problems, do **not** compute F1 directly on the full confusion matrix; use Macro-F1 or Weighted-F1.
> 5. **Confusion matrix transposition:** TP and FP are frequently swapped. Always verify — TP is the intersection of "Actual Positive" and "Predicted Positive".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **F1 Score** is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall, NOT the arithmetic mean.
- **Master formula:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$
- **Range:** [0, 1] — 1 is perfect, 0 is total failure.
- **Properties:** Symmetric in P and R; concave (≤ arithmetic mean); monotonically increasing in both P and R.
- **F-β family:** $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2)PR}{R + \beta^2 P}$ — choose β based on FP vs FN cost asymmetry.
- **β = 1** → F1 (balanced). **β > 1** → weights Recall. **β < 1** → weights Precision.
- **Multi-class variants:** **Macro-F1** (unweighted average per class) and **Micro-F1** (aggregate TP/FP/FN first).
- **Use F1 when:** dataset is imbalanced, classes have unequal cost, or you need a single scalar summary.
- **Avoid Accuracy when:** class ratio is skewed (e.g., 99:1), because high TN count masks poor minority-class detection.
- **Always show the derivation:** KTU examiners award partial credit for showing the harmonic-mean → final form algebraic transition.
- **Industrial relevance:** Search engines, fraud detection, medical AI, NER, RAG systems, and recommendation engines rely on F1 as a primary KPI.
- **KTU-favorite follow-up topics:** Precision-Recall (PR) curves, ROC-AUC, F-β generalization, multi-label classification metrics, and Class Imbalance Handling (SMOTE, class weights).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC Curve)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT721 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC Curve)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve** is a fundamental evaluation metric in machine learning that graphically represents the **diagnostic ability** of a **binary classifier** system as its **discrimination threshold** is systematically varied. The curve is plotted in a two-dimensional space where the **x-axis** represents the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** and the **y-axis** represents the **True Positive Rate (TPR)**.

The ROC curve was originally developed during **World War II** for the analysis of radar signals, and was later borrowed into the field of signal detection theory. In modern machine learning, it is a cornerstone of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for evaluating classification algorithms in Module 3 (Evaluation of Learning Algorithms).

The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** provides a single scalar value that summarizes the overall performance of the classifier across all possible thresholds. A perfect classifier has an **AUC = 1.0**, while a purely random classifier has an **AUC = 0.5**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Medical Diagnostic Analogy**: Imagine a doctor using a blood test to detect a rare disease. The test gives a numerical score for each patient. The doctor must choose a **cutoff threshold** to decide who is "positive" (sick) and who is "negative" (healthy).
> 
> - If the threshold is set **very low** (catches almost everyone as "sick"): Almost all truly sick patients are caught (high TPR), but many healthy people are also falsely flagged (high FPR).
> - If the threshold is set **very high** (only the most extreme cases are flagged): Very few healthy people are wrongly accused (low FPR), but many sick patients slip through undetected (low TPR).
> 
> The **ROC curve** plots ALL such possible trade-offs in a single graph — it tells the doctor "for every possible rate of false alarms, what is the corresponding rate of true catches?" The **better the test**, the more the curve bows toward the **top-left corner**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Discrimination Threshold**: The cutoff probability above which an instance is classified as positive.
- **Sensitivity** (Recall, Hit Rate): The proportion of actual positives correctly identified.
- **Specificity**: The proportion of actual negatives correctly identified.
- **Fall-out** (False Positive Rate): The proportion of actual negatives incorrectly classified as positive.
- **AUC (Area Under Curve)**: Aggregate measure of classifier performance across all thresholds.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recognition Tip**: Whenever a question mentions *"plotting TPR vs FPR"* or *"trade-off between sensitivity and specificity"*, the answer is **ROC Curve**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Every point on the ROC curve is derived from a **confusion matrix** computed at a specific threshold. For a binary classification problem:

| Predicted Negative (0) | Predicted Positive (1) |
|:---:|:---:|
| True Negative (**TN**) | False Positive (**FP**) |
| False Negative (**FN**) | True Positive (**TP**) |

Where:
- **TP**: Model predicts Positive, actual class is Positive (correct).
- **TN**: Model predicts Negative, actual class is Negative (correct).
- **FP**: Model predicts Positive, actual class is Negative (Type I error).
- **FN**: Model predicts Negative, actual class is Positive (Type II error).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of ROC Construction

1. **Train a probabilistic classifier** (e.g., Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, Neural Network with softmax) that outputs a probability score $\hat{p} \in [0,1]$ for the positive class.
2. **Sort all test instances** in descending order of their predicted probability $\hat{p}$.
3. **Vary the threshold $T$** from $1.0$ down to $0.0$ in small increments.
4. **At each threshold $T$**, classify instances with $\hat{p} \geq T$ as Positive, and $\hat{p} < T$ as Negative.
5. **Build a confusion matrix** for each threshold and compute **TPR** and **FPR**.
6. **Plot TPR (y-axis) vs FPR (x-axis)**. Connect adjacent points to form the curve.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for the ROC curve topic. Memorize all formulas — they appear directly in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| # | Metric Name | Mathematical Formula | Range / Ideal Value | Physical Interpretation |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **True Positive Rate (TPR)** / Sensitivity / Recall | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **1** | Fraction of actual positives correctly flagged |
| 2 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** / Fall-out | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **0** | Fraction of actual negatives wrongly flagged |
| 3 | **True Negative Rate (TNR)** / Specificity | $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **1** | Fraction of actual negatives correctly identified |
| 4 | **False Negative Rate (FNR)** / Miss Rate | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **0** | Fraction of actual positives missed by model |
| 5 | **Precision** / Positive Predictive Value | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **1** | Of all predicted positives, how many are correct |
| 6 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN} + \text{TN}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **1** | Overall fraction of correct predictions |
| 7 | **F1-Score** (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **1** | Balanced measure of precision and recall |
| 8 | **AUC** (Area Under ROC Curve) | $\displaystyle \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(FPR) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | $[0, 1]$; Ideal = **1** | Probability that classifier ranks random positive higher than random negative |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions Reference**:
> - A **perfect classifier** passes through the point $(0, 1)$ — zero false positives, all true positives caught.
> - A **random classifier** lies along the diagonal line $\text{TPR} = \text{FPR}$, i.e., $y = x$.
> - A **worse-than-random** classifier lies **below** the diagonal (it can be inverted to do better than random).

### 2.4 Why ROC Curves Matter in Engineering

- **Imbalanced datasets**: ROC is invariant to class distribution changes; it focuses on ranking quality, not absolute counts. Hence, when KTU 2024 problem statements mention *"fraud detection"* or *"rare disease diagnosis"*, ROC is preferred over accuracy.
- **Threshold-free evaluation**: Unlike accuracy, ROC evaluates the model across **all operating points**, making it suitable for deployment environments where the cost of false positives and false negatives may differ.
- **Model comparison**: AUC provides a single comparable scalar value across multiple classifiers.

### 2.5 Relationship Between ROC, AUC, and Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney Statistic

> [!TIP]
> The **AUC** is mathematically equivalent to the probability that the classifier will assign a **higher score to a randomly chosen positive instance** than to a randomly chosen negative instance. Formally:
> $$\text{AUC} = P(\hat{p}(x^+) > \hat{p}(x^-))$$
> This is identical to the **Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney U statistic** — a non-parametric test of whether two distributions are different. This is a frequently-asked conceptual question in KTU Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Building a ROC Curve from Scratch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU 2024-style numerical problem**. A probabilistic binary classifier has been evaluated on **10 test instances**. Each instance has a **true label** (1 = Positive, 0 = Negative) and a **predicted probability score** $\hat{p}$ of belonging to the positive class. We must compute TPR and FPR at every possible threshold and plot the ROC curve.

#### 3.1.1 Given Data Table

| Instance Index | True Label ($y_i$) | Predicted Probability ($\hat{p}_i$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 0 | 0.80 |
| 4 | 1 | 0.70 |
| 5 | 0 | 0.55 |
| 6 | 1 | 0.45 |
| 7 | 0 | 0.40 |
| 8 | 0 | 0.25 |
| 9 | 1 | 0.20 |
| 10 | 0 | 0.10 |

**Totals from the data**:
- Total actual positives (P) = instances with $y_i = 1$: {1, 2, 4, 6, 9} $\Rightarrow$ **P = 5**
- Total actual negatives (N) = instances with $y_i = 0$: {3, 5, 7, 8, 10} $\Rightarrow$ **N = 5**

#### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Sort Instances by Predicted Probability (Descending)

| Rank | Instance | True Label | $\hat{p}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 3 | 0 | 0.80 |
| 4 | 4 | 1 | 0.70 |
| 5 | 5 | 0 | 0.55 |
| 6 | 6 | 1 | 0.45 |
| 7 | 7 | 0 | 0.40 |
| 8 | 8 | 0 | 0.25 |
| 9 | 9 | 1 | 0.20 |
| 10 | 10 | 0 | 0.10 |

#### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Cumulative TP and FP at Each Threshold

At each rank $k$, we treat all instances up to rank $k$ as "predicted positive". So we cumulatively count TP (instances where $y_i = 1$) and FP (instances where $y_i = 0$):

| Threshold $T$ | Cumulative TP | Cumulative FP | Cumulative FN | Cumulative TN |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $T = 1.00$ (predict all as negative) | 0 | 0 | 5 | 5 |
| $T = 0.95$ (only instance 1 is positive) | 1 | 0 | 4 | 5 |
| $T = 0.85$ (instances 1, 2 are positive) | 2 | 0 | 3 | 5 |
| $T = 0.80$ (instances 1, 2, 3 are positive) | 2 | 1 | 3 | 4 |
| $T = 0.70$ (instances 1–4 are positive) | 3 | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| $T = 0.55$ (instances 1–5 are positive) | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| $T = 0.45$ (instances 1–6 are positive) | 4 | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| $T = 0.40$ (instances 1–7 are positive) | 4 | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| $T = 0.25$ (instances 1–8 are positive) | 4 | 4 | 1 | 1 |
| $T = 0.20$ (instances 1–9 are positive) | 5 | 4 | 0 | 1 |
| $T = 0.10$ (all 10 are positive) | 5 | 5 | 0 | 0 |
| $T = 0.00$ (still all positive) | 5 | 5 | 0 | 0 |

#### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute TPR and FPR at Each Threshold

Using the formulas:
$$\text{TPR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{\text{TP}}{P} \quad\text{and}\quad \text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{\text{FP}}{N}$$

With $P = 5$ and $N = 5$:

| Threshold $T$ | TP | FP | TPR $= \frac{\text{TP}}{5}$ | FPR $= \frac{\text{FP}}{5}$ | (FPR, TPR) Point |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1.00 | 0 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | (0.00, 0.00) |
| 0.95 | 1 | 0 | 0.20 | 0.00 | (0.00, 0.20) |
| 0.85 | 2 | 0 | 0.40 | 0.00 | (0.00, 0.40) |
| 0.80 | 2 | 1 | 0.40 | 0.20 | (0.20, 0.40) |
| 0.70 | 3 | 1 | 0.60 | 0.20 | (0.20, 0.60) |
| 0.55 | 3 | 2 | 0.60 | 0.40 | (0.40, 0.60) |
| 0.45 | 4 | 2 | 0.80 | 0.40 | (0.40, 0.80) |
| 0.40 | 4 | 3 | 0.80 | 0.60 | (0.60, 0.80) |
| 0.25 | 4 | 4 | 0.80 | 0.80 | (0.80, 0.80) |
| 0.20 | 5 | 4 | 1.00 | 0.80 | (0.80, 1.00) |
| 0.10 | 5 | 5 | 1.00 | 1.00 | (1.00, 1.00) |

#### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Plot the ROC Curve

The ROC curve is constructed by connecting the (FPR, TPR) points in order of increasing threshold. The unit-square plot with key reference points is shown in Section 4.

#### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute the Area Under the Curve (AUC)

We use the **trapezoidal rule**. For consecutive points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$, the trapezoid area is:
$$A_{\text{trap}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (x_2 - x_1) \cdot (y_1 + y_2)$$

Applying trapezoidal rule to all consecutive points:

$$\text{AUC} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (x_{i+1} - x_i)(y_i + y_{i+1})$$

Let us evaluate each trapezoid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &: \text{from } (0.00, 0.00) \to (0.00, 0.20) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.00 - 0.00) \cdot (0.00 + 0.20) = 0.000 \\
A_2 &: \text{from } (0.00, 0.20) \to (0.00, 0.40) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.00 - 0.00) \cdot (0.20 + 0.40) = 0.000 \\
A_3 &: \text{from } (0.00, 0.40) \to (0.20, 0.40) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.20 - 0.00) \cdot (0.40 + 0.40) = 0.080 \\
A_4 &: \text{from } (0.20, 0.40) \to (0.20, 0.60) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.20 - 0.20) \cdot (0.40 + 0.60) = 0.000 \\
A_5 &: \text{from } (0.20, 0.60) \to (0.40, 0.60) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.40 - 0.20) \cdot (0.60 + 0.60) = 0.120 \\
A_6 &: \text{from } (0.40, 0.60) \to (0.40, 0.80) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.40 - 0.40) \cdot (0.60 + 0.80) = 0.000 \\
A_7 &: \text{from } (0.40, 0.80) \to (0.60, 0.80) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.60 - 0.40) \cdot (0.80 + 0.80) = 0.160 \\
A_8 &: \text{from } (0.60, 0.80) \to (0.80, 0.80) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.80 - 0.60) \cdot (0.80 + 0.80) = 0.160 \\
A_9 &: \text{from } (0.80, 0.80) \to (0.80, 1.00) \\
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.80 - 0.80) \cdot (0.80 + 1.00) = 0.000 \\
A_{10} &: \text{from } (0.80, 1.00) \to (1.00, 1.00) \\
     &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (1.00 - 0.80) \cdot (1.00 + 1.00) = 0.200
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all the trapezoidal areas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 + A_5 + A_6 + A_7 + A_8 + A_9 + A_{10} \\
&= 0.000 + 0.000 + 0.080 + 0.000 + 0.120 + 0.000 + 0.160 + 0.160 + 0.000 + 0.200 \\
&= 0.720
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result**:
$$\boxed{\text{AUC} = 0.72}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: Since $0.72 > 0.50$ (random baseline) and $0.72 < 1.00$ (perfect classifier), the model has **moderate-to-good** discrimination ability. If the AUC were $\leq 0.5$, the model would be performing **worse than random** and would need to be inverted or retrained.

#### 3.1.7 Verification Using the Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney U Statistic

As a cross-check, the AUC can be independently computed as:
$$\text{AUC} = \frac{1}{P \cdot N} \sum_{i=1}^{P} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{p}(x_i^+) > \hat{p}(x_j^-)\right] + \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{1}\!\left[\hat{p}(x_i^+) = \hat{p}(x_j^-)\right]$$

For our 5 positive scores $\{0.95, 0.85, 0.70, 0.45, 0.20\}$ and 5 negative scores $\{0.80, 0.55, 0.40, 0.25, 0.10\}$, counting the number of positive-negative pairs where the positive score exceeds the negative score yields **18 wins** (out of 25 pairs), and there are **0 ties**. So:

$$\text{AUC} = \frac{18}{25} = 0.72 \;\checkmark$$

This matches our trapezoidal computation, confirming the result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ROC Construction

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end functional flow** used to construct a ROC curve from raw classifier outputs. This is the standard pipeline that a KTU 2024 examiner expects a student to draw in an ESE answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Test Dataset with True Labels and Predicted Probabilities] --> B[Step 1: Sort Instances by Predicted Probability in Descending Order]
    B --> C[Step 2: Initialize Threshold T to 1.0]
    C --> D[Step 3: For Each Threshold T Sweep from 1.0 to 0.0]
```
